Laboratory includes field trips, applications of concepts to ongoing restoration projects, and assessment of established projects.

title: Topics in Biology
description: Selected topics in biology not currently included in course offerings. May be repeated for additional credit as the topic/study changes.

title: Animal Behavior
description: Animal behavior describes and investigates the proximate and ultimate causes of animal actions. An interdisciplinary approach will examine and integrate the functional behavior of animals through ecological, evolutionary, genetic, neurophysiological and psychological bases.

title: Biology Practicum
description: Realities of the biological field through direct participation. May involve experience as a lab assistant, assistance with faculty research, or off-campus employment. Maximum of three credits per semester (no more than four credits counting towards major requirements). Recommended for juniors and seniors in the Department of Biological Sciences.

title: Lab: Genomics and Disease
description: Lab: Genomics and Disease

title: Genomics and Disease
description: Emphasizes the molecular nature of genes and genomes including structure, organization, expression, regulation, and function. Includes discussion of experimental and bioinformatic approaches used to study, analyze, and manipulate genetic information at both the single gene and whole genome levels, with application to the genetic basis of disease. Laboratory emphasizes recombinant DNA methodologies and bioinformatic analyses.

title: Cancer and Cell Biology
description: Exploration of cell and subcellular structure and function, emphasizing mechanisms leading to neoplasia and progression to malignancy and metastasis.  Topics include protein sorting, vesicular trafficking, and protein degradation; signal transduction pathways and oncogenes; cell cycle regulation, apoptosis, and tumor suppressor genes; and complex heterogeneous tumor-stromal interactions.  Laboratory highlights techniques of cell culture, protein immunodetection, subcellular localization, fluorescence microscopy, and histotechnology.

title: Lab: Cancer and Cell Biology
description: Lab: BIOL 416 Cancer and Cellular Biology

title: Immunology
description: Introduction to the mammalian immune system with emphasis on host-pathogen interaction. Control and specificity of innate and adaptive immunity are examined by studying the structure and function of antibodies and T cell receptors, development of immune cells, cytokines and chemokines, cell adhesion molecules, immunization, allergy, autoimmunity, graft rejection, and tumor immunology.

title: Plant Taxonomy and Systematics
description: This course emphasizes plant taxonomy and systematics of vascular flowering plants. Focuses on nomenclature, classification, identification, and taxonomy of plants. Topics also include evolutionary relationships, natural history, ecological adaptations, physiology, morphology, and reproductive biology of vascular flowering plants. Includes the collection, identification, and preservation of specimens.

title: Junior/Senior Research
description: Junior and/or Senior students carry out the research project proposed in the Research Methods course under the direction of a faculty mentor. Faculty and students will meet during the semester for progress updates and to discuss other topics as needed. The semester culminates with a written manuscript, and the dissemination of the work in an on-campus symposium. Maximum of 3 credits per semester.

title: Lab: Ornithology
description: Lab: Ornithology

title: Ornithology
description: The course will examine the modern birds (Neornithes) through cladistic and taxonomic approaches, while emphasizing avian morphology physiology, ecology, biogeography, and behavior. Field and laboratory experiences will develop the skills and knowledge necessary for professional application.

title: Lab: Herpetology
description: Lab: BIOL 458 Herpetology

title: Herpetology
description: This course will examine the modern amphibians (Amphibia) and reptiles (Reptilia) through cladistic and taxonomic approaches, while emphasizing their respective morphology, physiology, ecology, biogeography, and behavior. Field and laboratory experiences will develop the skills and knowledge necessary for professional application.

title: Lab: Physiology
description: Lab: BIOL 460 Physiology

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study or research under the supervision of an instructor whose approval must precede the student's enrollment.

title: Neuroscience
description: A study of the structure and function of the human nervous system. Emphasis on motor control, sensory input, the special senses, and behavior; including disease and dysfunction. Additional topics include sleep, learning and memory, and neural development.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Opportunities in Business
description: To familiarize students with the various disciplines taught within the field of business, the interrelatedness of the business disciplines, career possibilities available, the faculty of the department, and the relatedness to the liberal arts.

title: Lab: Gross Anatomy
description: Lab: BIOL 465 Gross Anatomy.

title: Gross Anatomy
description: A study of the gross anatomy of the human, including extensive laboratory experience in the dissection of human cadavers. Students utilize modeling software to supplement dissection.

title: Personal Money Management
description: A course in basic personal financial concepts designed to help students handle their money, and empower them to start their life with a solid financial foundation. Topics include: Christian stewardship, savings, budgeting, debt, career planning, credit, insurance (auto, home, health), investing, personal money management and the Christian faith.

title: Hospitality and Tourism Management
description: An overview of the hospitality and tourism industry will be provided in this course from the perspective of a business owner/manager. Students will learn about the main segments of the industry (hotels, cruises, restaurants and beverages, theme parks, conventions, and special events). Students will also learn about the scope and impact of the hospitality and tourism industry in the US and internationally.

title: Physiology
description: Study of the integrated physiological processes essential for maintenance of homeostasis.  Material spans cellular, tissue, organ, and systemic functions, with strong emphasis on human physiology.  The laboratory focuses on hypothesis-driven studies in several model organisms, including humans, and utilizes computerized instrumentation and clinical diagnostic tests.

title: Principles of Management
description: An introduction to the basic processes of management including human behavior in the workplace, leadership, teams, quality, change, organizational structure and design, international management, and management ethics.

title: Personal Finance
description: Budgeting, income taxes, investing and saving, various aspects of banking, credit and home ownership, insurance (life, auto, home), and estate planning with an integration of Christian faith throughout.

title: Operations Management
description: Study of the effective and efficient production of goods and services in both nonprofit and for profit organizations with emphasis on planning and scheduling of operations, design of production systems, allocation of resources including labor, materials, equipment, and control.

title: Global Entrepreneurship
description: This course will explore global entrepreneurship, including socio-cultural, political, and economic opportunities and challenges. Discussion will include how institutions interact with poverty and how social ventures, non-profits, and corporations can positively impact global environments. A feature of the class will be interaction with various global entrepreneurs and a heavy emphasis on the developing world.

title: Business as a Ministry
description: This course examines what business looks like when it is conceived as a ministry - as a means of honoring God - rather than as merely a secular, economic entity. By considering multiple models and abundant examples, students will learn how faith-based businesses around the world advance the common good and to point people toward God.

title: Small Business Development
description: A survey of the entrepreneurial process and the various aspects of launching a new venture. Topics covered include organizational models and team formation; legal structures; idea definition; raising capital; sales and marketing; business and financial models; product and service development; management; operations; customer support and sustainability. Current thinking in the business as mission and transformational development movements will be surveyed. Students will form interdisciplinary teams around a specific business venture approved by the instructor. Each team will develop a business plan for their venture based on course guidelines which will include an integration of the business as mission and transformational development models. Teams will critique each other's plans.

title: Business Practicum
description: Practical business-related experience under professional supervision or supervised participation as a teaching assistant in departmental courses.

title: Business Law
description: Introduction to the American legal process, the structure of the court system, and basic actions with an emphasis on contract law. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Business.

title: Entrepreneurship
description: Surveys current topics in entrepreneurship including business-as-mission and the social entrepreneurship movement. It will also provide students with significant interactions with actual entrepreneurs and a field experience with an entrepreneurial firm or organization.

title: Supply Chain Management
description: The course includes an introduction to managing global supply chains and operations within the context of an integrated value chain. The class presents a framework in today's global business environment to provide a competitive advantage resulting in increased revenue and improved profitability. Topics include global strategies for procurement and supply chain management, total cost of ownership, insourcing vs. outsourcing, design engineering management, global organizational considerations, sourcing considerations for OEM's, ODM's, and EMS companies, total quality management, inventory management, distribution and logistics management, negotiations, key metrics of supply chain performance, and legal and ethical considerations.

title: Independent Study in Management and Business
description: Independent study in management and business

title: Hospitality and Tourism Operations
description: Students will learn how to apply the process needed to plan, operate, and maintain hospitality and tourism organizations, with a focus on hotels, restaurants, resorts, and conventions. Experiential and guided assignments are a major part of this course.

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Making a Better World?
description: From the Borg to Matrix to I-Robot it is clear that fears of decision making machines make for popular movies. Students in this course will examine the foundation of these fears by studying the history, current practices and projected directions of management Science (based on mathematical and informational sciences) and Information Technology. Emphasis is placed on examining these areas from a Christian perspective.

title: Major Honors I
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Chemistry for Life
description: This course prepares non-science majors to recognize the significant role that chemistry has in the world around them. It focuses on the practical and fundamental principles of chemistry in the context of societal, political, economic, and technological issues that impact our lives. Students participate individually and in groups through class assignments that engage them in these complex issues. Students learn to read critically, think analytically, and assess information to achieve informed conclusions regarding the issues addressed. In the laboratory, students learn how to conduct and analyze simple investigations that illustrate the chemical concepts encountered in class.

title: Introductory College Chemistry
description: This course is designed to prepare students for continuation into the CHEM 105 / CHEM 106 sequence by focusing on the application of mathematical calculations in chemistry as well as basic chemical topics. Topics include exponents, logarithms, solving equations, dimensional analysis, the mole, stoichiometry, energy and bonding.

title: Topics in Business Administration
description: Study of selected topics in business administration. Content dependent on faculty and student interest.

title: Lab: Chemistry for Life
description: Lab: Chemistry for Life

title: Chemical Science
description: This course prepares students to become allied health or sustainability professionals. It focuses on the importance of understanding the principles of chemistry and their application in the health or environmental sciences. Relevant topics include the periodic table, chemical bonding, stoichiometry, chemical equilibria and molecular structure, and properties of organic and biological molecules. In the laboratory, students learn how to conduct and analyze simple investigations that illustrate the chemical concepts encountered in class.

title: Lab: General Chemistry I
description: Lab: General Chemistry I

title: Lab: Chemical Science
description: Lab: Chemical Science

title: General Chemistry II
description: Kinetics and thermodynamics of chemical reactions, types of chemical reactions, equilibrium and applications in aqueous solution, and electrochemistry.

title: Lab: General Chemistry II
description: Lab: General Chemistry II

title: Organic Chemistry for the Life Sciences
description: Essential foundations of organic chemistry for students needing a one semester survey of organic chemistry. Includes natural and synthetic substances, their properties, reactions, and preparation.

title: Lab: Chemical Analysis I
description: Lab: Chemical Analysis I

title: Chemical Analysis I
description: The theoretical basis and techniques necessary to accurately and precisely measure the chemical composition of natural and artificial materials. Specific topics include experiment design, sampling, and validation of results with supporting statistics; gravimetric and titrimetric analysis with supporting acid-base and solubility chemical equilibrium theory; introduction to instrumental techniques including molecular spectroscopy (UV-Vis), and chromatographic methods (GC and HPLC). Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Chemistry & Biochemistry.

title: Lab: Environmental Chemistry
description: Lab for Environmental Chemistry.

title: Independent Research
description: Independent experimental research intended for sophomores and juniors under the direction of a faculty mentor. One to two credits of research will culminate in a written report, with the option of presenting a poster at a campus research symposium. Students taking three credits of research in one academic year must complete the written report and a poster presentation for the spring research symposium.

title: Lab: Organic Chemistry I
description: Lab for Organic Chemistry I.

title: Organic Chemistry I
description: The characterization and chemistry of simple carbon compounds as organized by functional group. This includes nomenclature, three-dimensional structure, physical properties, reaction mechanisms, kinetics, thermodynamics, reactions, and NMR and IR spectroscopy.

title: Environmental Chemistry
description: The chemistry of surface and ground water, the atmosphere, and the geosphere, how these interact with each other and the biota, with particular emphasis on the impact of human activity. The laboratory consists of analytical methods used to measure pollutants in these parts of the environment.

title: General Chemistry I
description: This course is an introduction to the study of the material world from the perspective of molecules, atoms, and electrons. Students will demonstrate an understanding of atomic and molecular structure, stoichiometry, nomenclature, chemical bonding, chemical reactions (acid-base, solubility, and oxidation- reduction), introductory thermodynamics, the periodic table, gas laws, intermolecular forces, and states of matter. In the laboratory, students will demonstrate good lab technique, and gather and interpret experimental data that reinforces the topics of the course. Prerequisites: High school chemistry and satisfactory performance on a chemistry readiness assessment given at the beginning of the term, or high school chemistry and successful completion of CHEM 101 with a grade of C or higher.

title: Organic Chemistry II
description: The characterization of carbon compounds with increasing complexity. Emphasis will be on the chemistry of aromatic compounds, carbonyl compounds, amines, and biological molecules that contain multiple functional groups. This includes nomenclature, physical properties, reaction mechanisms, kinetics, thermodynamics, reactions, multi-step syntheses, and the integration of spectroscopic methods for the structure determination of organic molecules.

title: Lab: Organic Chemistry II
description: Lab for Organic Chemistry II.

title: Chemical Analysis II
description: A continuation of the theoretical basis and techniques necessary to accurately and precisely measure the chemical composition of natural and artificial materials. Specific topics include an expansion of experiment design, sampling, and validation of results with supporting statistics; titrimetric analysis with supporting compleximetric and oxidation-reduction chemical equilibrium theory; introduction to instrumental techniques including atomic spectroscopy (AAS, AES, and APS), chromatographic methods (GC/MS and HPLC), and electrochemical methods.

title: Lab: Organic Chemistry for the Life Sciences
description: Lab: Organic Chemistry for the Life Sciences

title: Lab: Chemical Analysis II
description: Lab: Chemical Analysis II

title: Independent Research
description: Independent experimental research intended for sophomores and juniors under the direction of a faculty mentor. One to two credits of research will culminate in a written report, with the option of presenting a poster at a campus research symposium. Students taking three credits of research in one academic year must complete the written report and a poster presentation for the spring research symposium.

title: Advanced Synthesis and Spectroscopy
description: One four-hour laboratory and two seminars per week. Laboratory will consist of the synthesis and analysis of organic and inorganic chemicals. The seminar includes instruction in interpretation of spectroscopic data, particularly modern NMR techniques; discussion of the results of the synthesis and analysis; and guidance in the presentation of written reports.

title: Inorganic Chemistry
description: Periodic variation of physical and chemical properties of the elements; bonding, structure, and spectroscopy of inorganic compounds, including coordination compounds; types of inorganic reactions.

title: Topics in Chemistry
description: Selected topics in chemistry not currently included in course offerings. May be repeated for additional credit as the topic/study changes.

title: Lab: Inorganic Chemistry
description: Lab: Inorganic Chemistry.

title: Chemistry Practicum
description: Exposure to the chemical field through direct participation. May involve experience as a laboratory assistant for a course, as an instrumentation assistant, or off-campus employment. Maximum of 3 credits per semester with the total not exceeding 4 credits. These credits cannot count towards the chemistry elective.

title: Lab: Biochemistry
description: Lab

title: Biochemistry I
description: Types of biochemical compounds with emphasis on structure/function relationships, i.e., bioenergetics, catabolism and human nutrition. The laboratory includes the isolation and purification of proteins, lipids and carbohydrates; qualitative and quantitative analysis of biological compounds; and enzyme kinetics.

title: Junior/Senior Research
description: Junior and/or Senior students carry out the research project proposed in the Research Methods course under the direction of a faculty mentor. Faculty and students will meet during the semester for progress updates and to discuss other topics as needed. The semester culminates with a written manuscript, and the dissemination of the work in an on-campus symposium

title: Advanced Organic Chemistry
description: Application of molecular orbital theory, thermodynamics and kinetics to the determination of organic reaction mechanisms and identification of reactive intermediates. Prediction of reaction mechanisms as applied to both organic and biological systems.

title: Lab: Advanced Synthesis and Spectroscopy
description: Lab.

title: Lab: Biochemistry II
description: Lab: Biochemistry II.

title: Biochemistry II
description: Anabolic reactions of organisms including DNA replication, RNA transcription,  and protein synthesis with an emphasis on reading the current literature.

title: Physical Chemistry I
description: An introduction to modern physical chemistry including classical thermodynamics, statistical thermodynamics, equilibrium and kinetics. Laboratory experiments will demonstrate principles of chemical thermodynamics and kinetics.

title: Lab: Physical Chemistry I
description: Lab: Physical Chemistry I

title: Physical Chemistry II
description: An introduction to modern physical chemistry including quantum mechanics, computational methods, approximation methods, and spectroscopy.

title: Senior Chemistry Seminar
description: Advanced work in the chemical literature to include leading class discussions on selected papers. Attendance at departmental seminars is required. The course also includes a unit on professional ethics in chemistry and biochemistry, as well as instruction on career planning. The Educational Testing Services Major Field Test in Chemistry will be administered in this course and is a requirement for graduation. Required of all chemistry and biochemistry majors.

title: Junior Chemistry Seminar
description: An introduction to the chemical literature, including the nature of the chemical literature, the process of doing a literature search, and the reading and critiquing of current papers. Students will give oral presentations based on papers in the literature. The course also includes a unit on chemical safety. Required of all chemistry, chemical education and biochemistry majors. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Chemistry & Biochemistry.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Fundamentals of Chinese I
description: This beginner-level Chinese course uses an innovative curriculum to develop students’ basic Chinese proficiency in all four language skills of speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Helping students to achieve the 5 language learning goals (Communication, Cultures, Connections, Comparisons and Communities), this course adopts a communicative approach to encourage students to use the language in authentic contexts. Upon successful completion of this class, students will be able to carry out simple conversations with native Chinese speakers.

title: Fundamentals of Chinese II
description: Building upon CHIN 101 , this Chinese course continues to advance students’ Chinese proficiency in all four language skills of speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Adopting a communicative approach, this course will strengthen students’ knowledge in the 5 language learning goals (Communication, Cultures, Connections, Comparisons and Communities) and encourage students to use the language in authentic contexts. Upon successful completion of this class, students will be able to carry out more complex conversations with native Chinese speakers.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Intermediate Chinese II
description: A continuation of CHIN 201 with a focus on further acquisition of reading, writing, listening, and speaking skills in Mandarin Chinese at the intermediate level, as well as cultural content.

title: Advanced Chinese Writing
description: This course is designed for third-year students to enhance their reading and writing skills.  Students will build fluency up to the advanced-medium level through studying and practicing functional grammar and methods of composing paragraphs as well as descriptions and narration  reading Chinese selected readings of cultural works to increase vocabulary and enhance grammar skills, and reading and writing skills. To be adequately prepared for the course, students should successfully complete CHIN 202 (or equivalent). Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Language, Literature & Writing.

title: Intermediate Chinese
description: This first half of the intermediate Mandarin course is designed for students who have successfully completed two semesters of Mandarin Chinese at the college level or the equivalent. Students will not only review previously learned vocabulary and grammatical structures, but also strengthen their communicative competence in speaking, listening, reading, and writing for continued success in Mandarin Chinese. Upon successful completion of this class, students will attain approximately the intermediate-mid level on the ACTFL proficiency scale.

title: Cross-Cultural:  China
description: This three week cross-cultural study course allows students to experience China, the second largest economy in the world. Through a short-term study in Hangzhou, a renowned metropolis, students will participate in a summer language and culture program at the International Exchange Center of Zhejiang University of Technology. In the first week, students will stay at the International Exchange Center of Zhejiang University of Technology in Hangzhou. On the second and third week, students will travel to three other large cities: Shanghai, Xi'an, and Beijing.

title: Advanced Conversational Chinese
description: This course is designed for third-year students to enhance their speaking and listening skills. Students will build fluency up to the advance-medium level through studying and practicing functional grammar, expanded vocabulary, and intensive cultural conversation dialogues to further develop communicative abilities. To be adequately prepared for the course, students should successfully complete CHIN 202 (or equivalent). Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Language, Literature & Writing.

title: Special Topics in Chinese
description: The study of selected topics in Chinese at the advanced level. Student writing in Chinese at the advanced level.

title: Independent Study
description: Advanced language study or research in Chinese.

title: Foundations in Christian Leadership
description: This course introduces students to foundational theories in leadership studies, combining theological and biblical reflection with ethical and spiritual inquiry. Students will consider their own vocational calls to leadership and the role of the chuch in society.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study of an advanced topic under the supervision of an instructor whose approval must precede the student's enrollment. This course rubric is not intended for laboratory research.

title: Field Experience in Christian Ministries
description: Field experience for students preparing for Christian ministry that provides observation and the development of an understanding of ministry roles. Students are expected to connect theoretical ideas with actions and decision-making processes associated with effective ministry.

title: Ministry with Youth I
description: The theoretical background of youth ministry is investigated with special attention given to the theological foundations of ministry with youth, the psychological characteristics of youth, the social manifestations of the youth culture, and qualities that enhance ministry with youth.

title: Theology of Ministry
description: This course explores the nature, authority and responsibility of the minister. More specific topics include the minister's relation to the general church and community, methods of ministerial work, the conducting of church services, and general church administration. Students will also be required to participate in Spiritual Formation groups that meet weekly during the chapel period.

title: Evangelism and Discipleship
description: This course examines biblical and historical understandings and practices of evangelism and discipleship. The relationship between evangelism, discipleship, and social action will be emphasized and applications will be made to local church contexts.

title: Modern Chinese Culture: A Cup of Tea
description: In-depth survey of modern Chinese culture. Students will discuss key elements of contemporary Chinese culture and life, with reference to cultural history and its impact on China today. Students will think critically about their own culture in relation to the differences they learn about Chinese culture and society. Topics include 1) basic introduction of Chinese language and history, 2) education and calligraphy, 3) social life, social media, food, and tea, 4) basic business culture, and 5) advice for Westerners living and working in China.

title: Worship
description: An introduction to the broad topic of worship, including such aspects as the theology of worship, the creation of worship materials, and the development of worship skills. Attention is also given to special worship services, the liturgical calendar, and the influence of culture on worship. Fulfills Writing in the Major for Music and Worship major.

title: Christian Spirituality
description: This course provides an examination of the Christian experience of God, with attention to the symbols used throughout history to describe this experience. By reflecting on a variety of approaches to God, students develop a comprehensive view of spirituality in the Christian tradition.

title: Teaching for Christian Ministries
description: Study of the biblical, historical, philosophical, theological, and psychological foundations of Christian education with special emphasis on how they relate to the aim, content/method, teacher-student relationship, environment, and evaluation of Christian education. This theoretical framework is then applied to pedagogy and the development of curricular materials for Christian ministry.

title: Ministry with Youth II
description: The practice of youth ministry is demonstrated through activities that acquaint the students with sound methods of evangelizing, discipling, teaching, and counseling youth. Attention is also given to planning and managing a comprehensive program of ministry with youth.

title: Pastoral Counseling
description: Role of the pastor in private, group, crises, referral, marriage enrichment, family, youth, and peer counseling.

title: Introduction to Preaching
description: The discipline of preaching is examined within the larger context of the church's call to proclaim the gospel. Various methods of sermonizing and types of sermons are considered, with the Bible regarded as the primary source of sermon material. Practical experience in sermon preparation, delivery, and critique are key components of the course.

title: Interfaith Leadership
description: An introduction to the theory and practice of interfaith leadership. Personal qualities and skills necessary for effective interfaith leadership will be developed through experiential learning.

title: Independent Study
description: Selected topics pursued independently with frequent consultation with faculty supervisor.

title: Creative Communication of the Gospel
description: An exploration and evaluation of emerging adolescent cultural trends and various means of communication, in order to study and practice effectively communicating biblical truths.

title: Mobilizing Congregations for Justice
description: An exploration of Christian beliefs, public theology, and ecclesial practices of grassroots justice and neighborhood activism. The course will emphasize the praxis of radical discipleship, peacemaking, liberation ethics, and prophetic witness, and their overlaps with nonviolent resistance, movement theory, and organizing theory.

title: Competitive Programming
description: This re-entrant course focuses on preparing the student for competitive programming. Students learn to solve difficult problems quickly and accurately utilizing various algorithms and implementation techniques. Problem solving strategies will be addressed using past contest problems as examples. The course will conclude with a culminating competition such as the ACM ICPC regional competition at Shippensburg in the fall semester and Dickinson Programming Competition in the spring. Additional competitions will be held with other schools as arranged.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record (3.5 cumulative GPA) for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Practicum
description: Supervised fieldwork congregational or youth ministries.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record (3.5 cumulative GPA) for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Introduction to Computer Science and Cybersecurity
description: This course provides a unified introduction to the field of Computer and Information Science (CIS). Students will learn how computers evolved. This course explores some of the major breakthroughs in the use of computers for computation, communication, automation, development, machine learning and security. Students will explore the application and ethical impact of computing technologies on our society and in the context of the Christian faith. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Computing, Mathematics & Physics.

title: Organization and Leadership for Ministry
description: An introduction to the basic processes of organization and leadership in a variety of ministry settings, including congregations and parachurch ministries. Topics include financial planning, organizational management, and leadership recruitment, development, and training. Diverse cultural perspectives on leadership will also be explored and addressed.

title: Web Development: Client Side
description: This course introduces web site design and development using EXtensible HyperText Markup Language (HTML) and Cascading Style Sheets (CSS). Students learn standard HTML and CSS and apply that to create and develop a web site. This course is open to any student in any discipline that desires to understand how web sites are created and developed.

title: Computer Programming I
description: This course provides a foundational introduction to computer programming, emphasizing features common to various programming paradigms: working with variables, decision structures, loops and sequential data.

title: NIX System Administration and Security
description: This course is designed to give students an overview of the system administration profession, and teach specific system administration skills using Linux as a server technology. Students will learn various elements of server technology such as installing, upgrading, and maintaining servers, planning for and responding to outages, designing and configuring computer networks, understanding computer security, and automating tasks with scripting.

title: Programming for User Interaction
description: This course introduces programming techniques and tools for developing interactive applications, using a suitable programming language and development framework (e.g., JavaScript and React). Students will also be introduced to concurrency via asynchronous programming for the web.

title: Hardware and Software
description: Examines the basic components of the hardware and the software of a variety of computers ranging from desktops and mobile devices to the Internet of Things. In addition to understanding the theory of how things work, students will be expected to troubleshoot and find solutions to actual computer problems without knowing in advance whether the problems actually exist or whether the problems are caused by the hardware, software, or network issues.

title: Business Systems Applications
description: This course acquaints students with a variety of ways in which computer applications are created, evaluated and used in a business. Students will participate in the application development process using an object-oriented language such as Net.

title: Data Structures
description: This course is designed to enhance students' proficiency in utilizing fundamental data structures such as arrays, stacks, queues, lists, trees, and hash tables for problem-solving purposes. The main emphasis of the course is on writing codes for linear and non-linear data structures, highlighting their implementation in contemporary programming environments and their application in addressing practical, real-world challenges.

title: Introduction to Mobile Application Design
description: Through semester-long mobile application design and development projects, students will be introduced to the range of hardware, software and communication technologies needed to support a web application targeted at mobile computing devices. Students will evaluate, considering both user-oriented and technology-oriented criteria, commercially developed mobile computing applications and applications created by their peers. Students will employ a requirements-based iterative software development process, working with a typical set of software tools used to create prototype mobile applications.

title: Data Communications and Networking
description: This course introduces the basics of data communication and networking. Students will develop an understanding of the general principles of networking as implemented in networks connected to the Internet. Specific attention will be given to the principles of network architecture and layering, multiplexing, network addressing, routing and routing protocols. Activities include setting up a local area network, the Internet, security, network management and network performance analysis.

title: Introduction to Game Design
description: Through a series of game design and development projects, students will be introduced to a range of video game genres and factors influencing games' playability, entertainment and educational value. Students will play and then evaluate, from a Christian perspective, both commercial games and games created by their peers. Students will employ a requirements-based iterative software development process, working with a typical set of software tools used to create prototype video games.

title: Web Development: Server Side
description: This course extends CIS 191 , Web Development: Client Side to dynamic web site development using server-side technologies. Students will build a web server to provide the home for the interactive, data-driven web site they will create. Topics include: server-side scripting languages, such as PHP and Java Server Pages, interfacing web applications with databases, and advanced topics in hypertext markup languages and client-side scripting. Security issues will be addressed including access control and secured transmissions and modern software tools for the server-side web application development will be introduced. Development for alternative devices such as mobile and hand-held devices will be introduced.

title: Software Engineering
description: Students will learn principles and methodologies essential for designing software systems within professional settings. The course encompasses the entire software development lifecycle, with a particular focus on user-centered design. Special emphasis is placed on the systematic capture and analysis of user requirements, along with the iterative processes of prototyping and testing interactive systems. Throughout the duration of the course, students will critically evaluate historical and contemporary trends in software development practices, with a specific emphasis on agile software development methods. By delving into these aspects, students will gain a thorough understanding of how to create robust, user-friendly software solutions while staying attuned to the dynamic landscape of industry practices. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Computing, Mathematics & Physics.

title: Database Concepts
description: Relational databases. Semantic object modeling. SQL in both local and client-server environments, in both embedded and stand-alone applications.

title: Information Assurance and Risk Management
description: Information Assurance is the practice of protecting against and managing risk related to the use, storage and transmission of data and information systems. An overarching model of information assurance for any organization needing a comprehensive security plan will be outlined. Students will learn how to construct an effective security policy from an understanding of the building blocks of security. Topics include asset identification, human factors, compliance with regulations, personnel security, risk assessment, business continuity, and ethical considerations are covered, as well as computer and network security tools and methods. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Computing, Mathematics & Physics.

title: Cloud Computing
description: This course provides students with an in-depth exploration of cloud computing including an emphasis on security considerations. Topics include service models, deployment models, identity and access management, security models, risk assessment, containers, compliance, lab exercises using popular cloud service providers, and security best practices.

title: Analysis of Algorithms
description: This course introduces students to the principles of designing and analyzing algorithms with a keen emphasis on efficiency and correctness. It centers on the development of implementations, gaining insights into their performance characteristics, and evaluating their potential effectiveness in real-world applications. This course delves into elucidating the distinctions among various problem-solving techniques, facilitating a nuanced understanding of efficient algorithm design.

title: Strategic Use of Information Technology
description: This course introduces students to the strategic, tactical and operational aspects of information technology (IT) as it relates to business. It surveys a broad range of topics, challenging students to understand the strategic relevance of IT to every aspect of business including, but not limited to quality, innovation, globalization, competitive advantage, collaboration, and outsourcing. The course also examines representative best practices of common IT business tools, including MS-Excel, Access, Word, and PowerPoint, as well as collaboration tools.

title: Network Security
description: Network security focuses on the prevention and monitoring of unauthorized access, misuse, modification, or denial of a computer network and network-accessed resources. As a follow up to the Data Communications and Networking course, students learn more about the vulnerabilities, risks, and attacks perpetrated on an organization's network and network resources and how to protect those resources. Topics include Internet security techniques and protocols, authentication, email security, encryption, web security, network management, firewalls, network security policies, and tools and techniques used by malicious network attackers.

title: Elements of Computing Systems
description: Through a series of hardware and software tasks, students are introduced to the lower level design and operation of computers. Topics include digital logic, computer architecture, assembly language, high-level languages and compiler design.

title: Computer Programming II
description: This course introduces object-oriented design using a suitable programming language (e.g., Java, C# or C++). Students will learn to manage complexity in a software project through the creation and use of custom data types, including types related to each other in an inheritance hierarchy.

title: Operating Systems and Computer Architecture
description: This course introduces the primary functions of a modern operating system through a series of systems software programming assignments. Topics include: systems programming in a Unix/Linus environment, memory management, compilation and linking process scheduling, virtual memory, system-level I/O, networking and concurrency.

title: Computer Science Practicum
description: Practical experience under professional supervision.

title: Application Development I
description: Students will be placed on teams for the first semester of a 2-semester long project. In this course, students will begin to solve a real-world problem which will be completed in the follow-up course, CIS 472 . The project will require student teams to integrate material from other CIS major courses to plan, define, analyze, design, build, test, and deploy an information system solution to solve a real business problem.

title: Machine Learning
description: Students will demonstrate understanding of topics in machine learning, including Unsupervised Learning, Supervised Learning, and Reinforcement Learning, and will consider advancements in the field as well as ethical implications.

title: Ethical Hacking
description: An ethical hacker, or white hat hacker, is a skilled individual who understands and knows how to look for weaknesses and vulnerabilities in target systems, using the same knowledge and tools as a malicious hacker, but in a lawful and legitimate manner to assess the security posture of a target system(s). Students will be exposed to hands-on study of ethical hacking and cryptanalysis. The focus will be on penetration-testing tools and techniques that security testers and ethical hackers use to protect computer systems. An emphasis will be placed on ethics and Christian character in the life of an ethical hacker

title: Digital Forensics
description: Digital forensics is a branch of forensics science that encompasses the recovery and investigation of material found in digital devices. Students will learn how to recover evidence in civil and criminal investigations using proper techniques and tools while developing an understanding of the types of computer and electronic crimes committed today. They will explore vulnerabilities of computer and electronic systems and techniques to exploit those vulnerabilities. Students will articulate how the Christian life-style enhances the legal and ethical aspects of any computer investigation.

title: Application Development II
description: Students, placed in teams formed in CIS 471 , will complete the 2-semester real-world project started in CIS 471 . The project will require student teams to integrate material from other CIS major courses to plan, define, analyze, design, build, test and deploy an information system solution to solve a real business problem. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Computing, Mathematics & Physics.

title: Topics in Computer Information Science
description: Each offering may deal with a different information systems area of current and practical relevance. Topics will be selected based on current and emerging issues, trends and technologies related to information systems. Such topics may include Decision Support Systems, Management Information Systems, computer simulation, advanced displays, intelligent interfaces, system planning and execution tools, Genetic Algorithms, Fuzzy Logic, Knowledge Based Systems, software metrics, information system acquisition models, computer forensics, and specific functional areas such as health care and financial information systems. This course may be taken more than once for credit (with a different topic).

title: Interactive 3D Graphics
description: This course introduces mathematical concepts and programming techniques underlying interactive 3D computer graphics applications (including computer animation, video games and scientific visualization applications). Through a series of projects students will explore the OpenGL graphics API, by way of its current WebGL standard. Topics include modeling, viewing and projection transformation, as well as custom lighting and effects made possible through the OpenGL Shading Language.

title: Cybersecurity Capstone
description: This semester-long capstone course involves a project that requires students to apply the skills acquired throughout the cybersecurity program. In this senior-level seminar, students will implement and verify a technical capstone project specific to their major discipline. This project is researched and designed under the supervision of the course instructor. Upon completion, projects will be fully documented and presented both in writing and orally to appropriate audiences. Additionally, students are encouraged to pursue cybersecurity certifications to enhance their professional credentials. The course places a strong emphasis on ethics and Christian character in the life of a cybersecurity professional, and it fulfills the writing-intensive requirement for the Cybersecurity major.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study in computer and information science under the direction of an instructor.

title: Introduction to Communication Seminar
description: Overview of the communication discipline with an introduction to the breadth of vocational options open to communication graduates.

title: Fundamentals of Oral Communication
description: This course is a study of basic principles of human communication. Students will explore modes such as intra- and interpersonal, small group, multicultural, digital and public communication. They will develop skills in sharing information and reasoned argument and examine the ethical responsibilities of oral communicators.

title: Newswriting
description: Introduction to journalism, including study and practice in writing news, with some attention to issues and controversies in journalism as they affect editors and writers. Fulfills Writing in the major for the Department of Communications.

title: Principles of Strategic Public Relations
description: Introduction to the nature, history, and current practice of strategic public relations, emphasizing appropriate communication practices for various publics and contexts including corporate, non-profit and government agencies. Focuses on the public relations strategic process of research, action planning, communication, and evaluation. Ethics, social media, media relations, and community relations are also discussed. Students create a personal ethics plan and conduct analyses of organizationsâ social media and overall public relations practices.

title: Organization of Programming Languages
description: This course introduces students to a variety of programming language features and design choices and to corresponding implementation challenges and tradeoffs.  Topics include functional programming, parsing and translation between alternative program representations, interpreted languages, static analysis, and type checking.

title: Cybersecurity Architecture
description: This course introduces students to understand, analyze, and create cybersecurity requirements for systems and develop an architecture to meet those needs. Participants will engage in hands-on activities, case studies, and collaborative projects to enhance their proficiency in each aspect of software engineering.

title: Basic Video&Editing Techniques
description: Students will gain an understanding of the equipment, techniques, workflows, and language of film to develop skills necessary to complete video projects for a variety of exhibition formats. Focuses on foundational principles of camera operation, care, and maintenance; editing with attention to framing, lighting, and sound; and broadcasting in a live studio.

title: Mass Media and Society
description: Overview of the history, organization, economics, content, control, and effects of mass communications in the United States with some comparison to foreign systems.

title: Communication Design
description: This course focuses on the principles and practice of visual communication including the use of digital photography, digital video production, multimedia, publication design and layout, and web site design. Students will analyze and practice creative content development for visual and digital communication using established industry tools and techniques.

title: Advanced Cinematography & Lighting Techniques
description: An overview of advanced cinematography and lighting techniques, this course will prepare students to work with both film and digital technologies, with an emphasis on framing, camera movement and lighting technologies and aesthetics. Students will delve deeper into DIT and post-production workflow, camera movement techniques, color theory and safety protocols for camera and grip work through hands-on technical instruction, analysis, exercises and projects.

title: Introduction to Film
description: Training in the language of cinema and video analysis and discussion of the basic issues involved in understanding film as an expressive cultural form. Areas covered include the shot, the scene, camera angles, lighting, montage, mis en scene, staging, camera movement, narrative structure, sound, film and digital media stocks, dialogue, and characterization.

title: Media in Everyday Life
description: Examines the way media messages are interpreted and used in everyday life. Special consideration of children and adults as consumers of information and entertainment with a specific focus on violence and gender conditioning will be given.

title: Film History I 1895-1960
description: The course is a comprehensive survey of the medium, its genres and forms. While exploring the Hollywood studio system, European art cinema, documentary and propaganda, and avant-garde/experimental cinema, we will also focus on technological changes (the advent of sound, color, and the age of television). Readings include essays and book excerpts introducing the films covered in the course and their methodologies, including the close analysis of particular movies, studies of national industries, and auterist approaches.

title: Writing for Mass Media
description: Introduction to and practice in writing for mass media, with a focus on commercial copy for the electronic media of radio and television. Fulfills Writing in the major for the Department of Communications.

title: Film History II 1960-Present
description: The course maps the evolution of several important tendencies in film history, including the rise of the 'new waves' and Third World cinema, the role of women in the industry, the blockbuster, and the impact of globalization and digital technology on film. While exploring the developments in film production, distribution and exhibitions, individual films and artists are situated within their social, cultural, and political contexts.

title: Screenwriting II
description: In this intensive writing seminar, students progress from writing scenes for film and television to beginning consideration of writing an entire feature length/episode length script.

title: Screenwriting I
description: This course uses scripts from significant films and viewings of those same films to analyze the most important features of film narrative. This course gives an introductory foundation for all other courses in screenwriting and teleplay writing. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Communications.

title: Persuasion: Theory and Practice
description: Principles and techniques of the art of persuasion as applied to oral discourse.

title: Fundraising Principles and Strategies
description: Foundations of fundraising for communication-related professions, including an overview of fundraising; methods for developing an effective, ethical, and integrated fundraising campaign; best communication practices including solicitation via various communication mediums (print, social, direct); and building and sustaining mutually beneficial relationships, and evaluating effectiveness of fundraising campaigns.

title: Topics in Media Studies
description: An in-depth exploration of a particular aspect of media studies. Subject matter may include areas such as digital media, popular culture or Christianity and media. The specific topic varies each semester. Please view course subtitle or consult with Instructor for details.

title: Rhetoric of the Civil Rights Movement
description: Few mass movements in the history of the United States of America have challenged and changed the nation in more fundamental ways than the civil rights crusade of the 1950s-1960s. However, to label the myriad initiatives for racial justice as a single movement distorts historical reality. The struggle to attain opportunity, equality, and security during that time involved a wide array of people and organizations, often at odds over tactics as well as goals. Throughout this course, we will explore not only the historical past of the movement, but also analyze its relevance for the present historical moment.

title: Advanced Editing & Effects
description: This course provides training in professional NLE (non-linear editing) software programs. Instructional foci involve editing workflow, media management and organization, editorial composition of scenes and the larger work, and color correction and grading.

title: Advanced Film and Video Production
description: Building upon the technical and aesthetic skills explored in Cinematography and Lighting, this advanced production course will focus on completion of a major semester-long film project, from preproduction through postproduction. Students will develop deeper knowledge and practice of producing and directing original work, as well as sound design, sound editing, and rerecording mixing.

title: Small Group Communication
description: Theory, research, and practice related to the structure and functioning of groups, including leadership, decision making, problem solving, and those variables requisite to effective communication within a group.

title: Audio/Visual Newsgathering
description: This course will introduce students to basic techniques of reporting with and editing sound and video. The emphasis of this course will be on the use of digital audio and video recorders in the field to produce news stories for radio, television, and the web. This course will provide fundamental instruction in standard audio and video editing software utilized in the industry. COMM 112 strongly recommended prior to taking COMM 243 .

title: Broadcast Performance and Production
description: This course will examine the profession of broadcast announcing. Students will investigate the technique of delivery as well as develop an understanding of the skills needed to create a polished, on-air product. Enunciation, pronunciation, volume, tempo, articulation, voice quality and ad-lib announcing will be emphasized.

title: Global Indigenous Cinema
description: This course looks closely at the film productions of members of groups known today under categorizations such as 'aboriginal' and 'indigenous.' The course explores cinema and theory relating to ethnographic and collaborative works produced by ethnographic filmmakers alongside aboriginal communities. It also examines films produced independently by indigenous cultures from around the globe, in hopes of seeing commonalities and differences in them, as well as the ways their cultures have affected their production and storytelling techniques.

title: Sports Journalism
description: This course examines the theory behind and the practices of sports journalism. We will examine facets of print, online and sports broadcasting.  This will include play-by-play, interviewing, anchoring sportscasts, blogs and podcasting.  The history of sports journalism and sports broadcasting will also be examined.  A hands-on approach will be stressed, although a mastery of the theoretical components will also be measured.

title: Latin American Cinema
description: Explore the evolution of Latin American cinema and TV series over the past thirty years, examining particular examples from Mexico, Brazil, Argentina, Bolivia, Colombia, Cuba, and Chile. Analyze global and local production representational strategies illustrated in the movies. Consider recent histories of military dictatorship and the ongoing struggles for economic development, democratization, and human rights.

title: Television Production
description: Introduction to and practical experience in television production studio operations. The course will provide weekly hands-on opportunities to learn best practices in the role and operation of all equipment involved in a studio production. Provides a base knowledge of television production practices, job responsibilities and procedures in the creation of a television studio production.

title: Business and Organizational Communication
description: This course explores how communication constructs and maintains organizational functioning at a variety of levels, as well as the skills and strategies of business communication in the era of social media, big data, and the internet of things. Students learn about recruitment and socialization, conflict, communication technologies, coworker and team communication, leadership, and predominant theories of organizational communication. Students also learn techniques of writing in new media, and basic skills in data analytics.

title: Electronic Newsgathering and Field Production
description: Students will learn to create news packages, corporate and promotional videos, TV commercials, music videos, and gain proficiency in the job responsibilities and procedures required to complete these projects.

title: Photojournalism
description: This course explores how we tell stories using pictures in newspapers, magazines and online publications. It covers legal and ethical considerations, visual storytelling rooted in semiotic theory, and focuses on hands-on practice and critique. Students will be required to provide a camera for this course, which may be borrowed or purchased. Contact instructor for specific camera requirements.

title: Multimedia Storytelling
description: This advanced multimedia course explores evolving practices in producing compelling stories across various multimedia platforms. Students will learn how to conceive, prepare, create, and distribute their content in a professional, compelling way. Topics will include story development, pitch, and planning; research methods and reporting techniques; video/audio production, design, and editing; and enhanced creative expression for a digital age. Students will create various targeted, short-form projects, including YouTube and TikTok videos, social media reels, GIFs, and podcasts.

title: Writing for Strategic Public Relations
description: This course focuses on building effective and ethical organization-public relationships with a variety of audiences through organizing, writing, and tailoring public relations materials such as media kits, newsletters, infographics, brochures, social media campaigns, and web writing. Basic graphic design and the publication/printing process are also briefly introduced. The course culminates in preparation and presentation of a final writing portfolio. COMM 205 is strongly recommended prior to taking COMM 351 . Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Communications.

title: Communication Theory
description: Students will learn about a diverse array of communication theories and distinguish differences among basic types of communication theories, including social scientific/objective, interpretive/humanistic, and critical. Communication theories in the subfields of interpersonal communication, media, rhetoric, intercultural communication, and organizational communication are examined. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Communications.

title: Intercultural Communication
description: Analysis of communication variables in cross-cultural situations, focusing on the individual, social, and cultural bases of human communication.

title: Crisis Communication and Media Relations
description: Analysis of corporate, institutional and governmental conflict and crises. Consideration given to nature of social conflict, negotiation, problem-solving, crisis planning, spokesperson training, news conferences and news releases. Through simulated crisis management plans and crisis press conferences, students explore shifting paradigms of crisis management in the social media climate. COMM 205 is strongly recommended prior to taking COMM 353 .

title: Rhetorical Theory
description: This course surveys the history and major theories of rhetoric from its foundations in ancient Greece to more recent postmodern manifestations. Students will gain a conceptual framework for speaking and writing persuasively as well as evaluating the structure and function of rhetoric in society. Fulfills Writing in the major for the Department of Communications.

title: Event Planning
description: This course prepares students to research, plan, execute, and evaluate small and large-scale events for a variety of purposes. Students will create multiple event plans covering event logistics, promotions and evaluation, as well as associated print, social, and internal communication materials. The course culminates in preparation and presentation of a final event planning portfolio.

title: Media Law and Ethics
description: This course offers instruction on the ethical and legal responsibilities and freedoms of print, broadcast, and online media under the U.S. Constitution, federal and state statutes, administrative law, common law and Christian codes of ethics. Specific topics include libel, privacy, contempt, copyright, broadcast regulation, the court systems, commercial speech, prior restraint, access, the civil and criminal judicial processes and obscenity.

title: Documentary and Promotional Film
description: This course will introduce students to the methods and practices of documentary, promotional, corporate and educational production for film. Students will learn to write persuasive non-narrative scripts, use professional interview techniques, perform camerawork, record sound in the field, locate appropriate music and do specialized editing assignments.

title: Methods and Issues in Film Studies
description: This course will explore the classic texts of film theory from the earliest dreams of what cinema might become in the 1890s through contemporary explorations of film and digital media and their role in global culture. Special attention paid to aesthetics, narrative theory, formal questions, cinema's relationship to other arts, exhibition, understanding the audience and the viewing experience.

title: Topics in Journalistic Writing
description: Rotating course options will include advanced topics in journalistic writing, such as travel, sports, entertainment, music, and humor writing.

title: Communication in Relationships
description: Human relationships are dialectical, presenting areas of tension and thriving. This course centers on the theory and practice of communication in human relationships such as romantic partners, friends, and family. Students will understand the intersections of identity, self-disclosure and society on healthy relationships. They will understand the dynamics of interpersonal conflict such as perspectives, power and style in relationships. Students will develop appropriate communication skills for sustaining healthy relationships, especially forgiveness and reconciliation, that truly make us live out the mandate of scripture “to act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God” (Micah 6:8).

title: Multimedia Storytelling
description: This advanced multi-media course examines the production and design of online journalistic content. Topics will include online storytelling, web site planning, organization and production. Students will contribute to a comprehensive multi-media news web site, taking advantage of the convergence of print and broadcast media. Additionally, conceptual and ethical issues related to publishing on the Internet will be discussed. COMM 112 strongly recommended prior to taking COMM 325 .

title: Topics in Media Production
description: Rotating course options will include advanced, specialized courses in media production, with specific application to professional competencies in convergence journalism and media.

title: Advanced Topics in Film Production
description: Advanced instruction in a specialized area of film production. Courses will cover topics such as cinematography and lighting, directing and producing, as well as advanced postproduction and sound aesthetics for film. Focuses on professional best practices and the function of each role in relation to the other vital components in the film production process. Strongly recommend completing COMM 220 prior to taking this course.

title: Sound Design for Film and TV
description: Using industry standard workflows and professional-grade production equipment and post-production technologies, students in this course apply theories in sound design to major film, video, and digital media projects.

title: Animation
description: Students will explore alternative techniques in animation, from stop-motion to rotoscoping. Focuses on student exploration and key concepts in developing, planning, and practicing animation techniques using photographic tools and hand-made film work.

title: History and Theory of Digital Media
description: In this reading-intensive course, we will explore how digital media imitate, advance or withdraw from accomplishments of older devices, how artifacts of specific media become desirable outcomes in others, and how these past processes impact the design and innovation of new tools. We will define new media and discuss the varous applications of digital technology in contemporary culture (filmmaking, YouTube and social networks, e-books, blogs, architecture and museums).

title: Strategic Public Relations Campaigns
description: Upper-level public relations majors participate in hands-on experiential learning through developing a strategic public relations campaign, start to finish, for a non-profit client. Students conduct research, create action plans, develop communication materials, and write evaluation plans to reach client goals, culminating in a final client presentation and extensive portfolio of work. The course is also open to other majors who have completed significant coursework in the public relations sequence.

title: Student Media Lab
description: This course is designed to provide oversight coordination and instruction for the production of student media. Students will be introduced to the basic processes of production associated with different media, will be engaged with questions related to the ethical and professional practice of journalism and media in a college context, and will be exposed to a model of multi-platform media production that is increasingly common in the world of journalism at large. Students taking this course for credit will be receive credit hours commensurate to their responsibilities in the production process.

title: Communication Senior Seminar
description: This course instructs students in vocation and professional development, examines the relationship between faith and students' academic discipline, and engages advanced research in communication ethics.

title: Junior/Senior Film Practicum
description: Students will gain intensive training in the complete experience of filmmaking. Students will gain experience in a specialized aspect of film production, for example, funding, pre-production, production, post-production, promotion and presentation. Students will serve in at least one capacity on each film project (e.g. director, producer, etc).

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study of research in communication under the supervision of an instructor whose approval must precede enrollment.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for junior- and senior-level students who have strong academic record for five previous semesters of college study.

title: Student Media Lab
description: This course is designed to provide oversight coordination and instruction for the production of student media. Students will be introduced to the basic processes of production associated with different media, will be engaged with questions related to the ethical and professional practice of journalism and media in a college context, and will be exposed to a model of multi-platform media production that is increasingly common in the world of journalism at large. Students taking this course for credit will be receive credit hours commensurate to their responsibilities in the production process.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for junior- and senior-level students who have strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Juvenile Delinquency
description: Examine the nature, causes, and consequences of juvenile delinquency. Analyze theories of delinquency and risk factors that contribute to youth involvement in crime. Explore the influence of child and adolescent development and the role of family, peers, education, and community in shaping juvenile behavior. Evaluate interventions and justice system responses aimed at prevention, behavior change, and restoration.

title: Lighting Techniques
description: This advanced film course focuses on the aesthetic and technical contributions lighting makes to a film as well as knowledge related to technology and equipment required in effective lighting design. Students will gain practical experience by applying lighting techniques and theory to specific film lighting projects.

title: Introduction to Criminal Justice
description: Understand the structure and processes of the American criminal justice system, including law enforcement, courts, and corrections. Appreciate the historical, sociocultural, legal, and constitutional foundations of the criminal justice system. Explore strengths, conflicts, and major areas for reform. Engage connections with Christian faith with particular emphasis given to restorative justice.

title: Law Enforcement
description: Examine the historical and contemporary practices shaping law enforcement and the evolving role of police personnel in relation to the community, courts, corrections, and constitutional guidelines. Explore the qualifications, recruitment, and selection methods for police officers, along with the essential components of job analysis and training. Analyze the impact of these practices on public trust, community engagement, and effective policing. Reflect on the challenges of law enforcement and critically assess and engage with the complexities of modern policing.

title: Criminal Law and the Court
description: Examine the evolution, challenges, and complexities in defining and codifying criminal law within adult and juvenile justice systems. Analyze key laws and landmark cases and their implications for practitioners in law enforcement, court services, and corrections. Explore the structure and rationale of centralized and decentralized legal systems, along with essential legal terminology for justice professionals.

title: Restorative Justice
description: This course focuses on the fundamental principles and practices of restorative justice within the United States and around the world. It examines historical vallues and assumptions that include Biblical and spiritual origins, and current principles and practices at community, national and international levels including the impace of restorative justice on victims, offenders, communities and criminal justice systems. This course examines policy implications and evaluates the potential of restorative justice to address social problems marked by human conflict. It will examine empirical evidence, identify critical issues in theory or practice, and review the integrity and future direction of restorative justice.

title: Communication Practicum
description: Work experience under professional supervision in such areas as broadcasting, organizational communication, Christian communication, public relations and information, advertising, entertainment, and visual media.

title: Corrections
description: Examine the evolution of correctional perspectives and practices and the ongoing debates surrounding community-based and institutional correctional programs. Analyze the relationships between accountability, behavior change, and public safety, and the tensions between protecting society and safeguarding individual rights. Evaluate the effectiveness of incarceration, probation, parole, diversion, and restorative justice programs, considering both proactive and reactive approaches to crime.

title: Criminology
description: Examine the historical evolution of criminological thought and the major theories that explain criminal behavior. Explore crime typologies and the processes of social science inquiry that shape criminological research. Analyze the roles of the justice system, social institutions, community, family, and peers in influencing pathways to crime and delinquency. Evaluate how criminological theories can inform policy, practice, and reform efforts aimed at addressing and preventing crime.

title: Topics in Criminal Justice
description: Selected topics in criminal justice not currently included in course offerings. Possible topics may include women in the criminal justice system, comparative law enforcement, ethics and criminal justice, and international justice issues. May be repeated for additional credit as the topic/study changes.

title: Forensics
description: Explore the diverse field of forensic science, focusing on the theories and applications of crime scene analysis, criminal investigation, and trace evidence processing.
