Examine the role of forensic science within the criminal justice system and its impact on solving and understanding crimes. Analyze techniques for evidence identification and collection, emphasizing the importance of accuracy and reliability in forensic practices.

title: Independent Study
description: Junior and senior students with at least a 3.0 GPA in their major may select a problem for independent investigation in a special area of Criminal Justice.

title: Major Honors I
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record, have second semester junior status, and/or have completed at least 69 credits of undergraduate study. An overall minimum GPA of 3.5, and a minimum GPA of 3.75 for courses in the major is required, and the student must have departmental approval for enrollment.

title: Ballet I
description: Fundamentals of classical ballet technique. Emphasis placed on proper placement, technique and classical ballet vocabulary.

title: Jazz and Dance Theatre I
description: An introductory-level studio course in jazz dance. This course will focus on basic jazz steps, isolations, strength, jumps, and turns.

title: Modern Dance I
description: Beginning level of modern technique using basic component of space, time and energy. Class work will focus on basic modern techniques combined with dance improvisation techniques.

title: Dance Performance Ensemble
description: Participation in dance performance ensembles as supervised and approved by the Dance faculty. Each of the main dance techniques will be cross-trained on a rotating basis. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Theater & Dance.

title: Ballet II
description: Fundamentals of classical ballet technique. Emphasis placed on proper placement, technique and classical ballet vocabulary at an intermediate level.

title: Victimology
description: Examine the relationship between crime and its impact on victims’ lives, including the nature and extent of loss, injury, and damage. Explore the dynamics between victims and offenders, justice-system professionals, family members, and broader societal institutions. Understand the evolution of victims’ rights and related services. Explore the role of restorative principles and practices in pursuing victim restoration.

title: Tap Dance I
description: The course establishes the fundamentals of tap technique. Proper executions of steps such as shuffle, flap, ball change, pull backs, and pick ups, among others.

title: Dance Pedagogy
description: An introduction to dance pedagogy. This course will explore aspects of teaching dance to children (K-12) in both public and private sectors. Students will consider historical trends and as well as current methods of dance pedagogy.

title: Tap II
description: The course establishes the fundamentals of tap technique. Proper executions of steps such as shuffle, flap, ball change, pull backs, and pick ups, among others. Students who have taken Tap I will work toward an intermediate level of tap dance.

title: Modern Dance II
description: Beginning level of modern technique using basic component of space, time and energy. Class work will focus on basic modern techniques combined with dance improvisation techniques. Students who have taken Modern I will work toward an intermediate level of modern techniques.

title: Ballet III
description: For students with extensive ballet training. Intermediate to advanced ballet technique with use of complex classical ballet vocabulary, musicality and artistry.

title: Jazz and Dance Theatre II
description: An introductory-level studio course in jazz dance. This course will focus on basic jazz steps, isolations, strength, jumps, and turns. Students who have taken Jazz I will continue development of jazz techniques.

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record, have second semester junior status, and/or have completed at least 69 credits of undergraduate study. An overall minimum GPA of 3.5, and a minimum GPA of 3.75 for courses in the major is required, and the student must have departmental approval for enrollment.

title: Jazz and Dance Theatre III
description: Building on Jazz I and II, this advanced jazz dance technique course includes more advanced use of isolations, turns, jumps and combinations of jazz movement from various American jazz dance styles.

title: Dance Ministry
description: Explore liturgical dance and various ways dance can be used as a ministry. Learn how to create liturgical dance, organize a dance team and how to perform on church stages. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Theater & Dance.

title: Dance History
description: Reflect on the nature, methods, and purpose of artistic expression by exploring global and cultural dance practices that have found their home in the United States. Recognize and identify noteworthy dance figures, works, and events that have contributed to the history and culture of dance in America. Articulate connections between Christian faith and the arts by researching and developing one’s own dance family tree.

title: Improvisation
description: Introduction to the world of dance improvisation in terms of spatial design, dynamics, rhythm, and motivation. This course explores the utilization of dance improvisation as a fundamental choreographic tool and a vehicle for creative thinking.

title: Ballet IV
description: For students with extensive ballet training. Advanced ballet technique with use of complex classical ballet vocabulary, musicality and artistry. This level would include pointe work.

title: Dance Practicum
description: Dance work under the supervision of a dance professional.

title: Choreography
description: Introduction, development and refinement in the art of making dances. A study of the fundamental elements and structure of dance composition. Class format is approached through improvisation and choreographic assignments.

title: Dance Capstone Project
description: Senior Dance majors will conduct a significant final portfolio project as supervised and approved by the Dance faculty.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study or research in dance under the supervision of an instructor.

title: Modern Dance IV
description: Intermediate/advanced level of modern dance. Deeper understanding and execution of modern techniques and improvisation.

title: Introduction to Data Science
description: This course introduces students to the field of data science. Students will display data, discuss strengths of different data representations, and appropriate use of technology. Students will also learn to articulate career opportunities, ethical considerations, and how Christian faith impacts the data science field.

title: Modern Dance III
description: Intermediate/advanced level of modern dance. Deeper understanding and execution of modern techniques and improvisation.

title: Digital Literacy
description: This course introduces participants to using technologies to tell stories, equipping them to effectively learn new technologies in future contexts. Upon completion of this course, participants will demonstrate competencies gained in many areas including: Web Design, Data Visualization, Audio Production, Video Production, and 3D Printing/CAD Design. All projects require active collaboration with others, consideration of social responsibilities, and an awareness of digital integrity.Â Students need access to a computer with mouse, and mobile video recording device or smart phone to complete this class. Â

title: Video and Church Media Seminar
description: Synthesis of Digital Media disciplines with a focus on video and church media, and introduction to their use by professionals.

title: Time Series Analysis
description: Students will review and expand regression analysis skills from STAT 292 and will learn time series analysis and forecasting. Topics include simple and multiple linear regression, testing and confidence intervals in regression, model building and verification, an introduction to linear time series models, moving average and ARIMA models, estimation and analysis with time series models, forecast errors and confidence intervals. The course includes the use of the R language.

title: Advanced Statistical Methods
description: Students will analyze linear models using matrices and see how the general principles apply in various data settings. Topics include analysis of covariance, multivariate analysis of variance, discriminant analysis, factor analysis, model selection, stepwise regression, and logistic regression. Students will use SAS® and R to analyze data, work individually and in groups to analyze real-world data, and write summaries of the outcomes of these analyses appropriate for various audiences. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Computing, Mathematics & Physics.

title: Foundations in Creative Digital Media
description: Foundational principles, ideas, technologies, and skills of the disciplines of Digital Media, and of the creative process as practiced in those disciplines.

title: Issues in Environmental Economics
description: Introduces the nature and application of economic analysis methodology to environmental problems and issues. Students will analyze case studies and explore policy options relevant to local, national, and international environmental concerns.

title: Economics of Social Issues
description: An examination of particular contemporary social issues such as crime, poverty, environmental quality, discrimination, healthcare, international trade, and unemployment, using analytical tools of economics. Fundamental aspects of the price system are emphasized throughout the course.

title: Principles of Microeconomics
description: A study of the fundamentals of individual market behavior, emphasizing the use of demand and supply analysis. The focus is upon understanding the implications of actions taken by individual decision-makers, operating within various market structures, upon the allocation and use of resources, distribution of income, and ability of markets to meet the needs and desires of individuals. Special attention is given to understanding the impact of market outcomes upon social and individual justice, and upon the environment.

title: Principles of Macroeconomics
description: A study of the fundamentals of economic analysis, with an emphasis on understanding varying motivations behind decisions and on the impact of such decisions on markets such as the labor market (unemployment), the money market (inflation) and the goods market (tax implications). Monetary and fiscal policies are evaluated with the intent of deepening knowledge that fosters humility, conviction and grace.

title: Senior Seminar and Project
description: This is the capstone course for the Digital Media major. Students will reflect on the intersection of their Christian faith with their work in digital media, explore career opportunities, and create a final digital medial project for presentation or exhibition.

title: Jazz and Dance Theatre IV
description: Building on Jazz I, II, and III this course explores dance technique and combinations inspired by Broadway musicals.

title: Cross Cultural: Perspectives on Development
description: This course serves as an introduction to the topic of development through experiential education within a cross-cultural setting in a developing country. Students look at the theology of poverty, and then get an overview of economic and community development from a faith-based perspective. The role of the Church in the development process will also be explored within the study of 'transformational development.'

title: Intermediate Macroeconomics
description: Examines the development of various macroeconomic models as tools for analyzing factors which influence the level of national income, output, inflation, employment, etc. Explains the usefulness and limitations of these models in determining appropriate fiscal, monetary and international trade policies.

title: Intermediate Microeconomics
description: This course studies individual market behavior and applies microeconomic concepts to decision-making processes of business. Primary focus is on demand, cost, production, market structure, and pricing policies.

title: Money and Financial Markets
description: The role of finance in the economy and the nature of money and other financial assets. Emphasizes the role of principal financial markets and their relationship to the economy through study of fund flows, monetary theory, and policy.

title: Comparative Political Economy
description: This course surveys the historical development of economic analysis and current economic schools of thought in a philosophical, social, political, and cultural context. Among the various schools of thought examined are 'Classical' and 'Modern Liberalism,' Marxian, Keynesian, and 'New' Classical/Supply-Side Analysis. An emphasis is placed upon examining and critiquing the underlying methodological and philosophical principles of each school of thought, and exploring the implicit and explicit links between economics and politics.

title: The Economics of Wages and Employment
description: The economics of labor markets, labor unions, and collective bargaining. Topics include labor force participation, labor mobility, employment and unemployment, wage rate determination (education, training, labor market discrimination, and collective bargaining strategies), and public policies affecting income distribution. Both orthodox and heterodox frameworks of analysis are considered, with attention placed on questions of justice and equity.

title: Microfinance
description: The goal of this course is to help students gain a holistic understanding of poverty, the role that microfinance initiatives can play in alleviating poverty, how to access financial resources, design and deliver products as well as monitor and evaluate the fulfillment of an organization's mission. The course specifically highlights the importance and relevance of microfinance when done from a Christian perspective.

title: Economic Development I
description: Examination of the process of economic development with reference to developing countries. Considers a variety of case studies and viewpoints. Fulfills Writing in the Major for Economic Development Majors.

title: Topics in Economics
description: Each offering of this course will deal with a different area of economic analysis. Possible topics include public finance, comparative economic systems, urban and regional economics, and econometrics.

title: Game and Art Design Seminar
description: Synthesis of Digital Media disciplines with a focus on game and art design, and introduction to their use by professionals.

title: Disability and Society
description: Examine contemporary societal views about disability, and function of systems serving individuals with disabilities in the United States, including how disability interacts with marginalized race, ethnicity, gender, socio economic status, and/or religion. Employ socio-cultural and Christian faith frameworks and perspectives to reason about disability in society, including barriers experienced by individuals with disabilities, and one’s role in promoting good for individuals with disabilities. Fulfills Intercultural Perspectives-US General Education Requirement.

title: Introduction to Special Education
description: The purpose of this course is to introduce students to historical, philosophical, ethical, and legal foundations of special education and human exceptionality. In addition, the principles of inclusive education including differentiated instruction and Universal Design for Learning (UDL) are addressed.

title: Economics Practicum
description: Practical business-related experience under professional supervision or supervised participation as a teaching assistant in departmental courses.

title: Field Experience in Development: Developing Country or Urban Development
description: Field experience in urban development or developing country. Students fulfilling this requirement through IBI, IDCC 260 (2nd developing country cross-cultural in addition to ECON 260 ) should register for ECON 393 for 0 credits.

title: Economic Development II
description: The main goal of this course is to get students to begin to think critically about development theory and practice. The course analyzes cross-country differences in development and focusses on problems related to the process of economic development, in the countries of Asia, Africa, and Latin America in particular. Special topics include the gendered impact of economic development policy, the international economics of development, and development strategies.

title: Inclusion Practices
description: In this course students will learn to use formal and informal assessment to guide instructional planning and decision making for the purpose of providing appropriate programs for all learners with special needs in the regular education classroom, including learners from culturally and/or linguistically diverse backgrounds. Special attention will be given to instructional adaptations and accommodations within the general curriculum, instructional strategies, evidence-based practices, Universal Design for Learning (UDL), differentiated instruction, creating a positive learning environment, behavioral management techniques and team collaboration.

title: High Incidence Instructional Strategies
description: In this course students will learn to use formal and informal assessment to guide instructional planning and decision making for the purpose of providing appropriate educational programs for learners with high incidence disabilities, including learners from culturally and/or linguistically diverse backgrounds. Special attention will be given to instructional adaptations, accommodations and modifications within the general curriculum, and evidence-based intervention strategies for reading, writing, mathematics and general learning strategies.

title: Low Incidence Instructional Strategies
description: In this course students will learn to use formal and informal assessment to guide instructional planning and decision making for the purpose of providing appropriate educational programs for learners with low incidence disabilities, including learners from culturally and/or linguistically diverse backgrounds. Special attention will be given to instructional adaptations, accommodations and modifications within modified general curriculum, functional curriculums, evidence-based instructional strategies, differentiated instruction, creating a positive learning environment, and team collaboration.

title: Infants, Toddlers & Families
description: This course examines the role of the family and other caregivers in the development of infants and toddlers (ages 0-3). Early intervention and assessment techniques will be explored along with developmentally appropriate practices. Students will become familiar with the Individualized Family Service Plan (IFSP), and other resources and services that may be available to infants and toddlers and their families.

title: Psychological Testing
description: This course is a detailed study of screening, assessment, and diagnostic procedures and instruments used in determining eligibility, and planning and evaluating instruction for individuals with disabilities from birth through 12th grade who are accessing the general education curriculum or functional curricula. It includes the historical, philosophical, and sociological foundations of assessment and its relationship to educational decision-making and instructional design, measuring student attainment of essential skills in a standards-based environment, and interpreting assessment data to inform educational decisions.

title: Positive Behavior Support for Learners with Emotional, Behavioral & Social Disabilities
description: This course is designed to engage learners in thoughtful exploration and discussion of models of human behavior, principles of behavior management and practical application of evidence-based behavioral strategies. Application emphasizes both proactive and responsive interventions for learners with behavioral, emotional and social disabilities. Included will be foundations in interpreting, assessing and managing behavior, strategies to prevent and remediate challenging behavior, and strategies for teaching pro-social replacement behavior by using functional assessment and applying positive behavior supports.

title: Teaching English Language Learners in K-12 Schools
description: This course provides an experiential approach to studying linguistic and cultural diversity in the classroom, as well as theories and issues of second language acquisition. Students will acquire knowledge, skills, and dispositions necessary for teachers to address instructional needs of English Language Learners (ELLs). Students will learn strategies for creating classrooms that promote language content learning, as well as an appreciation for diversity. This course includes 20 hours of self-directed cross-cultural experiences, outside the public school system.

title: Principles and Structures of Mathematics
description: This math content course will give students deep knowledge of the foundational math content that is covered in the elementary grades. While students will already have learned some of this content during their own K-12 experience, the research on mathematics teaching and learning in the U.S. tells us that most students will have significant gaps in their understanding and many will have negative affect toward the study of mathematics. By providing them with a strong foundational understanding of topics such as number theory, algebra, fractions, and geometry, students will be better prepared cognitively and affectively to teach these topics to children. Course objectives will be aligned with the PA Academic Standards and Assessment Anchors for elementary grades.

title: Language Development
description: Principles of language development during early childhood years: theory, structure, semantics, and phonetics. Sociocultural influences and resultant implications, relationships of language development and reading readiness, and basic assumptions for a language development program.

title: Cultural and Political Geography
description: This course will introduce students to the interdisciplinary field of social studies. The course will include content from the disciplines of history, geography, economics, and anthropology. Students will learn ways in which history and economics are influenced by physical and human geography. The course will use the study of geography to develop students' understanding of interactions between individuals and groups in historical and contemporary contexts.

title: Literacy: Climate, Curriculum & Instruction II
description: Examination of reading, writing, speaking, and listening in the elementary intermediate grades. Emphasis on concepts of literacy as students transition from 'learning to read' to 'reading to learn.' Includes content-specific subjects, study skills, and the use of technology. Must be taken at the Grantham campus. Open to interested students who are not pursuing teacher certification with instructor approval.

title: Children's Literature
description: Critical and extensive study of children's literature and its application to the classroom with attention to requirements of text, illustration, and historical development. Includes exploration of a variety of genres, socio-cultural considerations, censorship issues, and technology implications.

title: Communication Disorders
description: This course is designed to acquaint students with the fields of speech language pathology and audiology. We will discuss a range of communication processes as well as common communication disorders that affect these processes across the lifespan. Special emphasis will be given to what speech-language pathologist (SLPs) and audiologists do to facilitate communication in individuals with communication impairments including family interventions and direct interventions.

title: Literacy: Climate, Curriculum and Instruction I
description: Introduction to reading theory for the primary grades. Emphasizes the transactional nature of the reading and writing process, the classroom teacher's role, approaches to early literacy practices, and the relationship between assessment and instruction. Includes the connection of reading, writing, speaking, and listening to the total curriculum.

title: Mathematics: Climate, Curriculum and Instruction for Primary Grades
description: Teaching strategies for mathematics in the primary grades including children's development of number ideas, problem solving, computation, calculator, and computer applications, and philosophy and content of primary math programs.

title: English Language Learning in TESOL/ESL Classrooms
description: This course examines the linguistic and grammatical structures of English as contextualized in an English language development classroom. Students will examine and apply these concepts with an emphasis in integrating them into their teaching.

title: Middle Level Methods
description: This course will provide students with opportunities to explore the school-related world of young adolescents. The purpose of the course is to show the intersection of school and adolescence and how this intersection can best be structured to meet the needs of middle school students. The course will introduce students to the philosophy of middle schools and show how effective middle schools and middle school classrooms address the unique needs of young adolescents. Students in this course will also be enrolled in a field experience in a grade 6-8 setting. In this field experience they will be able to apply their understanding of young adolescents and the middle school philosophy to a real life context.

title: Mathematics: Climate, Curriculum and Instruction for Intermediate Grades (with Field Experience)
description: Teaching strategies for math in the intermediate grades including integers, rationals, continuation of mathematical problem-solving, geometry, metrics, assessment, error diagnosis at pre-algebra level, use of calculators and computers in math programs and philosophy and content of intermediate grade math programs. Must be taken at the Grantham campus. Open to interested students who are not pursuing teacher certification with instructor approval.

title: Educational Psychology
description: This course examines the theoretical concepts and principles necessary for understanding human thought and behavior in various educational environments. The course focuses on variables employed by educational professionals during teaching and learning. The course explores memory, transfer, problem-solving, motivation, instructional planning, classroom management, and assessment. The course also explores the links between Educational Psychology and the unique role of the Christian Educator and their personal faith journey.

title: TESOL/ESL Methods and Assessment
description: This course examines traditional and current methodology and assessment practices in the teaching of English as a second and foreign language. This course focuses on English language teaching in diverse contexts to a broad range of ages and levels, in the four domains; reading, writing, speaking, listening. This course aligns language acquisition theory with lesson design, delivery, and assessment.

title: Instructional Design I
description: Students develop instructional planning and assessment skills by applying a systems model of instructional design. Emphasis is on thinking and decision-making processes necessary for developing instruction and evaluating the achievement of learning goals in elementary and early childhood learning environments. Topics include instructional goals, learning outcomes, content analysis, classroom assessment strategies, instructional objectives, instructional technology, instructional sequences, lesson planning, and unit planning. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Education.

title: Preschool Field Experience
description: In this field experience, early childhood education minors will spend approximately 80 hours in an early childhood center. Students will do a minimal amount of classroom observation to become acclimated to their particular context, but most of the time will be spent working with children individually, in small groups, and large group settings. Students will complete assignments from concurrent courses, using the PA Learning Standards for Early Childhood.

title: Social Studies: Climate, Curriculum and Instruction for Elementary School
description: Consideration is given to the aims, content, and organization of social studies appropriate to the elementary school and to methods and materials utilized in social studies.

title: Instructional Design and Assessment for Middle and Secondary Grades
description: Students develop instructional planning and assessment skills by applying a systems model of instructional design. Emphasis on thinking and decision-making processes necessary for developing instruction and evaluating the achievement of learning goals in secondary-level learning environments. Topics include instructional goals, learning outcomes, content analysis, classroom assessment strategies, instructional objectives, instructional technology, instructional sequences, lesson planning, and unit planning. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Education.

title: Education Practicum: Socio-cultural Variability
description: Structured opportunities for students to experience educational environments that reflect significant degrees of learner diversity (e.g., socioeconomic, ethnic, racial, linguistic, familial). Students spend off-campus time observing, analyzing, assisting, and completing course assignments in the field. Class sessions engage students in group discussion, reflection, and problem-solving activities related to cultural variables within their particular field placements.

title: Socio-Cultural Perspectives on Education
description: Examines contemporary issues on diversity and pluralism in schools and society and discusses how sociocultural variables including race, ethnicity, gender, culture, socio-economic class, sexuality, ability, language, religion, spatial location, and age impact schooling and learning. Provides new insights for students to examine how power and identity shape classroom pedagogy, and critiques current school practices as a way to promote classroom and school environment that is supportive of all students, their communities, and teachers. Employ critical “reconstructionist” pedagogy and “Christian faith” frameworks as the underlying philosophy, and focuses on knowledge, reflection, and action (praxis) as the basis for social justice and change.

title: Field Experiences in ECE
description: In this senior level experience, students will participate in developing, teaching, assessing, and modifying curriculum for preschool children, in accordance with the PA Learning Standards for Early Childhood. Students will do both independent and collaborative planning and teaching in the Lab School program at the Early Learning Center, under the supervision of College faculty.

title: TESOL/ESL Internship
description: This 40 hour internship involves observing, lesson planning, and reflective teaching in a setting where English is taught as another language. This course requires clearances and that students provide their own transportation for the field experience.

title: Seminar and Field Experience in Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages
description: This course provides advanced study of TESOL methodology, with opportunity to combine theory and practice in diverse contexts including ministry contexts. A 40-hour supervised field experience is required. Teacher education students will complete a minimum of 30 field experience hours in the public school system, in grades 3-12. Non-teacher education students complete the field experience through the Agape Center. (International options may also be possible). This course requires clearances and that students provide their own transportation for the field experience.

title: Seminar in TESOL/ESL
description: This course encourages students to view the field of English language learning in multiple contexts. Students will learn from global and local professionals in TESOL and will consider these contexts while developing their philosophy of teaching.

title: Student Teaching Seminar
description: This seminar is intended to be part of the senior capstone experience (along with EDUC 420 ) for our students. Students will examine critical issues in Education and schools. Faculty will support student reflection on these issues and students' responses as Christians in the field of Education. Faculty will also use this time to clarify essential practices to enhance the professional development semester. Faculty or supervisors may lead the seminars or may bring in experts to speak on a specific topic.

title: Evidence-Based Practices in Early Childhood Education
description: Considers the connection and application of theory and practice and enhances the student teaching experience through research of current issues, individualized projects, and self-evaluation.

title: Developmental Programming: Curriculum and Instruction for Early Childhood
description: Study of the theory and methods of building programs to facilitate the appropriate development of preschool children with special emphasis on operational and administrative procedures.

title: Independent Study
description: Selected topics in education taken independently with a faculty member.

title: Professional Issues in Educational Studies
description: Considers educational issues in the following areas: philosophy of education, legal and ethical dimensions of the profession, school culture and relationships with community stakeholders, appropriate roles, attitudes, and actions of Christians in educational contexts, and reflections as a means for advocacy, improved practice, and reconciliation .

title: Professional Issues in Education
description: Considers educational issues in the following areas: classroom management, PDE regulations and school law, professional ethics, school administration, curriculum regulations, special needs learners, health-related issues, professional negotiations, and other current issues/trends in education.

title: Science: Climate, Curriculum and Instruction for Elementary School
description: A lecture and activity designed methods course based on the National Science Education Standards (NSES). Emphasis on development of critical thinking skills, inquiry-based learning activities, classroom laboratory/activity safety procedures, integration of science with other content areas, participation in environmental programs, and application of learning theories.

title: Experiential Learning: Service Learning
description: Experiential Learning Service.

title: Experiential Learning: Practicum
description: Experiential Learning Practicum.

title: Experiential Learning: Leadership
description: Experiential Learning Leadership.

title: Experiential Learning: Research
description: Experiential Learning Research. Students may register for this course in conjunction with the credit-bearing research course or use for non-credit bearing research experiences.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent scholarship supervised by one or more Department of Education faculty mentors for junior- and senior-level students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Introduction to Poetry
description: Read, discuss, and respond to significant poems and poets representing a diverse range of eras, identities, and poetic practices. Explore the techniques of diverse literary expressions within the poetic tradition and reflect on the active role poetry plays in personal growth and social transformation. Readings will vary by semester and instructor.

title: Critical Reading and Writing
description: Students develop skills in ethical and effective writing through the critical processes of thinking, reading, research, and drafting. This course emphasizes academic inquiry, rhetorical knowledge, argumentation, conventions, and collaboration to support student writing in diverse academic, public, and digital genres.

title: From Script to Screen
description: Introduction to strategies for interpreting major plays written in English with primary focus on Shakespeare. Evaluates changes and adaptations as plays are transformed into film, with a goal of helping students become critical viewers of the screen.

title: Short Story
description: Read, discuss, and respond to significant short stories. Explore the techniques of diverse literary expressions within the short story tradition and reflect on the active role stories play in personal growth and social transformation. Readings will vary by semester and instructor but will reflect diverse forms and content and may include stories from the 19th century through the contemporary period and from around the world.

title: Writings of the Inklings
description: Read, discuss, and respond to literature composed by the Inklings, the British writing group that numbered among its members C. S. Lewis and J. R. R. Tolkien, as well as their peers and inspirers. Explore the various themes, genres, and modes of expression that united and distinguished this influential group of writers. Readings will vary by semester and instructor.

title: Selected Works of British Literature
description: Read, discuss, and respond to significant British literary texts. Explore the techniques of diverse literary expressions within British traditions and reflect on the active role literature plays in personal growth and social transformation. Readings will vary by semester and instructor but will reflect diverse British experiences and may include the medieval through the contemporary periods and genres in poetry, drama, fiction, and nonfiction.

title: Nineteenth Century British Women Writers
description: Introduction to several genres of literature written by outstanding British women writers of the 19th century. Through its focus on early writings in the women's movement, the course will provide a context for developments in 20th-century women's discourse.

title: Introduction to Shakespeare
description: Read, discuss, and respond to Shakespeare’s dramatic and literary texts. Explore the techniques of diverse literary expressions within Shakespeare’s diverse canon and reflect on the active role literature plays in personal growth and social transformation. Readings will vary by semester and instructor but will reflect Shakespeare’s many ways of exploring and examining both individual and communal human life.

title: Selected Works of American Literature
description: Read, discuss, and respond to literature by significant American writers. Explore the techniques of diverse literary expressions within the American tradition and reflect on the active role literature plays in personal growth and social transformation. Readings will vary by semester and instructor but will reflect diverse American experiences and may include the colonial through the contemporary periods and genres in fiction, nonfiction, poetry, and drama.

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent scholarship supervised by one or more Department of Education faculty mentors for junior- and senior-level students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Workshop: Critical Reading and Writing
description: Students develop skills in ethical and effective writing through the critical processes of thinking, reading, research, and drafting. This course emphasizes academic inquiry, rhetorical knowledge, argumentation, conventions, and collaboration to support student writing in diverse academic, public, and digital genres. Taken in conjunction with ENGL 110 , this workshop supports students to make progress on ENGL 110 writing projects with direct support from the instructor; participate with peers in collaborative reading, writing, and research workshops designed by the instructor; identify and make progress on individual writing needs, skills, and goals with instructor; and reflect on their learning and development as college writers.

title: Introduction to Ethnic Literatures of the United States
description: Introduction to literature that explores the cultural diversity of the United States. Readings may be selected from the variety of ethnic literatures in the United States. At the discretion of the instructor individual courses may focus on a single ethnic literature such as African American or Chinese American literature, or may focus on comparing the literatures of various ethnic groups.

title: Introduction to World Literature
description: Read, discuss, and respond to significant World literary texts. Explore the techniques of diverse literary expressions within World traditions and reflect on the active role literature plays in personal growth and social transformation. Readings will vary by semester and instructor but will reflect diverse non-American experiences and may include ancient as well as contemporary poetry, drama, fiction, and nonfiction from around the world.

title: Of Gods and Humans
description: A study of some of the most important literary works of the Ancient World (Middle East, Greece, and Rome) with consideration given to the present relevance of the works as well as to their classical greatness.

title: Studies in the Bible as Literature
description: Study of selections from the Bible with particular reference to literary types.

title: Literature and the Life of Faith
description: Read, discuss, and respond to fiction, poetry, and essays, primarily in the Judeo-Christian tradition, which engage issues of faith and life. Readings will vary by semester and instructor but will reflect the faith experiences of a diverse array of Judeo Christian writers and thinkers, such as Chaim Potok, Marilynne Robinson, and Shusaku Endo, and may include a range of periods, geographies, and genres.

title: Literature and the Environment
description: Introduction to authors and works from the body of literature dealing with nature, creation, and the environment.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: This course is a multi-genre introduction to creative writing. Students examine the craft of writing by reading closely across a broad spectrum of literary genres and producing creative work of their own. Students write, workshop, and revise original poetry and fiction while participating in a collaborative writing community.

title: Introduction to English Studies II
description: This course emphasizes the development of research skills for literary study, writing, and vocation formation. Students implement methods of literary analysis, read deeply within the critical and scholarly conversations about literary texts, design and complete a major literary research project, and begin to explore vocational and professional possibilities for their study of English. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Language, Literature & Writing.

title: Critical Theory
description: This course explores how different critical approaches shape the experience of literary texts. Students examine, compare, and practice a wide variety of established critical approaches to literary study, such as theories of Formalism, Psychoanalysis, Marxism, Reader Response, Deconstruction, Cultural Studies, Post-colonialism, Gender, Race, and Disability, in order to understand better the role of the critic in producing knowledge and to develop their own critical capacities. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Language, Literature & Writing.

title: Introduction to Professional Writing & Rhetoric
description: This course introduces students to the principles of writing in a range of professional genres and presents options that they may pursue in future course work and careers.

title: Linguistics
description: This course provides an introduction to language and linguistics with an emphasis on tools and methods for language study.

title: Topics in Creative Writing
description: This upper-level course examines a special topic in Creative Writing.

title: Formal Poetry Workshop
description: This upper-level creative writing workshop, explores the art and craft of writing poetry using traditional forms, including blank verse, sonnets, villanelles, sestinas, and other metered or rhyming forms. Using the workshop model, students read, write, and revise their original formal poetry.

title: Teaching English in Secondary Schools
description: This course introduces students to theory and best practices of teaching English at the middle and high school level. The course will introduce students to current theory and effective strategies for teaching writing, reading comprehension and literature, grammar, oral communication, listening, and visual media. Students will discuss and develop English curriculum informed by Pennsylvania's Standards Aligned System, and the National Council of Teachers of English and International Reading Association standards, as well as engage in their own research to improve teaching.

title: Fiction Workshop
description: This upper-level creative writing workshop provides instruction, practice, and an audience for the writing of original contemporary short fiction. By reading, talking about, writing, and workshopping short fiction, students develop an understanding of conventional and contemporary practice in fiction writing, and skills, working habits, and attitudes appropriate to those who wish to write fiction of diverse genres.

title: Free Verse Poetry Workshop
description: This upper-level creative writing workshop, explores the art and craft of writing free verse or open form poetry. Using the workshop model, students read, write, and revise their original free verse poetry, learning to develop shape, tension, and music in their poems without the use of traditional forms.

title: Playwriting Workshop
description: This upper-level creative writing workshop explores the art and craft of writing for the stage. Through weekly assignments, peer collaborations, and extensive classroom analysis, students produce a short play, as well as numerous scenes and dramatic exercises.

title: Women's Literature
description: Read, discuss, and respond to literature by significant women writers. Explore the techniques of diverse literary expressions, consider the intersections of gender and literature, and reflect on the active role literature plays in personal growth and social transformation. Readings will vary by semester and instructor but will reflect diverse women’s experiences and may include selections across historical periods and global communities and from genres in fiction, nonfiction, poetry, and drama.

title: Literary Nonfiction Workshop
description: This upper-level creative writing workshop considers the nonfiction essay in several forms. Students examine voice, craft and content in the tradition of the personal essay, consider what it means to use research in this genre, and write their own literary nonfiction pieces. Students collaborate to workshop the essays produced in the class, read carefully and deeply, and converse broadly on the concerns that attend this genre.

title: Introduction to English Studies I
description: This course emphasizes close reading skills and methods of literary analysis in three major genres â poetry, fiction, and drama. Students read selected literary texts from American, British, and Global Non-Western traditions in the context of the kinds of questions explored in the English major curriculum.

title: Writing for Social Change
description: This upper-level professional writing course explores the strategies, genres and media used by citizens, activists, and scholars advocating for social change through writing. Students practice strategies as writers and agents of social change.

title: Writing Studies
description: This course introduces current theory and pedagogical practices in writing studies. Students examine various notions of writing and the implications of these for their own practices as writers, editors, tutors, or prospective teachers.

title: Nature and Travel Writing
description: This upper-level professional writing course examines the conventions of nature and travel writing for diverse audiences. Drawing on personal experience and research, students read and discuss both professional and literary authors such as Henry David Thoreau and Wendell Berry who write in this form and then practice the conventions of the form in their own original place-based writing.

title: Topics in Professional Writing
description: This upper-level course examines a special topic in Professional Writing.

title: Writing for the Workplace
description: This upper-level professional writing course teaches the basic principles of communication in the workplace, and offers practice in writing and presenting effective memos, letters, reports, proposals, and other workplace documents. It is designed to improve clarity, conciseness, correctness and efficiency in writing for the workplace.

title: Victorian Literature
description: This course examines selected poetry and prose of the Victorian era in the context of the challenges posed to traditional Christian faith by the advent of new discoveries in science and new critical approaches to reading biblical texts that challenged long-standing interpretations both of particular biblical texts and of what kind of book the Bible is. The primary focus will be on the Victorian novel, especially George Eliot and Thomas Hardy. This course falls in the Questions of Faith in Literature category.

title: John Milton
description: This course examines selected poetry and prose of perhaps the most important Christian poet in the English language with close consideration of how his dynamic Christian faith evolved over the course of his long career in response to political and religious upheavals in mid-seventeenth-century England, as well crises in his personal life â his first marriage and his blindness. The primary focus will be on Miltonâs epic, Paradise Lost. This course falls in the Questions of Faith in Literature category.

title: Slavery & Abolition in Literature
description: This course examines stories of enslavement and freedom by writers of American poetry and prose. Students interpret literary texts as aesthetic, political, social, and theological objects, paying special attention to the ways American writers adapt, interpret, and critique biblical texts as contributors to the practice of enslavement and freedom in the United States. The course may include authors such as Frederick Douglass, Harriet Beecher Stowe, Herman Melville, Mark Twain, Richard Wright, and Toni Morrison. This course falls in the Questions of Faith in Literature category.

title: Topics in Literature in History
description: This course examines a special topic in the Questions of Literature in History category. Courses in this category explore the ways authors and literary texts shape and are shaped by aesthetic and socio-political histories.

title: The Inklings
description: Courses offered under this heading might include a survey of later British literature, genre studies such as Victorian poetry or postmodern British fiction, literary movements such as the Romantic poets, or specific author courses such as Jane Austen, George Eliot, or James Joyce.

title: Romanticism
description: This course examines selected British and American writing from the Romantic Period of the late 18th and early 19th centuries. It considers how literature cultivated a Romantic spirit emphasizing the individual, the imagination, and the natural world. The course may include essays from American Transcendentalists, slave narratives by Frederick Douglass and Harriet Jacobs, Gothic fiction by Mary Shelly and Edgar Allan Poe, and poetry from William Wordsworth, John Keats, Emily Dickinson, and Walt Whitman. This course falls in the Questions of Literature in History category.

title: Medieval/Renaissance Literature
description: This course surveys selected lyric, narrative, and dramatic poetry from Beowulf to the contemporaries of Shakespeare with special attention to historical changes that shift the medieval worldview into early modern understandings of God, humanity, and the purpose of life and literature that came to be called "The Renaissance." Texts may include Chaucerâs Canterbury Tales, Danteâs Divine Comedy, Christopher Marloweâs plays, and lyric poetry by John Donne and George Herbert. This course falls in the Questions of Literature in History category.

title: Modernism
description: This course examines selected poetry and prose in Europe and America during the first half of the twentieth century in the context of enormous upheavals in worldviews generated by thinkers like Marx, Freud, and Nietzsche, as well as the devastating effect of two world wars. It considers how literature both mirrors and informs significant historical changes in human understandings of self and society â and especially the purpose of literary art. The course may include authors such as Virginia Woolf, James Joyce, T.S. Eliot, William Carlos Williams, and Wallace Stevens. This course falls in the Questions of Literature in History category.

title: Ethnic Literatures of the US
description: The course focuses on advanced study of literature that emphasizes the ethnic diversity of the United States. Particular attention is given to intersection of race and ethnicity as reflected in the form and content of literary works. Readings may focus on a single ethnic literature or compare the literatures of various ethnic groups. The course may include authors such as W.E.B. DuBois, Langston Hughes, Art Spiegelman, Maxine Hong Kingston, Edwidge Danticat, Sherman Alexie, Junot DÃ­az, Colson Whitehead, and Jhumpa Lahiri. This course falls in the Questions of Culture, Identity, and Power category.

title: Topics in Culture, Identity, Power
description: This course examines a special topic in the Questions of Culture, Identity, and Power category. Courses in this category explore the ways authors and literary texts expose and participate in cultural systems of power, including race, ethnicity, gender, and class.

title: American Women's Writing
description: This course examines literary texts by American women writers with diverse voices, experiences, and perspectives of life in the United States throughout the 20th and 21st centuries. The course emphasizes a chronological history of women writers and gives special attention to the political, cultural, and literary contexts that shape their writing as well as the ways their writing shapes politics, culture, and art. The course may include authors such as Kate Chopin, Nella Larsen, MinÃ© Okubo, Sylvia Plath, Sandra Cisneros, Marilyn Robinson, Alice Walker, Toni Morrison, and Louise Erdrich. This course falls in the Questions of Culture, Identity, and Power category.

title: Postcolonial/World Literature
description: This course examines non-Western literature from Latin America, Asia, Africa, and Arabia, exploring different understandings of culture and identity, especially as those understandings differ from western, European models of culture and identity. The course may include authors such as Arundati Roy, Mahmoud Darwish, Chinua Achebe, Huzama Habayeb, and Ibrahim Nasrallah. This course falls in the Questions of Culture, Identity, and Power category.

title: Magazine Writing
description: This course introduces magazine journalism, entailing the planning, research, and writing of several feature articles, and their submission for publication. It emphasizes interview techniques, correspondence with editors, and the writerâs development of long-range publishing plans.

title: Topics in Faith & Literature
description: This course examines a special topic in the Questions of Faith in Literature category. Courses in this category explore the ways authors and literary texts practice, interpret, and critique Christian faith.

title: Ancient Epic
description: This course examines the formation and development of the classical epic from its oral beginnings in The Epic of Gilgamesh and Homerâs Iliad and Odyssey. Subsequent texts may include Virgilâs Aeneid, Ovidâs Metamorphoses, and Lucretiusâ On the Nature of Things. This course falls in the Questions of Form, Genre, and Craft category.

title: Topics in Form, Genre, Craft
description: This course examines a special topic in the Questions of Form, Genre, and Craft category. Courses in this category explore authorsâ approaches to craft and examine literary texts as they participate in and challenge conventions of form, genre, and craft.

title: Shakespeare
description: This course examines selected plays by Shakespeare with special attention to how he reworks generic forms such as comedy, history, tragedy, and romance both in relation to previous drama and in relation to his own plays. Shakespeare is unique in the way he reworks his own revisions to traditions in dramatic genres. This course may survey multiple genres or focus on a single genre such as comedy or tragedy. This course falls in the Questions of Form, Genre, and Craft category.

title: Young Adult Literature
description: This course examines the ongoing formation and development of diverse young adult literary forms, including realist novels, fantasy, science fiction, verse novels, and graphic novels. This course emphasizes a chronological history of the young adult genre, and invites students to respond the literary texts from the perspective of educators, scholars, and creative writers. The course may include authors such as Madeline LâEngle, Harper Lee, S.E. Hinton, Walter Dean Myers, Jacqueline Woodson, Gene Luen Yang, and Angie Thomas. This course falls in the Questions of Form, Genre, and Craft category.

title: Vocation Seminar
description: This course prepares students to connect the English major to the world of work. Students read and discuss diverse academic, creative, and professional sources to examine and articulate the value of the work of English; identify and cultivate the skills applicable to specific fields of interest; and link those skills to specific work through interviewing and shadowing experiences.

title: Staff Writer Practicum
description: Staff Writers who have distinguished themselves in style and profession (determined by faculty in English and Communications) may receive one elective credit per semester for significant written contributions to The Swinging Bridge on a regular basis: this entails submitting at least one dedicated story per issue and attending (helping with) training sessions and staff meetings.

title: Contemporary Poetry
description: This course examines the ongoing development of contemporary poetic forms and genres through the study of diverse poetic voices that inhabit, reflect, and produce contemporary cultures through the craft and practice of poetry. The course may include authors such as Li-Young Lee, Louise GlÃ¼ck, Claudia Rankine, Natasha Trethewey, and Natalie Diaz. This course falls in the Questions of Form, Genre, and Craft category.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study or research (normally no more than three credits per term) under the supervision of an instructor whose approval must precede enrollment

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent research program for junior- and senior-level students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Introduction to Engineering
description: Develop essential skills in creative thinking, problem-solving, and communication. Learn and apply the engineering design process while developing as a team member. Broad exposure is provided to various engineering disciplines and practices, along with fundamentals of CAD software, electronics, and machine shop tools.

title: Language, Literature, and Writing Seminar
description: Seminar students collectively explore, research, and reflect on the discipline and vocation of language, literature, and writing. Through collective reading, significant discussion, and individual writing projects, students will demonstrate ethical analysis of cultures and texts, articulate the integration of Christian faith in fields related to their areas of study, and explore vocational opportunities in those fields of study

title: Major Honors I
description: Independent research program for junior- and senior-level students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Lab: Engineering Design Tools
description: Lab: Engineering Design Tools.

title: Project Management
description: Enhance skills related to teamwork, project planning, risk management, and technical writing. Work in teams to plan and carry out a semester-long project, in preparation for the Engineering Project course sequence.

title: Lab: Introduction to Engineering
description: Lab: ENGR 111 Introduction to Engineering.

title: Student Publications Practicum
description: The editor-in-chief of official student publications may receive three elective credits per semester for significant contributions to the publications on a regular basis. The section editors of the official student publications may receive one elective credit per semester for significant contributions to the publications on a regular basis. May be repeated for a total of six credits. Students must complete both ENGL 112 Newswriting and ENGL 305 Writing: Magazine Writing prior to or concurrently with their service as editors in order to receive academic credit.

title: English-Journalism Practicum
description: Work experience under professional supervision in areas such as public relations, journalism, and publishing.

title: Engineering Design Tools
description: Develop key skills for success in engineering across three categories: visualization and engineering graphics, spreadsheet and numerical analysis, and computer aided design software. Develop key skills for success in engineering across three categories: visualization and engineering graphics, spreadsheet and numerical analysis, and computer aided design software.

title: Lab: Programming for Engineers
description: Lab: Programming for Engineers.

title: Programming for Engineers
description: Develop computer programming skills using the Matlab programming environment. The course develops general programming techniques through a series of assignments and an overall course project that emphasizes solving engineering problems and applying these techniques, and the engineering design process, to deliver and demonstrate a final course project.

title: Materials Engineering
description: Study the relationship between material properties and micro-structure. Examine various processing techniques that alter the micro-structure allowing the engineer to control properties. Select appropriate materials for various design and manufacturing settings. Metals and alloys are emphasized with a survey of ceramic, polymer and composite materials

title: Engineering Statistics
description: Learn statistical methods for common engineering applications, including probability, descriptive statistics, confidence intervals, and graphical presentation. Students learn experimental design and perform data analysis and interpretation through hypothesis tests and analysis of variance as well as regression of single and multi-variable systems. Students design an experiment, analyze the results, and produce a report.

title: Lab: Materials Engineering
description: Lab: Materials Engineering.

title: Mechanics II
description: Develop advanced skills related to stress and strain associated with mechanical, structural, and geotechnical engineering. Determine the internal state of stress due to complex load and boundary conditions. Indeterminate analysis and stress transformations are included.  Calculate deflections for systems under various load conditions. Principles for design of mechanical/structural members are emphasized, including introduction to finite element analysis software and application of common failure theories.

title: Control Systems
description: Analyze and design open loop and feedback control systems for electrical and mechanical applications. Consider transient and steady-state behavior, analyze system stability, and apply root locus and frequency response methods to design control systems. MATLAB/Simulink computer simulations verified with laboratory implementation.

title: Lab: Circuits I
description: Lab: Circuits l.

title: Seminar I
description: Critically evaluate cultural and personal perspectives related to Christian faith, vocation, technology, and engineering practice. Consider the various constraints that impact engineering design. Learn career search strategies and prepare a professional resume. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Engineering.

title: Lab: Control Systems
description: Lab: ENGR 324 Control Systems.

title: Lab: Biomechanics
description: Lab: ENGR 331 Biomechanics.

title: Seminar II
description: Integrate principles of Christian faith and vocation particularly regarding discipleship, stewardship, leadership, and purposeful living. Assess engineering ethics practice in light of biblical witness and apply professional engineering ethical codes to practical case studies.  Students articulate their personal vocational vision in a culminating capstone paper. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Engineering.

title: Biomechanics
description: Study the mechanics and motor control of human movement. Quantitatively assess human motion by applying principles of physics, calculus, programming, classical mechanics, and anatomy to topics such as balance and gait. Complete a research project including literature review, data collection and analysis, and crafting a scientific paper.

title: Biomedical Laboratory Techniques
description: Develop foundational skills in biomedical laboratory techniques including microscopy, aseptic cell culture, DNA isolation, polymerase chain reaction, electrophoresis, and image analysis. Develop skills related to record-keeping, applying the scientific method, documenting protocol, and communicating findings.

title: Lab: Construction Methods and Materials
description: Lab: ENGR 344 Construction Methods and Materials.

title: Lab: Structural Design I
description: Lab: ENGR 341 Structural Design l.

title: Mechanics I
description: Develop core competencies associated with mechanical, biomechanical, and structural engineering. Physics principles are used to determine internal and external forces in various types of members, including introduction to analysis software. The material's response to load - stress and strain - is also emphasized. Principles for design of simple mechanical and structural elements are introduced.

title: Construction Methods and Materials
description: Develop core competencies in three civil engineering areas - construction engineering management, construction materials, and surveying. The construction engineering management unit includes project delivery methods, estimating, and economics considerations. The construction materials unit includes concrete, steel, wood, asphalt, and geosynthetic material characteristics. The surveying unit covers basic leveling and traversing methods and introduces modern total station techniques.

title: Circuits I
description: Develop foundational skills for analyzing and designing both analog and digital circuits in a blended introductory course. Emphasis is on direct current analog circuits, op amp circuits, and combinational logic circuits. Time dependent response of RC circuits and latches are also introduced. Students design and troubleshoot circuits in the laboratory sessions.

title: Lab: Biomedical Laboratory Techniques
description: Lab: Biomedical Laboratory Techniques

title: Structural Design I
description: Develop foundational skills related to structural analysis and design. Traditional structural analysis, concrete design, and steel design topics are blended in a project-based learning environment. Topics include analysis of indeterminate beams, application of industry-standard structural analysis software, design of steel tension members and columns, design of concrete beams, and introductory structural dynamics.

title: Lab: Fluid Mechanics
description: Lab: ENGR 345 Fluid Mehanics.

title: Transportation Engineering
description: Develop a working knowledge of highway and transportation engineering principles. Learn strategies for transportation planning, traffic signal timing, and geometric design of highways.

title: Fluid Mechanics
description: Study static and dynamic effects in fluid kinematics using Lagrangian and Eulerian analysis. Apply continuity, momentum, and energy equations in integral and differential form for incompressible flows. Introduction to boundary layer theory and transient fluid motion. Comparison of theory to experimental and computational fluid dynamic results. Internal flow and open channel flow are emphasized.

title: Environmental Engineering
description: Develop foundational skills related to water supply and wastewater treatment, sanitation, solid waste management, stormwater management, air pollution, human health risk assessment, hazardous waste remediation, and water quality. Integrate chemical, physical, and biological principles to prevent, treat, and assess environmental pollution. Understand the historical, regulatory, and public health context of environmental engineering. Laboratory experience emphasizes water chemistry, treatment processes, and chemical, physical, and biological assessment of water quality.

title: Lab: Environmental Engineering
description: Lab: Environmental Engineering.

title: Lab: Analog Electronics
description: Lab: ENGR 362 Analog Electronics.

title: Lab: Circuits II
description: Lab: ENGR 361 Circuits ll.

title: Analog Electronics
description: Explore the theory and practical applications of discrete semiconductor devices such as the design of circuits using diodes, Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs), and Field Effect Transistors (FETs), with a view toward extensions needed to create integrated analog and digital circuits.

title: Embedded Systems Design
description: Design embedded microcontrollers for practical applications. Learn microcontroller theory and architecture including standard internal functionality such as A/D converters and Serial Interfaces. Program microcontrollers using both assembly and C. Laboratories involve the design, construction, and programming of microcontroller-based projects.

title: Lab: Embedded Systems Design
description: Lab: ENGR 363 Embedded Systems Design.

title: Electromagnetics
description: Explore electromagnetic phenomena with application to contemporary engineering practice. Apply Maxwell's equations in static and dynamic field situations, including capacitive structures, solenoids, magnetic circuits, motors, generators, propagating waves and antennas. Use vector calculus and alternative coordinate systems to represent 3D quantities. Address effects of material properties. Special project allows hands-on experience with specific application.

title: Linear Systems
description: Analyze and design linear shift-invariant discrete-time systems for digital signal processing (DSP) and other applications. Describe single input-output systems using linear difference equations, block diagrams, and impulse-responses. Use Discrete Time Fourier Series methods to analyze discrete signals and examine system frequency response. MATLAB used extensively including the design of digital filters and applied projects in both the time and frequency domain.

title: Water Resources Engineering
description: Apply principles of surface water and ground water hydrology to the practice of water resources engineering. Emphasis is on calculation and management of stormwater runoff and the design of stormwater management systems. Hydraulic and hydrologic principles are also applied to the analysis and design of open channel systems, groundwater, pipe networks, and reservoirs.

title: Electrical Devices
description: Culmination of electronics course sequence with emphasis on operational amplifier circuits and field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs). Topics include practical applications of operational amplifiers, active filters, oscillators, FPGA hardware, FPGA system design using Verilog, and timing/clocking considerations.

title: Lab: Electrical Devices
description: Lab: ENGR 364 Electrical Devices.

title: Thermodynamics
description: Explore the science of energy and its application to engineered systems. Develop foundational knowledge related to thermodynamic properties and data, the first and second laws of thermodynamics, entropy and availability concepts, and control volume analyses. Apply those principles to thermodynamic systems and cycles for power generation, refrigeration, and heat pumping.

title: Instrumentation & Measurement
description: Study standard devices used for measuring mechanical quantities. Analyze experimental data obtained using mechanical and electrical instruments to determine the statistical relevance of measurements. Conduct laboratory experiments designed to reinforce measurement principles and techniques.

title: Circuits II
description: Extend foundational concepts for both Analog and Digital circuits begun in ENGR 215 . Analog circuit analysis topics include dependent sources, investigation of the natural and step responses of RL, RC, and RLC circuits, AC sinusoidal steady state circuit analysis using phasors, and power.  Digital topics include codes, advanced combinational digital logic both discrete and implemented with Verilog, minimization techniques, sequential logic, discrete and implemented with Verilog including design of counters, timers, registers, memory, finite state machines and Analog to Digital Converters.  FPGAs are used throughout the coverage of Digital topics.

title: Dynamics and Vibrations
description: Analyze kinematics and kinetics of particles and rigid bodies. Topics include translation, rotation, and relative motion; Newton’s Second Law; and work-energy and impulse-momentum applications. Introduction to forced and free vibration of single and multiple degree-of-freedom systems.

title: Lab: Instrumentation & Measurement
description: Lab: ENGR 373 Instrumentation and Measurement.

title: Fluid Dynamics
description: Study of static and dynamic effects in fluid kinematics using Lagrangian and Eulerian analysis. Continuity, momentum, and energy equations in integral and differential form for incompressible flows. Introduction to boundary layer theory and transient fluid motion. Comparison of theory to experimental and computational fluid dynamic results. Internal and external flow is emphasized

title: Manufacturing Processes
description: Study of manufacturing methods common in production. Consideration of tooling techniques, fabrication techniques, and the impact of those techniques on characteristics of parts produced, along with influence of design and materials on manufacturability. Lab experiences involving designing parts for ease of manufacturing and manufacturing the parts designed. Field trips to local industrial facilities arranged.

title: Engineering Project
description: Students engage the knowledge content of their academic discipline in the context of a specific technology problem or need and in collaboration with a partner organization or client community. Teams demonstrate effective project management strategies, report on progress consistently, and document significant design activity, and engage in engineering design reviews. Course offered in partnership with the Collaboratory for Strategic Partnerships and Applied Research. The sequence of these credits is typically 1-1-2-2 over the student’s last four semesters in the program of study.  Alternative sequencing must be approved by the Engineering Department Chair. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Engineering.

title: Lab: Manufacturing Processes
description: Lab: ENGR 378 Manufacturing Processes.

title: Lab: Robotic Systems
description: Lab: ENGR 421 Robotic Systems.

title: Biomedical Instrumentation
description: Use electrical and mechanical instruments to collect data from biological and physiological systems. Explore the theory of device operation and experiment with measurement principles and techniques. Analyze data to determine statistical relevance. Topics include biomedical sensors, property assessment of biomechanical materials, stimulation of excitable tissues, and biological and medical imaging techniques.

title: Lab: Industrial Automation
description: Lab: Industrial Automation

title: Lab: Design of Medical Devices
description: Lab: ENGR 432 Design of Medical Devices.

title: Lab:Biomedical Instrumentation
description: Lab: ENGR 431 Biomedical Instrumentation.

title: Industrial Automation
description: Learn to implement industrial automation technology commonly leveraged in manufacturing and assembly across a wide range of industries. Students will practice using programmable logic controllers (PLC) to interface with sensors and industrial displays and to control various actuation modalities. Machine vision technology will be explored, with students gaining experience in vision system setup and programming for part acceptance and defect detection. They will also demonstrate control of 3-axis gantries along with an understanding of the physical construction common to such systems. Students will specify, assemble, and control pneumatic actuation and valving systems and various conveyance strategies will be introduced. PLCs, machine vision, and pneumatic actuation will ultimately be implemented by students in the context of a part conveyance system, with all elements programmed to function synchronously for a desired operation. The class embeds concepts of lean manufacturing and systems engineering and includes a culminating project.

title: Structural Design II
description: Expand on topics from ENGR 341 to include design of steel members for flexure and concrete members in compression. Advanced topics include concepts for the design of steel plate girders, concrete discontinuity regions, and prestressed concrete. Timber design is introduced. An integration of traditional analysis and design topics is emphasized through a project-based learning approach.

title: Lab: Fluid Dynamics
description: Lab: ENGR 377 Fluid Dynamics.

title: Design of Medical Devices
description: Combine knowledge of physiology, engineering, and the current healthcare environment to design medical devices. Students will critically analyze and discuss scientific literature, develop and refine design concepts, perform market analyses, conduct risk management activities, navigate intellectual property and governmental regulations, and craft testing and development strategies.
