Culminates with a written design proposal and oral presentation.

title: Robotic Systems
description: Combine sensing, computing, and actuating systems to automate a task. Study mechanics of robotic manipulators, principles of mechatronic sensors and actuators, and applications of systems engineering and automation. Simulate and program a robotic manipulator to perform complex tasks. Construct various sensing and actuating systems. Complete a project to design and build a custom electromechanical system.

title: Geotechnical Engineering
description: Study the engineering principles relating to soil properties and apply them to geotechnical engineering solutions. Consider soil structures, index properties, soil classification, permeability, strength, compaction, consolidation, soil testing procedures, and soil stresses. Apply those concepts to design related to slope stability, retaining walls, and bearing capacity of shallow foundations.

title: Water and Wastewater Management
description: This course is a study of the physical, chemical, and biological principles and unit processes for the treatment of municipal drinking water and wastewater. It covers the design and operation of integrated treatment systems for water supply and for wastewater management.

title: Hazardous Waste and Air Pollution Management
description: The processes governing the transport and fate of contaminants in the environment and their treatment are emphasized. Applications include design of processes to handle, treat, and safely dispose hazardous waste. The course will cover principles and design practices to control both particulate and gaseous contaminants to meet air quality regulations.

title: Lab: Geotechnical Engineering
description: Lab: ENGR 443 Soil Mechanics and Foundation Design.

title: Lab: Structural Design II
description: Lab: ENGR 441 Structural Design ll.

title: Civil Engineering Design
description: Integrate ideas for various civil engineering sub-disciplines in a culminating design experience. Design projects will incorporate prior learning in structural, geotechnical, environmental, construction, transportation, and water resources engineering into a realistic context that emphasizes the role of each sub-discipline in a comprehensive project.

title: Lab: Power Electronics
description: Lab: ENGR 462 Power Electronics

title: Heat Transfer Analysis & Design
description: Explore fundamental principles of conduction, convection, and radiation heat transfer, and apply them to engineering problems. Study steady and transient conduction, forced and free convection, multi-mode heat transfer, heat exchanger design, and methods for enhancement of heat transfer. Laboratory experience includes application of theory and computer modelling through finite control volume and finite element methods.

title: Lab: Heat Transfer Analysis & Design
description: Lab: ENGR 471 Heat Transfer Analysis and Design.

title: Mechanical Design
description: Design of components for common machines and mechanical devices through application of engineering mechanics, materials engineering, and manufacturing concepts. Topics include combined stress, contact stress, stress concentrations, theories of failure, fatigue, deflection, elastic stability, impact, and corrosion and wear. Principles of good design are emphasized.  Laboratory experiences involve an introduction to common machine elements and computer modeling.

title: Environmental Techniques and Policy: Terrestrial
description: This course will provide project-driven experience in the application of landscape navigation and mapping techniques, data gathering and analysis methods for terrestrial systems. The student group operates as a consulting company with an outside client being the recipient of the course products.

title: Environmental Issues and Sustainable Solutions
description: Students engage interdisciplinary issues surrounding environmental sustainability. Examine society’s relationship with the ecological world and consider the Biblical response of stewardship to various environmental challenges. Study ecological challenges as a way of discovery and knowing. Examine ethics, economics and public policy as they relate to resource use, population growth, food production, pollution, biodiversity protection, energy consumption, and climate change. Discuss Judeo-Christian perspectives on environmental stewardship and consider ecosystems in our broader definition of the common good.

title: Lab: Mechanical Design
description: Lab: ENGR 472 Mechanical Design.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study in engineering under the direction of an instructor.

title: Lab: Environmental Techniques and Policy: Terrestrial
description: Lab: ENVS 270 Environmental Techniques and Policy: Terrestrial.

title: Communication Systems
description: Explore electronic communications systems with emphasis on digital and wireless techniques. Investigate the probablistic nature of information (entropy).  Compare coding and modulation techniques including AM, FM and PM types.  Account for signal energy, power, bandwidth as well as the impact of circuitry on signal-to-noise ratio and channel capacity.  Assess how the information rate depends on bit error rate, error detection and correction techniques.  Identify methods of information recovery. Use MATLAB as a tool for simulation.

title: Environmental Techniques and Policy: Water and Wetlands
description: This course will provide project-driven experience in the application of landscape mapping techniques, data gathering and analysis methods for wetland and aquatic systems. information will be applied to wetland permitting and watershed management. The student group operates as a consulting company with an outside client being the recipient of the course products.

title: Environmental Ethics
description: A seminar course contrasting Judeo-Christian with other religious and humanistic views of environmental ethics. Emphasis is on developing an individual ethic within a Biblical framework of Creation Care to address environmental issues such as pollution, resource use, and others related to human population. Course also addresses the connection of environmental stewardship with issues of justice in developing countries. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Biological Sciences.

title: Exploration of Off-Campus Study
description: This course is required for students pre-approved to participate in an international semester-long off-campus program through Messiah University who have not already completed levels of 202 Chinese or 206 Spanish, French, or German. This course is designed to equip students with the skills and tools needed to effectively engage and develop while living and learning cross-culturally. Chief among these skills and tools is the development of cultural intelligence and the ability to effectively communicate with and live in an environment other than Grantham, PA. These truly inter-disciplinary skills will prove invaluable in life and vocation beyond a student’s time at Messiah University.

title: Financial Management
description: An overview of the significant functions and roles performed by the corporate financial manager. Students will gain a working knowledge of managerial finance by learning to: develop a systematic approach to financial analysis; apply techniques for planning, managing, evaluating and recommending improvements in the organization's financial performance. Major topics include financial asset valuation, risk and return, and capital budgeting.

title: Environmental Techniques and Policy:Water and Wetlands
description: Lab: Environmental Techniques and Policy: Water and Wetlands.

title: Power Electronics
description: Study design and application of circuits and devices for electrical energy conversion including motor applications. Emphasis on DC-to-DC un-isolated Buck, Boost, hybrid or isolated Flyback, Forward, and other types versus DC-to-AC inverters.  Exploration of motors and their principles of operation with emphasis on the DC and Induction types to prepared students for practical work and more advanced study.  Labs allow students to measure the performance of common power electronics devices and motor types over a range of conditions illustrating their tradeoffs for various applications.

title: Estate Planning
description: Estate Planning is a required course of CFP® certification. This course covers the process of developing an estate plan, which includes taxation, property titling and beneficiary designations, strategies to transfer property, estate and incapacity planning documents, and postmortem estate planning techniques.

title: Investments
description: Deals with a thorough analysis of the theory and application of investments in relation to business cycles, institutional behavior and risk and return opportunities in the economic context. The course is presented from the investor's viewpoint and incorporates applied and empirical methodologies while covering investment strategies and policies. Major content areas include an introduction to the investment environment, modern portfolio theory, capital markets and instruments, security analysis, portfolio management, and derivatives.

title: Education and Retirement Planning
description: Education and Retirement Planning is a required course of CFP® certification. The course covers many items related to education and retirement planning including but not limited to: types of educational and retirement savings plans, educational and retirement needs analysis, Social Security and Medicare planning, and eldercare planning.

title: Special Topics in Finance
description: Study of selected topics in finance. Content dependent on faculty and student interest.

title: Corporate Finance
description: An advanced course in corporate finance. The core material will include working capital management, financial planning and forecasting, risk analysis in capital budgeting, capital structure theories, raising capital, and risk management. In addition, at least one current issue will be analyzed (e.g., corporate social responsibility).

title: Security Analysis and Evaluation
description: An in-depth study of modern security valuation techniques of equity and debt instruments both from a fundamental and technical perspective. Fundamental analysis will include the ability to create Excel spreadsheets to model and forecast key financial datapoints (free cash flow, net income, EPS, EBITDA, book value) and apply appropriate market multiples to formulate security value. Course will also encompass technical analysis related to the ability to decipher basic chart patterns, key areas of support/resistance and secondary indicators. Current equity research reports authored by leading Wall Street analysts will be used to supplement the course material.

title: Financial Planning Capstone
description: Financial Planning Capstone is a required course of CFP® certification. This capstone course brings academic coursework and actual practice management together as students are provided the skills and knowledge needed to develop a comprehensive financial plan for a client.

title: Insurance and Risk Management
description: Insurance and Risk Management is a required course of CFP® certification. This course covers fundamental insurance principles and the role insurance plays in financial and estate planning.  It also provides tools to for students to learn how to help clients analyze and evaluate their risk exposure.

title: Advanced Corporate Finance
description: An intense study focused on training students to think critically and objectively in their evaluation of key financial issues facing modern corporations. Emphasis throughout the course will be placed on financial modeling and forecasting techniques using Microsoft Excel to develop integrated projections of financial statements and discounted cash flow (DCF) valuation models. Students will be exposed to both theory and practice underlying complex financial topics including mergers & acquisitions, capital structure optimization, derivative valuation, leveraged buyouts (LBOs), share repurchase economics and enterprise risk management.

title: Securities Industry Brokerage Preparation
description: This course is taught specifically for students who intend to pursue a career in the financial services industry and will likely need to understand the content of and/or pass the Securities Industry Essentials (SIE) Exam. Financial planners/consultants, advisors, stock brokers, insurance agents, trust officers and investment bankers are among those who are required to pass the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) SIE exam. Areas of focus include analysis of the securities markets, customer investment suitability, option strategies, investment products and proper account opening procedures.

title: French Culture and Language
description: Study of the culture, geography, and history of France and the French-speaking world.

title: Fundamentals of French I
description: This beginner-level French course develops students’ basic French proficiency at the novice level. This course will develop students’ knowledge and ability to communicate orally and in writing in French at the novice level; identify and produce grammatical structures and vocabulary at the novice level; demonstrate knowledge of the francophone culture; and articulate connections between the Christian faith and language study.

title: Money and Financial Markets
description: The role of finance in the economy and the nature of money and other financial assets. Emphasizes the role of principal financial markets and their relationship to the economy through study of fund flows, monetary theory, and policy.

title: Fundamentals of French II
description: Building on FREN 101 , this French course continues to advance students’ French proficiency at the novice level. This course will strengthen students’ knowledge and ability to communicate orally and in writing in French at the novice level; identify and produce grammatical structures and vocabulary at the novice level; demonstrate knowledge of the francophone culture; and articulate connections between the Christian faith and language study.

title: Intermediate French
description: Building on FREN 102 , this French course begins to advance students’ French proficiency at the intermediate level. This course will strengthen students’ knowledge and ability to communicate orally and in writing at the intermediate level in French; identify and produce grammatical structures and vocabulary at the intermediate level in French; articulate knowledge of the francophone cultures; and articulate connections between the Christian faith and language study.

title: Contemporary French Culture
description: Topics include the French concepts of family, home, friendships, etc.

title: Advanced Composition and Conversation I
description: Conversation and composition on a variety of contemporary topics.

title: Selected Topics in French
description: Intensive study of a selected author, genre, literary movement, French-speaking country, or current events derived from periodicals and/or the internet.

title: Advanced Composition and Conversation II
description: Conversation and composition on a variety of contemporary topics.

title: Foundations of Geology
description: This course will familiarize you with the structure and composition of the Earth, and the dynamic physical processes that continually mold its surface. It will give you an awareness of the interplay of diverse processes that regulate Earth systems. Such knowledge is critical for the intelligent evaluation of many complex scientific, technological, and environmental issues that face our society.

title: Financial Institution Management
description: A rigorous examination of financial institutions (banks, credit unions, shadow banking system) and their vital role in supporting a fully functioning economy by maintaining ready access to funding, meeting creditor obligations and serving as credit intermediaries. Provides a comprehensive review of current risk management techniques as well as the regulatory regime in which financial institutions operate (e.g., stress testing, resolution planning, minimum capital requirements, liquidity standards).

title: Fundamentals of German I
description: This beginner-level German course develops students’ basic German proficiency at the novice level. This course will develop students’ knowledge and ability to communicate orally and in writing in German at the novice level; identify and produce grammatical structures and vocabulary at the novice level; demonstrate knowledge of the German culture and cultural competence; and explain how the Christian faith connects with language study.

title: Lab: Foundations of Geology
description: Lab: GEOL 201 Foundations of Geology.

title: Psychology of Aging
description: Study focuses on the psychological stability and change experienced by older persons. Topics include sensation and perception, intellectual functioning and learning, psychomotor skills, personality, and psychopathology as related to older persons. Students develop a relationship with an Elder Mentor for the duration of the class.

title: World Regional Geography
description: Students examine the world through the lens of geography’s impact on culture, language, religion, politics, gender, migration, economic and urban development, as well as international relations. Geography has a profound impact on humanity and its institutions, and this non-western human geography course employs the use of history and historical understanding to enrich student engagement with and analysis of the impacts of phenomena such as globalization, colonialism, and human-environment interactions on place, regions, and cultural landscapes. This course incorporates the Pennsylvania Academic Standards for Geography and Geography for Life: National Geography Standards to support engagement and learning.

title: Fundamentals of German II
description: Building on GERM 101 , this German course continues to advance students’ German proficiency at the novice level. This course will strengthen students’ knowledge and ability to communicate orally and in writing in German at the novice level; identify and produce grammatical structures and vocabulary at the novice level; demonstrate knowledge of the German culture; and articulate connections between the Christian faith and language study.

title: Biology, Nutrition, and Healthy Aging
description: This course is devoted to the biological, nutritional and health aspects of the aging process. Emphasis is given to maximizing healthy aging.

title: Intermediate German
description: Building on GERM 102 , this German course begins to advance students’ German proficiency at the intermediate level. This course will strengthen students’ knowledge and ability to communicate orally and in writing at the intermediate level in German; identify and produce grammatical structures and vocabulary at the intermediate level in German; articulate knowledge of the culture of the German world, and articulate connections between the Christian faith and language study.

title: Aging Practicum
description: Field experience for persons pursuing possible careers in the field of gerontology. Students work under professional supervision for a minimum of 40 hours per credit. Ten additional hours of reading and other integrative activities per credit required.

title: Academic Strategies
description: Assists in making a smooth and successful transition in all classes; provides growth experiences both in the educational and personal realms; introduces a variety  of a college survival skills (note taking, test taking, etc.) as well as personal survival skills (goal setting, time management, self-awareness, effective communication skills, etc.). The emphasis is on exploring how to learn more efficiently and effectively and on developing and practicing skills necessary to be academically successful.

title: Independent Study
description: Advanced language study or research. May not be taken for more than six credits.

title: Introduction to Geographic Information Systems
description: A Geographical Information System (GIS) is a cross-disciplinary computer-based data processing program designed to manage and analyze spatial information. The emphasis of this course is on software training using ArcGIS Desktop, a PC-based software package used for visualizing, exploring, querying, editing and analyzing data linked to geographic locations. Students will develop a core base and general skills on which they can build further expertise in ArcGIS Desktop. Expectations: Basic skills in MS Windows, spreadsheets, and databases. Students from all majors are welcome.

title: Adjusting to Life in the United States
description: The purpose of this course is to assist international, missionary and third-culture students with their unique adjustment to Messiah University. The course will provide a well-rounded, but general knowledge base about the U.S. educational system and broader U.S. culture, with special attention given to the skills necessary for academic success at Messiah University. Through dialogue, guest speakers, readings and individual reflection, the course will seek to foster an awareness of the diversity in U.S. society while assisting students through their own unique adjustment.

title: Principles of Global Health and Development
description: This course introduces the foundational principles of global health and development, emphasizing the intersection of health, economics, and sustainable growth in diverse contexts. Students will explore the social determinants of health, the impact of culture and religion, the global burden of disease, and strategies to addressing health disparities. Key global development theories are examined with a focus on the indices for human development and economic policies that promote global progress.

title: Lab: Intro to Geographic Information Systems
description: Lab: GIS 245 Introduction to Geographc Information Systems.

title: Advanced Studies in Global Health and Development
description: This course will serve as a culminating experience for the certificate. Students will develop a deep understanding of how Biblical principles—such as power, justice, and shalom—inform individual and institutional responses to social transformation. Through reflective engagement, students will cultivate faith-based approaches to addressing global and local health challenges, including poverty and health equity, integrating ethical and sustainable solutions into their professional and community endeavors.

title: International Community Research
description: Emphasizing a "place-based education" approach, students engage directly with diverse communities to assess health inequities, development initiatives, and the structural factors driving economic stability and growth. Through experiential learning, they explore the complex intersections of global health challenges with local traditions, governance structures, and economic conditions. In the first half of the course, students further develop their underst a nding of global health strategies and their implementation in community-based settings. In the latter half, they apply this knowledge by completing a Community Profile in an international setting with a special emphasis on water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH).

title: Fundamentals of Greek II
description: This course introduces elements of Greek, which are studied and applied with a goal of mastering the rudiments of the language and acquiring a carefully selected vocabulary. It lays a foundation for students to continue reading and studying the Greek New Testament and related literature.

title: Fundamentals of Greek I
description: This course introduces elements of Greek, which are studied and applied with a goal of mastering the rudiments of the language and acquiring a carefully selected vocabulary. It lays a foundation for students to continue reading and studying the Greek New Testament and related literature.

title: Sociology of Aging
description: This course provides a introduction to the field of social gerontology. Special emphasis is given to the aged in context: how older adults both affect and are affected by society. Topics include demography, cultural values, diversity of aging experiences, friendship and family relationships, work and retirement, living arrangements, political activity and social programs. Students engage in the Elder Service Partner Program for the duration of the semester.

title: Intermediate Greek
description: This course equips students to handle complex aspects of Greek syntax, vocabulary, and grammar, while developing efficiency in translation and strategies for interpretation. Students translate and analyze passages from the New Testament and Apocrypha with attention to the historical and cultural contexts in which they were written.

title: Opportunities in Human Development and Family Science
description: The course is an introduction to the fields of human development and family science and family and consumer sciences education, internship opportunities, professional organizations, and career possibilities following graduation. Emphasis is given to helping students maximize their college experience.

title: Foundations of Marriage & Family
description: Healthy families are critical to personal and social vitality. This course provides foundational knowledge about family dynamics, guided by Christian, multicultural, historical, contemporary, and strength-based perspectives. The course provides an overview of the family science discipline including topics such as: family theories, marriage and parenting, individual and family development, gender and sexuality, family communication and stress, and family traditions and folklore. The course equips those beginning the Human Development and Family Science major as well as those wanting to gain a more robust understanding of families, including one's own.

title: Principles of Peer Relationships
description: This course, designed for Resident Assistants and Apartment Life Coordinators, will consider numerous essential components for effective peer relationships and helping skills based upon the premise that competent students know themselves, know others, and know Messiah University.

title: Introduction to Interpersonal Relations
description: All aspects of life are touched by relationships in one way or another. This course looks at intrapersonal and interpersonal needs, goals, and behaviors in an effort to enhance interpersonal competence, with an emphasis on skill development necessary to function more effectively in various types of interpersonal roles and contexts. Whether private or professional, recreational or romantic, students will gain a deeper understanding of relational dynamics they may experience on a daily basis while looking at the topic through Christian, secular, and multicultural lenses.

title: Child Development
description: This class examines the individual from conception through late childhood. Attention is given to the physical, social-emotional, linguistic and cognitive development of the child as well as theoretical and methodological issues related to child development. Students should gain observation and application skills.

title: Human Sexuality
description: This course examines sexual development, human reproduction, sexual function and dysfunction, gender, ethics, attitudes and sexual expression within relationships. It investigates psycho-social issues as well as their impact on family relationships and their intersection with Christian faith.

title: Death, Grief and Caring
description: This course critically examines the psychological, emotional and social responses to death, dying, loss and grief across the life span. Attention will be given to personal, multicultural, current, and historical approaches, beliefs, and responses to death, dying and grief. Students should develop skills and competencies necessary to help bereaved individuals and families.

title: Family Resource Management
description: An examination of management basics such as decision making, time and energy management, work simplification, and critical thinking as they apply to the functioning of family systems through the life span. The student will acquire a thorough understanding of the acquisition, distribution, and utilization of resources as these relate to unique individual/family needs and wants.

title: Domestic Community Research
description: Emphasizing a "place-based education" approach, students engage directly with diverse communities to assess health inequities, development initiatives, and the structural factors driving economic stability and growth. Through experiential learning, they explore the complex intersections of globa l health challenges with local traditions, governance structures, and economic conditions. In the first half of the course, students further develop their understanding of global health strategies and their implementation in community-based s ettings. In the latter half, they apply this knowledge by completing a Community Profile in a US setting with a special emphasis on water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH).

title: Textile Arts
description: Students will be introduced to and practice basic hand and machine sewing techniques, including the use of patterns, creating a variety of projects suitable for those teaching in a Family and Consumer Sciences classroom.

title: Family & Consumer Science Curriculum & Instruction I
description: Provides an examination of the spectrum of theories, techniques, methods, and approaches to the teaching of family and consumer sciences, with particular attention to the Pennsylvania Department of Education's Academic Standards and Guidelines for FCS. Students will be expected to develop instructional materials that could be used in classroom.

title: Community Services for Individuals and Families
description: The course introduces students to different types of community-based services in low income communities. Special attention will be given to the study of poverty, community development and the analysis of government policies on families.

title: Research Methods and Analysis in HDFS
description: This course introduces students to quantitative and qualitative research methods used in the field of Human Development and Family Science. Through the development of research projects students will gain skills in the area of research design, survey construction, data analysis and development of a poster presentation. Special attention will be given to the use of SPSS in analyzing quantitative data and thematic coding in analyzing qualitative data.

title: Parenting
description: This class examines parenting processes, styles, techniques and theories throughout the family life cycle. Attention is given to parenting within diverse cultural and family structural contexts. Students will explore existing parent education programs and experience creating their own parenting lessons. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Human Development & Family Science.

title: Adult Development
description: This course provides a development-focused approach to the understanding of early and middle adult years, in particular, the interaction of physiological, cognitive, psychological, and sociological aspects in the study of adult development. Students will utilize qualitative research methods conducting structured interviews and collecting, analyzing and presenting narrative data.

title: Adolescent Development
description: This class offers a development-focused approach to understanding adolescents. Attention is given to the physical, emotional, social, and cognitive development of adolescents as well as theoretical and methodological issues as related to the study of adolescents. Applications are made to family, peers, schools, church, and culture.

title: Dynamics of Family Interaction
description: This course provides students with the theoretical lenses needed by professionals within the field of family science to analyze interpersonal processes and interaction patterns in families and other intimate groups. Students will also utilize a tool for family assessment by constructing family genograms. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Human Development & Family Science.

title: Child Life Field Experience
description: This course offers students practical experience working with hospitalized children and exposes them to the role of the Child Life Specialist in a healthcare setting. This field experience runs concurrent with course work for HDFS 361 . Students spend time weekly working with hospitalized children for a total of at least forty hours over the course of the semester.

title: Marital and Couple Relationships
description: Healthy couple relationships provide a solid foundation for individual and family development, and healthy marriages reflect Christ’s relationship with the Church.  In addition to examining historical trends, this course will take a topical approach to understanding developmental processes and dynamics of marriage and couple relationships at various points across the life cycle.  Topics addressed include couple formation and maintenance; cohabitation; attachment-based love; rituals and traditions; theology of marriage; gender roles; communication and conflict; intimate partner violence; separation and divorce; marital strengths; and relationship stability and longevity.  Students will gain deeper understanding of marital and couple relationships; interact with couples at various stages of marriage; critically examine their own views; and learn ways to approach relationship education. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Human Development & Family Science.

title: Latino Families
description: Students examine the cultural values and specific patterns of family interaction found in the most predominate Latino groups in the United States. Study immigration and acculturation with particular attention to how historical, political, and economic factors have influenced the adjustment of these families into mainstream American culture. Investigate current practices in the delivery of social services to these families and consider effective ways to serve existing needs in this community.

title: Interior Design
description: This course will examine the fundamentals of interior design, recognizing the aesthetic quality of space and human built environments.

title: Introduction to Child Life
description: This course will offer a basic introduction to the field of child life, the role of the child life specialist in healthcare, theory, history, scope of professional practices, overview of Child Life Council and the certification process. The fundamental skills of a child life specialist (assessment, medical play, and psychological preparation, provision of coping strategies, sibling work, disease education and end of life support) will be discussed.

title: Family Ethnicity & Human Services
description: Students discuss the main cultural values, life cycle transitions and patterns of family interaction found among the most predominate American ethnic/racial minority groups in the United States. Examine issues of racism, acculturation, and ethnic identity development, as well as the role that cultural sensitivity and competency plays in the assessment and delivery of human services.

title: Family Service Practicum
description: Field experience for junior and senior students wishing to clarify vocational goals and gain hands-on experience. Students work under professional supervision for a minimum of 40 hours per credit. Ten additional hours of reading and other integrative activities per credit required.

title: Marriage & Family Therapy
description: The field of marriage and family therapy (MFT) is a thriving profession meant to develop competent practitioners with knowledge of individual, couple and family dynamics. This course introduces students to systems theory and foundational concepts of family therapy. The course examines the historical context of current therapy-related issues as well as emergent trends in family therapy, from a Christian, integrative perspective. Students considering MFT or counseling as a possible career path will gain valuable knowledge of various pathways into the profession.

title: Topics in Human Development and Family Science
description: Selected topics in family science not currently included in course offerings. Possible topics may include families under stress, play, the history of the family, the family in later life, family violence, work and families, the family and media, women in relationships, family therapy, and family diversity. May be repeated for additional credit as the topic/study changes.

title: Play and Development
description: Play and leisure are important activities in facilitating healthy human development. This class explores the social, emotional, cognitive, and physical benefits of play and leisure. Emphasis is given to the theoretical understanding of play and leisure, as well as the therapeutic uses of play. Careers that rely heavily on the use of play will be explored.

title: Infants, Toddlers & Families
description: This course examines the role of the family and other caregivers in the development of infants and toddlers (ages 0-3). Early intervention and assessment techniques will be explored along with developmentally appropriate practices. Students will become familiar with the Individualized Family Service Plan (IFSP), and other resources and services that may be available to infants and toddlers and their families.

title: Human Development & Family Science Seminar
description: In this capstone course students will examine their readiness for professional pursuits in light of their preparation in Human Development and Family Sciences or Family and Consumer Sciences, as well as their personal Christian faith journey. Students will have the opportunity to assess their own personal growth, knowledge, and preparedness for careers serving others in various capacities.

title: Early Childhood Administration and Practice
description: This course offers students a basic introduction to early childhood care, positive guidance, curriculum and administration.  Students will learn evidence-based practices, developmentally appropriate curriculum, and basic guidelines for following government standards and regulations.

title: Independent Study
description: Junior and senior students with at least a 3.0 GPA in their major may select a problem for independent investigation in a special area of family science.

title: Early Childhood Administration and Practice Experience
description: This course offers students practical experience working with preschool-age children in childcare or preschool settings.  Students will implement developmentally appropriate early childhood curriculum.  Students will spend time weekly working with children at private or high school-based early learning centers for a total of at least forty hours over the course of the semester. Students must have a TB test within the past year and provide proof of Child Abuse Clearances.

title: Major Honors I
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Senior Thesis
description: The mission of Ethnic & Area Studies is to graduate students with a multidisciplinary understanding of culture and in-depth knowledge of at least one culture or cultural/racial/ethnic tradition who can apply this knowledge in church and society to become effective agents of social justice and reconciliation. This capstone course will include independent research on a topic chosen by the student in consultation with the faculty supervisor, and will thoroughly engage a primary area of interest in at least one culture.

title: Fundamentals of Hebrew II
description: This course advances students’ ability to read and translate biblical Hebrew proficiently. Emphasis is placed on mastering the essential verb paradigms in the Qal stem and being able to recognize the distinctive markers of derived stems. Students expand their knowledge of frequently recurring Hebrew words and learn introductory matters of Hebrew syntax. Select portions of the Hebrew Bible are translated.

title: Fundamentals of Hebrew I
description: This course equips students with the skills needed to begin reading and translating biblical Hebrew. It introduces students to basic terminology used in the study of biblical Hebrew and emphasizes the memorization of frequently recurring Hebrew words. Students learn the basic phonemes and morphemes of biblical Hebrew, and they obtain a basic understanding of Hebrew grammar. Select portions of the Hebrew Bible are translated.

title: Intermediate Hebrew
description: Students taking Intermediate Hebrew will already have completed HEBR 205 and HEBR 206 and should possess a working knowledge of the basics of Hebrew grammar. This primary focus of this course is to equip students to read and translate passages from the Hebrew Bible with greater accuracy and efficiency. Students learn advanced Hebrew syntax and demonstrate mastery of Hebrew words occurring fifty times or more in the Hebrew Bible.

title: Ancient Mediterranean World
description: The civilizations of the ancient Mediterranean world have profoundly influenced the ideas, languages, cultures, and institutions of western societies. Students explore the historical development of these diverse civilizations from the Bronze Age to Late Antiquity and learn to analyze ancient sources to interpret the past. Topics include Bronze Age civilizations, Greek cities, Athenian democracy, Alexander the Great, the Roman Empire, early Christianity, and the diversity and interactions of ancient civilizations.

title: Seminar: Modern European History Survey
description: An introductory survey of modern European history. Themes include social, economic, political, religious, and cultural developments over time and space. The course will explore topics such as the Renaissance and Reformation, the Industrial Revolution, the modern nation state, colonialism and empire, global conflicts of the twentieth century, and the various interactions of modern populations.

title: Strategies of Family Life Education
description: The purpose of this capstone course for the HDFS major is to investigate models and methods for strengthening interpersonal and family relationships utilizing primary prevention strategies such as family life education and family enrichment. Students will conduct a needs assessment utilizing current research and develop a family life education curriculum.

title: Seminar: Ancient Mediterranean World
description: An introductory survey of Mediterranean history from the Bronze Age to the end of antiquity. Themes include social, economic, political, religious, and cultural developments over time and space. The course explores topics such as Bronze Age civilizations, the Greek city state, Athenian democracy, Platonic philosophy, Alexander the Great, the Hellenistic age, Roman political culture, the growth of Christianity, and the diversity and interactions of ancient populations.

title: Modern European History Survey
description: An introductory survey of modern European history. Themes include social, economic, political, religious, and cultural developments over time and space. The course will explore topics such as the Renaissance and Reformation, the Industrial Revolution, the modern nation state, colonialism and empire, global conflicts of the twentieth century, and the various interactions of modern populations.

title: Seminar: United States History Survey to 1865
United States History Survey to 1865
description: A study of the political, economic, social, cultural, and religious developments of the entity that became the United States from early European settlement in the sixteenth century to the end of the Civil War in 1865. Students will examine the diversity of the people and their interaction with the movements that have shaped early American society to form an understanding of this past. Topics include European exploration and colonization, colonial British America, American Revolution, the early American republic, slavery in America, and the American Civil War.

title: Seminar: United States History Survey Since 1865
description: Seminar: Begins with Reconstruction and continues through the present day.

title: United States History Survey Since 1865
description: A study of the political, economic, social, cultural, and religious developments of the United States from the end of the Civil War in 1865 to the early years of the 21st century. Students will identify and analyze the various social and cultural factors that have shaped the daily experiences of people living in the U.S., including debates about justice, equality, and citizenship. Topics include Reconstruction, Progressivism, the era of Jim Crow, Women’s Suffrage, the Great Depression, social movements, and shifting American politics.

title: Historical Methods
description: This course serves as an introduction to the discipline and methods of history. Students will be introduced to the process of historical research and writing using primary and secondary sources. They will identify historical problems, undertake research by locating and analyzing sources, and develop critical reading, writing, and citation skills to synthesize information and construct arguments. The course will also foster interdisciplinary engagement, discussions, and public presentations. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of History, Politics & International Relations.

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Ancient Roman World
description: The Ancient Roman world has profoundly influenced the political, social, and cultural ideals and values of western societies. Students explore the diversity and historical developments of the Romans from a small city-state to the end of antiquity and learn to analyze ancient sources to interpret the past. Topics include the origins of the city, Republican forms of government, imperialism, the civil wars, the Roman Empire, daily life, and the emergence of Christianity.

title: Early Christian World
description: The early Christian world has profoundly influenced the religious, social, and cultural ideals and values of modern societies. Students explore the diversity and historical developments of Christianity within the contexts of the Roman empire and learn to analyze ancient sources to interpret the past. Topics include Jewish backgrounds and Christian origins, the growth of the church, Roman persecution and assimilation, the conversion of the emperor Constantine, religious pluralism and violence, and the nature of Christianization between the first and seventh centuries.

title: Effective Historical Writing
description: This course provides an opportunity to master the syntactical skills and rhetorical techniques of effective historical writing in both narrative and analytical modes. Students will study these skills and techniques with faculty mentoring, and then workshop and peer review drafts of their papers (assigned in the companion HIST 202 Historical Methods course) in preparation for final submission. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of History, Politics & International Relations.

title: Ancient Greek World
description: The Ancient Greek world has profoundly influenced the political, social, and cultural ideals and values of western societies. Students explore the diversity and historical developments of the Greeks from prehistoric times to the Hellenistic age and learn to analyze ancient sources to interpret the past. Topics include the Trojan War and Bronze Age Civilizations, the emergence of the city state, Athenian democracy, the Peloponnesian war, and classical philosophy, drama, mythology, art, and architecture.

title: Knights, Peasants, and Bandits
description: A study of how ordinary (and some not-so-ordinary) people coped with both daily life as well as major historical events in England from the Norman Conquest to the Tudor dynasty. Special emphasis is placed on life within the communities of family, village, court, church, and town. Learning outcomes include developing fluency in the methods of historical inquiry through analysis of material and textual artifacts; discovering the diversity of people, ideas, culture, society, material conditions, and religious practice in medieval England; communicating one’s own discoveries and ideas effectively to others; and discerning intersections between Christian faith and the discipline of history.

title: Medieval Europe
description: A study of the cultural, social, economic, religious, and political developments in Europe from the tenth to the fifteenth centuries. Major themes include the emergence of distinctively European institutions and modes of thought, Christian spirituality, and cultural interactions with Jewish, Byzantine, and Islamic communities. Learning outcomes include developing fluency in the methods of historical inquiry through analysis of material and textual artifacts; discovering the wide diversity of peoples, ideas, cultures, societies, material conditions, and religious practice in premodern Europe; communicating one’s own discoveries and ideas effectively to others, and discerning intersections between Christian faith and the discipline of history.

title: Civil War America
description: An examination of the causes, nature, and consequences of the American Civil War. Covers the period from 1848-1877 and discusses such topics as the nature of slavery, the rise of abolitionism, the collapse and reconstruction of the American political system, and the realities of war.

title: War, Peace, Memory in America
description: This course offers a study of how the experience of war has shaped Americans' identities and definitions of citizenship. Themes include the incorporation of women into the military, gender roles, propaganda, PTS(D) and its precursors, veteran studies, and social movements for peace. Readings provide an investigation into how wars that Americans have fought are remembered in the collective memory, whether through national narratives, in the media, or in popular culture. These address whose histories are remembered and forgotten, the ways myths of war are created for political purpose, definitions of heroism, and how memories of war shift over time. Writing in the major requirement for the Peace and Conflict Studies major.

title: World History since 1500 CE
description: Students examine the varied, complex, and rich histories of the human experience across the globe since 1500 CE. Explore the intercultural history of the planet that produced new patterns of order and interaction that have generated the transformative motors of world history, including the growth of capitalism, European Imperial formations, the cultures of colonialism, modernity and Globalization, and the condition of postmodernity. Students engage content from the Global South in ways that enhance intercultural literacy, self-awareness, and the possibilities for reconciliation in the midst of planetary wear and tear.

title: America & World War II
description: This is a history of American experiences during the war and in the immediate postwar period, including an examination of the home front, European and Pacific theaters. Themes include propaganda, PTS(D) and its precursors, veteran studies, trauma, the integration of various groups into the U.S. military, gender roles, and peace movements. We will explore the collective memory of the war through national narratives, the media, and popular culture. Questions of whose histories are remembered and forgotten, definitions of heroism, and the ways myths of war are created for specific purposes will be discussed.

title: World History to 1500 CE
description: Students examine the varied, complex, and rich histories of the human experience across the globe before 1500 CE. Explore the intercultural history of the planet when new patterns of order and interaction emerged to connect the world in unprecedented ways, including the growth of political complexity from chiefdoms to imperial formations, the emergence of sophisticated worldviews, nomadic, and Islamic cultures, “feudalism,” forms of premodern globalization, and subaltern histories. Students engage the complexities of the premodern history of the Global South in ways that enhance intercultural literacy and self-awareness.

title: Introduction to History
description: This course will explore the ways in which human communities (including historians) think about the past, both as a way of thinking and living in the world and also as a disciplined practice. Students will consider the relationship between Christian faith and the study of history, careers in history, and how to begin thinking about putting a history degree to use in the world. While this course is required of all first-year history majors, anyone interested in an introduction to history is welcome.

title: History of Asia since 1500 CE
description: Students examine the varied, complex, and rich histories of Asia since 1500 CE that produced new patterns of order and interaction as the continent became increasingly drawn into intercultural engagements with the Global North, including the gradual demise of traditional state formations, the rise of European Imperialism, the emergence of national and subaltern movements, the growth of capitalism, modernity and Globalization, and the condition of “postmodernity.” Students engage content from the Global South in ways that enhance their intercultural literacy, self-awareness, and the possibilities for reconciliation in the midst of ongoing planetary wear and tear.

title: History of Asia to 1500 CE
description: Students examine the varied, complex, and rich histories of the human experience across Asia before 1500 CE. Discover the new patterns of order and interaction that characterized the intercultural history of the continent, including the growth of political complexity from minor kingdoms to imperial formations, the emergence of sophisticated worldviews, nomadic, and Islamic cultures, debates around “feudalism,” forms of premodern globalization, and histories of the marginalized. Students engage the Global South in ways that enhance intercultural literacy and self-awareness without losing sight of the complexities at play in premodern Asia.

title: Digital History
description: What does it mean to practice history in the digital age? In this course, we explore how technology is changing the way we think about, research, and present the past. Our emphasis will be on the practice of digital history through specific exercises in GIS, data collection and manipulation, internet archiving, database creation, website development, social media, image and video editing, and digitization. Through a range of applications, tools, and collaborative exercises, we will see how digital tools readily intersect with the practice of history and how these applications are changing the way we understand our discipline.

title: The Trial of Joan of Arc
description: This course provides a deep study the extraordinary person, career, and trial of Joan of Arc. Her heresy trial in particular provides an important window into contemporary attitudes towards not only politics and authority but also religion and gender. Because the course is a reading seminar students will also be afforded ample opportunities to explore the methods of scholarly historical inquiry, with emphasis on the methods by which historical knowledge is produced, and then to assess the strengths and limitations of such attempts to reconstruct and comprehend peoples and cultures from the deep past, both historically as well as historiographically.

title: Archaeology and Historical Interpretation
description: An introduction to the methods of archaeology as they relate to and produce historical analysis, interpretation, and conclusions.

title: Age of Hamilton
description: An examination of the early American republic (1787-1815) through the life of Alexander Hamilton, the first Secretary of the United States Treasury. Topics covered include Hamilton’s childhood in the West Indies, his participation in the American Revolution, his personal and family life, his economic and political philosophy, his role as a leader of the Federalist Party, and his famous duel with Aaron Burr.

title: Topics in Modern European History
description: Selected topics related to a specific area of Modern European history.

title: Late Antiquity
description: A study of the transformation of the Roman world from the third to seventh-centuries AD, examining the end of the ancient world and the birth of new medieval societies in Europe and the Mediterranean. The course gives attention to such topics as the Christianization of the Roman Empire, shifting boundaries of empire and political structures, changes in society and economy, transformation of town and countryside, Germanic migrations, the rise of the papacy, and the emergence of Islam.

title: Tudor-Stuart England:1400-1700
description: An introduction to the history of England from the Later Middle Ages through the Tudor/Stuart era. Major themes include social, economic, and religious change and the ways in which those changes influenced politics and culture from the Wars of the Roses through the Elizabethan Age and the English Civil War.

title: European Imperialism and its Legacies: 1500 to Present
description: This course focuses on one of the most crucial facets of modern European history-the interaction of European peoples with the rest of the world after Columbus 'discovered' the Americas in 1492. This course examines European imperialism in the Americas after 1492 as well as the 'New Imperialism' of the late 19th century, which occurred in a very different historical context. It considers the two-way significance of European imperialism-its impact on the Americas, Africa, and Asia, and on Europe itself.

title: Colonial America
description: A study of the political, social, and religious history of the North American colonies from 1620 to 1763. Emphases include the transformation of European and African settlers into a distinctly American people, and the often stormy relationships between Native Americans and European immigrants.

title: America in the Age of Revolution
description: An examination of the development of the United States between 1790 and 1848. Emphasis is given to the political, social, economic, and cultural factors which shaped American life during the Jeffersonian and Jacksonian eras.

title: Modern America: U.S. History, 1945-Present
description: A study of the U.S. from the end of World War II to the present. Special attention given to the social and cultural influences of the Cold War, changes in the political economy, and protest movements in the late 20th Century.

title: African-American History Since 1865
description: Explore the experiences of African Americans from the abolition of slavery to the present. See both the rich and varied experiences of Black people and the ways in which they played important roles in the history of the nation. Consider both the challenges that Black Americans posed to the political and social order and the value of Black culture for its own sake. Examine the monumental civil rights movement of the mid-20th century and enter into the debate: Where do we go from here?

title: U.S. Urban History
description: An examination of the process by which America moved from a society characterized by small farms and villages to one dominated by large cities and sprawling suburbs. Themes include the effects of technology and planning on city-building and the effects of the urban form on race, ethnic, and gender relations.

title: History of Premodern S Asia
description: This course looks at the broad outlines of the history of the Indian subcontinent prior to 1500. Through a combination of lecture, film and discussion students will explore the development of the Indus Valley cultures, the Vedic age, Hindu, Buddhistic, and Jaina worldviews, early kingdoms and empires, medieval state formations, Islamicate culture and Sultanic regimes which reveal the multilayered and complex character of the subcontinentâs history, one that goes beyond simple labels of "Hindu" and "Muslim."

title: Topics in American History
description: Selected topics related to a specific area of American history.

title: Topics in World History
description: Selected topics related to a specific area of World history.

title: Teaching History and Social Studies
description: A seminar emphasizing disciplinary content issues that are specific to secondary school history and social studies teaching. Areas of focus include curricular and instructional decision-making processes; classroom management strategies, assessment techniques, adaptations for exceptional learners; instructional technology applications; historical thinking, and professional development. This course is required for History-Social Studies majors, but is also open to other students as an upper-division history elective.

title: Topics in European History
description: Selected Topics related to a specific area of European History.

title: History of South Asia After 1500
description: This course examines the broad contours of the history of the Indian sub-continent after 1500 especially those parts covered by India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Nepal. Major themes include: the Mughal Empire, European colonial interventions and indigenous responses (reform, rebellion, and nationalism), and the painful emergence of south Asian nations and their postcolonial predicaments. These themes will also intersect with the following concerns: Mughal state making, colonial governance and its forms of knowledge, subaltern histories, gender and caste studies, communalism, and discourses on development.

title: Women & Gender in Amer. History
description: Students will examine women and gender in American history, focusing on a comparative perspective of Americans’ experiences and constructions of gender. Organized chronologically and thematically, we will explore themes of power, identity, citizenship, race, and human rights. Students will identify the significance of gender in state, family, religious, and economic institutions, as people developed a sense of consciousness of their rights and negotiated for them throughout different moments in history.

title: Public History
description: A study of how history is presented in the public sphere-including museums, commemorations, documentaries, community histories, and public memory. Addresses a variety of activities and careers for historians outside of academia. May include or be taken in conjunction with an internship.

title: Special Topics in Public History
description: Selected topics related to a specific area of public history.

title: Museums, Memorials, & Monuments
description: This course offers a study of how American experiences have been remembered and memorialized through museum exhibits, monuments, and memorials. Themes include the ways organizations influence the construction of memorials and monuments, federal government funding for national monuments, whose histories are prioritized, histories that are forgotten, the historical context of monument erections and the establishment of memorial sites, and national debates over the construction of historical narrative.

title: Major Honors I
description: Independent research program for students who have strong academic records for a minimum of five previous semesters of collegiate study.

title: Pennsylvania History
description: This class provides students with the opportunity to gain an introductory understanding of the social, political, and economic developments that have characterized Pennsylvania from its colonial roots through the de-industrialization of the 1970s and 1980s and the individuals who helped shape these events.

title: Historiography: History & Theory of Historical Writing
description: A study of the meaning and interpretation of history, with special attention to movements of historical thought and the historians who influenced those movements. This capstone seminar course is designed specifically for senior History Majors.

title: Special Topics in History
description: Selected topics related to a specific area of historical inquiry including American history, European history, non-Western history, and historiography.

title: Honors Culminating Seminar
description: An interdisciplinary capstone seminar for students in the University Honors Program. The thematic focus of each seminar will be defined by the professor and will include intensive reading, discussion, and a written research paper.

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent research program for students who have strong academic records for a minimum of five previous semesters of collegiate study.

title: Senior Honors Project I
description: The first course of a two-semester project that can take a variety of forms, including an interdisciplinary thesis, creative arts project, or service learning project. All projects must fulfill the parameters for the Senior Honors Project.

title: Senior Honors Project II
description: The second course of a two-semester project that can take a variety of forms, including an interdisciplinary thesis, creative arts project, or service learning project. All projects must fulfill the parameters for the Senior Honors Project.

title: Motor Learning
description: An introductory investigation of principles affecting performance and retention of motor skills. Topics include learning stages, information processing, feedback, transfer, practice conditions, memory, arousal, task analysis, data analysis, fundamental experimental and statistical concepts.

title: Lifeguarding/First Aid/CPR
description: An American Red Cross Lifeguard Training certificate (valid for three years) will be awarded to students based on correctly answering at least 80% of the written test questions on the CPR and Lifeguarding test, correctly demonstrating all course critical skills, and possessing current certification in CPR for the Professional Rescuer.

title: Sports Officiating
description: Understanding of basic philosophy of officiating, including methods, rules interpretation, and technique. Students develop basic officiating skills through participation and rating, and are introduced to officiating rating examinations

title: Foundations of Physical Education
description: Overview of the physical education profession; brief history, philosophical, scientific, and sociological foundations and principles; a brief introduction to career opportunities plus overall education objectives of health and physical education programs. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Health, Nutrition & Exercise Science.

title: Community and Personal Health
description: Major global, community, and personal health issues will be explored. Emphasis will be on personal and community responsibility to address topics such as stress, sexual behavior, drug use, weight control, and other relevant health concerns. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Health, Nutrition & Exercise Science.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study or research under the supervision of an instructor whose approval must precede the student's enrollment.

title: Curriculum and Instruction in Health Education
description: Curriculum planning, methods, materials, and teaching techniques for teaching health to grades K-12.

title: Curriculum and Instruction in Secondary Physical Education
description: Theories, principles, and practices related to curriculum development and instruction in secondary physical education. Practical experience is included. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Health, Nutrition & Exercise Science.

title: Sociology of Sport
description: In this course, students will explore the intersection of sport with various social institutions, identities, and phenomena. Students will use classical and contemporary sociological theories to examine current realities, myths, and ethical issues in sport. Additionally, students will analyze the social structures in sport within and between different cultures and societies. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Health, Nutrition & Exercise Science.

title: Curriculum and Instruction in Elementary Physical Education
description: Theories, principles, and practices related to curriculum development and instruction in elementary physical education. Practical experience is included. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Health, Nutrition & Exercise Science.

title: Teaching Adapted Physical Education
description: Practical experience applying principles and practices concerning conditions and diseases (e.g., orthopedic, neurological, emotional, etc). which influence participation in physical education and recreation.

title: Principles of Administration
description: A study of leadership, basic skills, processes, and functions of management pertaining to physical education, fitness, and sport. The development of an extensive policies and procedures manual that includes, but is not limited to the following components: mission statement, objectives/goals, code of ethics, organizational plan, policies, facility plans, and operational budget. The case study approach is heavily utilized for the student practice decision-making skills. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Health, Nutrition & Exercise Science.

title: Principles of Coaching
description: A study of principles and practices of coaching with an emphasis on methodology, administration, and conditioning. In addition, social-psychological dimensions of coaching such as motivation, stress, group dynamics and leadership will also be included. Students will be challenged to develop a philosophy of coaching and critique competition in light of their Christian faith.

title: Majors Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Independent Study in Physical Education
description: Independent research with faculty supervision.

title: Majors Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Human Resource Management
description: Examination of the process and problems in effectively selecting, developing, utilizing, and evaluating human resources in an organization.

title: Employee and Labor Relations
description: Study of the relationship between the employee and the employer including a foundation for understanding how employee and labor relations relate to management practices and to the full scope of human resources. Examination of the historical development of labor and labor relations, current laws that work to balance the rights and interests of employees with the rights and interests of the employer, and the conflicts and concerns that develop with the balance is broken. Investigation of employee/employer communication strategies, performance management concepts, grievances, the structure of unions, collective bargaining, and dispute resolution. Through the use of case studies, emphasis is placed on developing the ability to recognize employee/employer concerns, identify the laws that apply, and develop appropriate solutions.

title: Working Internationally
description: Human resource management practices in other countries differ from those in the U.S., each having advantages and disadvantages. This course investigates how a country's history, culture, religion, politics, and economy impact the practice of HRM. Human resource systems in the European Union in general and the Netherlands in particular will be analyzed and compared. Different paradigms will be explored with the goal of advancing a just system of human resources.

title: Leading Organizational Change
description: The current environment in which organizations operate is characterized by unprecedented change powered by rapidly emerging technologies, information overload, globalization, higher customer expectations, and changing values, lifestyles, and attitudes. This requires effective leaders to be proactive toward change or be lost in the tide. This course examines change from an organizational perspective by exploring the change process, representative change interventions, and the role of the leader in working with organizational members to accomplish successful change initiatives. Course concepts are drawn from many disciplines and explore change at individual, group, and organizational levels. Students will have the opportunity to analyze success, failure, and multiple dilemmas of public and private sector organizations in order to better understand the causes, implications, potential leader action, and strategies of organizational change.

title: Compensation and Benefits Management
description: Explores the design and evaluation of compensation programs that will attract, retain, and motivate competent employees. Topics include job evaluation, pay discrimination and comparable worth, internal and external equity comparisons, and benefit plans; the study of wage and salary administration in public and private organizations; determinants of general wage and salary levels and structures; total compensation systems, incentive wage systems, supplementary benefits; legal framework and overall compensation strategy.

title: Human Resources Practicum
description: Practical business-related experience under professional supervision or supervised participation as a teaching assistant in departmental courses.

title: Humanities Past and Present
description: A foundational seminar for majors focused on a comparative study of the humanities through time and a methodological study of the humanities as a diverse yet universal search for what it means to be fully human. The purposes of the humanities to both humanize individuals and nurture civic commitment will be explored.

title: Topics in Human Resource Management
description: Seminar course with variable topics related to a specific area of human resource management. Possible topics include comparative HRM systems, work and family relationships, motivation, performance management, and self-directed teams. May be taken more than once for credit.

title: Health, Physical Education, Recreation Practicum
description: Practical experience related to health, physical education, athletic or leisure programming or health and fitness. May involve experience as an instructional aide, athletic trainer, recreational sports supervisor, or off-campus employee.

title: Digital Public Humanities
description: An introduction to concepts, theories, and practices of digital public humanities. The course provides an overview of the digital humanities through weekly seminars focused on key readings in the field. This course is required for all students minoring in Digital Public Humanities.

title: Humanities Projects II
description: Students employ one or more digital technologies or skillsets in a public humanities project over the course of an academic term. Students will typically develop greater sophistication in digital technology previously employed in HUMA 220 , or will be given opportunity to employ new digital tools in a public humanities project. May be repeated for up to 4 credits.

title: Humanities Projects IV
description: Students employ one or more digital technologies or skillsets in a public humanities project over the course of an academic term during their junior year. A continuation of HUMA 420 pursued during J-term or Spring semester.

title: Humanities Projects I
description: Students employ one or more digital technologies or skillsets in a public humanities project over the course of an academic term.

title: Organizational Behavior
description: Principles, concepts, and processes involved in the interface among individuals, groups, and organizations. Topics such as personality, perception, leadership development, role theory, motivation, power and conflict, group behavior, and job design.

title: Humanities Projects III
description: Qualifying students will be assigned leadership responsibilities for implementing public humanities projects. Students will continue to employ one or more digital technologies or skillsets in a public humanities project over the course of an academic term. May be repeated for up to 5 credits. Requires permission of the instructor. Registration restricted to SO, JR, SR level students.

title: Seminar in Human Services
description: This course focuses on the knowledge, principles and skills for practice in the human services. Emphasis is placed on an examination of the broad range of services and functions of the human service profession. The course will highlight the continued development and utilization of effective human services skills with emphasis on the importance of ethical, professional and culturally sensitive service delivery.

title: International Trade & Finance
description: A survey of the analytical and institutional aspects of international trade and finance. The historical and contextual elements are the foundation for the examination of current theoretical and empirical approaches to international economic and financial relations. Major areas of emphasis include international finance, international trade, and globalization.

title: Global Business Strategy
description: This course is intended to integrate the field experiences and presentations by guest faculty in the areas of international business with current developments and literature in this field. Students will be expected to prepare analytical reports on the various firms and institutions visited in the program. Major topics included in the reading and discussion in this course are the role of the multinational firm in the world economy, international business strategy, organizational behavior, international human resource management, and financial management for the multinational firm. The discussion of values and ethical issues related to international business is also included.

title: Global Marketing
description: The theory and practice of contemporary global marketing management. The context or environment of international marketing is covered along with the task of marketing within a variety of national or cultural markets. Major topic areas include the global marketing environment, global market research and market entry, and product planning and strategy in a global context. Several projects involve students in global marketing research and analysis.

title: International Business and World Economy
description: Introduction to international economic relations with emphasis on both private and public sectors.
