description: Investigation of the most important Asian philosophers and schools of philosophy (e.g., Hinduism, Buddhism, Daoism, and Confucianism), with a particular emphasis on their interrelationship with the Christian faith. Writing in the Major requirement for the Biblical, Religious & Philosophical Studies Department.

title: Aesthetics
description: An exploration of the history of aesthetic theory with particular focus on the philosophy of imagination in relation to Christian thought.

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: Selected topics in philosophy not currently included in course offerings such as philosophical problems, figures, schools of thought, or historical periods. May be repeated for additional credit as the topic/study changes.

title: Practicum in Peace and Conflict Studies
description: Field experience for junior and senior students in the area of peacemaking and/or conflict resolution.

title: Minds and Machines: Ethics and Artificial Intelligence
description: A machine with strong or general artificial intelligence is capable of though, understanding, and consciousness. There is much debate about whether such machines can be built and (if they can be built) what it would mean for a computer to be capable of genuine understanding. In this course, we will examine these conflicting claims. In addition, we will consider some of the philosophical, ethical, and practical implications of such machines.

title: Philosophy Seminar
description: Research seminar on selected philosophical themes such as realism, relativism, virtue, justice, or rationality. The writing and class presentation of a major research paper is required.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record (3.5 cumulative GPA) for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record (3.5 cumulative GPA) for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Ethics
description: Students will explore the fundamental questions surrounding ethics. Students will discuss the nature, scope, and underlying assumptions of moral language and concepts. Students will engage with the intricacies of ethical and meta-ethical theories, critically examining how we understand and discuss moral truths, language, and the nature of morality itself. By the end of the course, students will have a deeper understanding of the philosophical foundations of ethics and the complex debates surrounding the nature of moral reality.

title: Introductory Physics I
description: Survey of classical mechanics, stressing basic concepts and problem solving. Topics include vectors, motion in one and two dimensions, gravitation, Newton’s Laws, the conservation of energy and momentum, elementary thermodynamics, torque and balance, harmonic motion, and waves. Three lecture periods and three hours laboratory per week. Recommended for biology, chemistry, and health science majors.

title: Independent Study
description: Selected subjects pursued independently with frequent consultation with a faculty supervisor.

title: Lab: Foundations of Physical Science
description: Lab: Foundations of Physical Science

title: Introductory Physics II
description: Survey of electricity, magnetism, optics, and fluid dynamics, stressing basic concepts and problem solving, culminating in a review of applications of modern physics in health science. Recommended for biology, chemistry, and health science majors.

title: Lab: Introductory Physics I
description: Lab: PHYS 201 - Introductory Physics I

title: General Physics I
description: First of a two-semester calculus-based general physics sequence. Models static and dynamic mechanical phenomena by classical methods for basic analysis, prediction, and problem solving. Experiments provide practice in measurement, data analysis, and illustrations of physical principles. Vector analysis and standard SI units are utilized. Topics include kinematics, Newton's Laws, work and energy, momentum and impulse, rotational kinematics and dynamics, and oscillations and waves. Required for engineering, mathematics, chemistry and physics majors; Recommended for computer science majors.

title: Lab: General Physics
description: Lab: PHYS 211 General Physics

title: Lab: Introductory Physics II
description: Lab: PHYS 202 Introductory Physics II

title: Science, Knowledge, and the World
description: Students will engage with key thinkers regarding how science works and gains knowledge of the world, along with its limitations. They will also engage with views regarding what science tells us about reality – for example, in contemporary physics and evolutionary biology – and/or the ethical and social-political implications of science, such as artificial intelligence. Throughout, the interrelationships between the Christian faith and science will be front and center. Students should expect to gain the ability to logically analyze key philosophical issues and thinkers as they relate to science and the Christian faith.

title: Lab: General Physics II
description: Lab: PHYS 212 General Physics II

title: General Physics II
description: Second of a two-semester calculus-based general physics sequence. Builds on basics of classical mechanics and other methods introduced in PHYS 211 . Emphasis on concepts, calculations, measurements, problem solving, and applications. Topics include electrostatics, basic circuits, magnetism, light and optics. Required for engineering, mathematics, chemistry and physics majors; Recommended for computer science majors.

title: Modern Physics
description: Topics in Modern Physics: problems with classical physics, special theory of relativity, quantization of charge and energy, wavelike properties of particles, Schrodinger Equations, spectra of the atoms, molecular structures and spectra, nuclear and particle physics.

title: Lab: Modern Physics
description: Lab: Modern Physics

title: Speech and Hearing Science
description: This course is designed to provide students with a foundational understanding of the physical principles involved in speech and hearing. Topics covered include: simple harmonic oscillation, resonance, pressure, sound waves, vibrational modes, interference, and principles of sound measurements. These topics will be directly related to human speech and hearing in physiological and pathological situations.

title: Optics
description: The course presents a foundation for classical optics, including Maxwell's equations, plane waves, Gaussian beams, diffraction, optical transfer functions, interference, polarization, dichroism, and image formation. In addition, applications to modern optics, including nonlinear and quantum optics, will be considered.

title: Classical Mechanics
description: This course is a development of classical mechanics beyond the introductory physics level. Topics include: Motion with drag forces, damped and driven oscillations, Lagrange's equations, non-inertial reference frames, rotational motion of rigid bodies, precession and Euler's equations, coupled oscillators and normal modes, and Hamilton's equations.

title: Foundations of Physical Science
description: This course is designed for non-science and education majors. Students will demonstrate an understanding of the principles and models used across the physical sciences. Students will describe the major concepts in physics, chemistry, astronomy, meteorology and geology, with a focus on the conceptual understanding of these fields and their applications to everyday life. In the laboratory, students will gather physical data across the disciplines in the physical sciences and interpret the data in the light of scientific models.

title: Quantum Mechanics
description: Topics include: Modern Quantum Mechanics, the formalism, time evolution of wave function, identical particles, harmonic oscillator, hydrogen atom, perturbation theory.

title: Particle Physics
description: This course is a survey in particle physics. Recent developments in the field have seen a symbiosis of elementary particles and astrophysics. The focus of the course will be particles and interactions, Standard Model, extensions to the Standard Model, early universe, dark matter, dark energy, particles and radiation in the cosmos, cosmic rays, and particle physics in the stars.

title: Senior Research
description: This course is intended to allow senior science majors to carry out research projects in collaboration with, and under the direction of a faculty mentor. Students will typically carry out the research projects proposed in the prerequisite CHEM 393 Research Methods course under the direction of a faculty member. The semester will typically culminate with the submission of a written thesis and an oral presentation to an open gathering that is advertised campus wide. Maximum of three credits per semester with the total not exceeding 6 credits.

title: Senior Physics Seminar
description: This is a capstone senior seminar, in which students articulate their career interests, engage in discussions about physics and society such as faith and ethical issues, and conduct research on a topic of their choice. The research findings are disseminated through a presentation and a scientific style journal report. The Educational Testing Service's Major Field Test in Physics will be administered in this course.

title: Solid State Physics
description: This course provides a basic understanding of the arrangement of atoms and electrons in solid state materials, and how atomic structure is used to explain the macroscopic properties of the material. Topics include crystal structure, crystal diffraction, reciprocal lattice, lattice vibrations and phonons, free electron theory, energy bands in solids, semiconductors, and optical properties of materials.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study in physics under the direction of an instructor.

title: International Politics
description: An introduction to contemporary international politics. Special attention is given to the nature of the nation-state system, globalization, economic and political development, and regional politics, including Europe and the Middle East. Required of all Politics Majors. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of History, Politics & International Relations.

title: Politics in Film
description: This course will examine the ways in which films contribute to political understanding by reflecting political culture and structure. Political themes to be covered include: the historical and potential roles of the executive, legislative, and judicial branches; racial and gender inequality; and issues in civil liberties.

title: Comparative Politics
description: Across the world, the culture and history of people groups shape the political systems that develop and the policies that those systems produce. Students will analyze the multiple elements, such as culture, economics and institutions that shape contemporary politics around the world, with specific focus on the ways in which democracy does and does not work. With a foundation of social scientific approaches and lenses of faith, students will analyze the implications of these system-level choices for themselves and for humanity more broadly. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of History, Politics & International Relations.

title: Parties and Elections
description: An examination of the role of American political parties and the nomination and electoral process at the national level. Special emphasis on the voting behavior of individuals and groups.

title: Independent Research
description: Independent experimental research intended for sophomores and juniors under the direction of a faculty mentor. One to two credits of research will culminate in a written report, with the option of presenting a poster at a campus research symposium. Students taking three credits of research in one academic year must complete the written report and a poster presentation for the spring research symposium.

title: Political Research Methods
description: In this course students will learn how to investigate a political question, enabling them to: evaluate the process of research in political science; focus on research design; recognize the importance of reading comprehension and writing skills as prerequisites for good research; discuss ethical issues in research; acquire conceptual components of the research process (theories, hypotheses, etc); understand qualitative and quantitative methods of analysis and processing; develop computer-based training in quantitative analysis and interpretation of basic statistics. Required of all Politics Majors.

title: Politics in Latin America
description: This course will examine the interdependent relations of various factors (political, social, historical, cultural, and economic) both internal and external to the political system in Latin American states. Students will develop an appreciation of the nature of cross-national/cultural analysis, as well as an understanding of the impact of global policies on Latin American states. The culmination of this course will result in the acquisition of analytical skills to explain patterns of political stability and political change within nation-states and regions of this area.

title: American Political Thought
description: In this course, students will examine the Lockean (or classic liberal) political culture of the United States, particularly its foundation, variations, evolution, and interaction with competing, non-liberal cultural traditions. Students focus especially on how faith and the quest for greater freedom, equality, and justice have contributed to the evolution of the liberal tradition in the United States.

title: US-Latin American Relations
description: An examination of the relationship between the United States and the Latin American region, including its historical and contemporary cultural, economic and political aspects.

title: Politics of China
description: Students will examine the politics of modern China from historical developments during the late Qing Dynasty to contemporary issues under the Communist Party and Xi Jinping. We will discuss China's political, economic, and social structures with careful consideration paid to the impact of major political events and policies such as the "Great Leap Forward," the death of Mao, protests in China, and Chinese influence in international affairs.

title: Constitutional Law: Governmental Powers and Constraints
description: By reading and analyzing Supreme Court opinions, students will become familiar with how the Court has answered questions regarding the powers of Congress, the President, and the federal judiciary, as well as constraints on those powers. In addition, significant attention will be given to the Court's changing understanding of the relationship between the national government and the states, and the government's power to regulate economic activity. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of History, Politics & International Relations.

title: American Government
description: In this course, students will acquire a comprehensive understanding of the institutions and forces that comprise and distinguish the U.S. political system. More than a “nuts and bolts” overview of how American government operates in the contemporary era, the course focuses primarily on the origins and historical development of U.S. political culture, governing institutions, and political life. By examining both continuities and critical changes in U.S. political history, students will acquire a deeper understanding and appreciation of contemporary American government and politics.

title: Gender, Family and Politics
description: This course will examine the social norms of gender, the role of gender in politics and its impact on public policy. Students analyze the impact that various socialization factors (such as families), demographic factors (such as race, ethnicity and class) and gender stereotypes have on the development of women and men. In addition, the course focuses on the impact of women and men in the public (political) and private (household) spheres, along with the resulting impact on policy relating to gender and family.

title: Constitutional Law: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights
description: In this course, students will examine the Supreme Court's historical willingness and unwillingness to protect individual liberties and rights from governmental infringement. Specific topics include: applying the Bill of Rights to the states; free exercise of religion; religious establishment; freedom of speech, assembly, and association; freedom of press; the right to bear arms; the right to privacy; the rights of the criminally accused; and discrimination on the basis of race, gender, and sexual orientation.

title: Public Policy
description: Examines the planning, implementation, and evaluation of public policy with special attention given to public policy at the state and federal level. Specific public policy issues, including social, economic, and environmental topics, are discussed.

title: Terrorism
description: Investigates the theory and practice of terrorism as a means of political change. Also considers approaches to, as well as the moral basis of, counter-terrorism.

title: Russia after Communism
description: In this course, students will acquire a comprehensive understanding of Russian political development since 1985. By the end of the course, students should be able to explain and analyze how history, culture, economics, and politics have shaped Russia in ways that both resemble and differ from the American political experience.

title: American Presidency
description: This course examines important readings (both primary and secondary) on the American presidency, paying special attention to the historical evolution of the office. After discussing competing visions of the presidency, we will review the development of the presidential selection system. Next, we will approach the office “topically” – that is, with a focus both on the president’s different roles and on the relationship between the office and other political actors, both in and out of government. At the same time, we will never lose sight of the ways in which these various roles and relationships originated and changed over time.

title: Congress
description: A study of legislators, the legislative process, structures, and policy impact of the United States Senate and House of Representatives.

title: Polling and Public Opinion
description: The formation, measurement and expression of public opinion serves as a cornerstone to modern democratic government. In this course, students will explore the ways that individuals, including themselves, form opinions on political issues with attention to political psychology and sociology. Through the study of methods for gathering and interpreting public opinion, students will evaluate the role of the public within American democracy, including its effect on government decisions.

title: Poland: Nationalism and Identity
description: This course will explore the contemporary political system of Poland, including the impact of its culture and complex history.

title: Special Topics in Politics
description: Selected topics related to a specific area of politics including American government and normative theory.

title: Theories of International Relations
description: A survey of theoretical approaches to international relations, including realism, theories of conflict, integration, and decision making. Examines contending normative and empirical methodologies. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of History, Politics & International Relations.

title: Causes of War
description: Students will investigate the causes of war through the lens of social science and consider policies designed to manage and mediate violence. Investigate topics such as interstate wars, civil conflicts, peacekeeping operations, and crisis negotiations. At the end of the course, students will present a quantitative case study or research design applying one of the theoretical frameworks of the course, in a format suitable for a policy audience. Writing in the major requirement for the Peace and Conflict Studies major.

title: United States Foreign Policy
description: An examination of U.S. foreign policy with special attention given to the Cold War and its aftermath.

title: Topics in International Relations
description: Selected topics related specific themes in international and comparative politics.

title: Preparing for Public Service
description: The purpose of this course is to prepare students for some of the practical and theoretical skills required for successful public service. Specifically, this course looks at the disciplines of public service that are often avoided in school. The "soft skills" of networking, story telling/public narrative, brainstorming, running a meeting, proper meeting attendance behavior, etc have traditionally been viewed as non-academic, but there is lots of literature around all these areas worthy of attention, and these skills are critical in distinguishing the successful from the unsuccessful in professional settings.

title: Pre-Law Practicum
description: Practical experience in a law firm under professional supervision.

title: Applied Topics in International Relations
description: This course provides applied knowledge of a thematic area relevant to careers in international affairs and global service. Course materials include analysis of policies and case studies in many issue areas such as foreign policy, political economy, conflict resolution, environmental policy, international law, human rights, non-governmental organizations and international organizations.Â

title: Politics Practicum
description: Practical experience under professional supervision.

title: Topics in Regional Politics
description: Political, social and economic systems of a region of the world.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study or research under the supervision of an instructor.

title: Internship in Political Research
description: Practical experience in politics and government obtained through placement in a state office or political organization.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have strong academic records for a minimum of five previous semesters of collegiate study.

title: Writing for the Social Sciences
description: Emphasis on the basic style, format, and processes of writing, following the publication manual of APA. This includes conducting a literature search, writing a literature review, and producing a scholarly paper.

title: Introduction to Psychology
description: This course will introduce students to the field of psychology as the scientific study of behavior and mental processes. Students will learn about major theorists and methodologies utilized within the field, including a broad overview of the biological, psychological, and social factors that influence behavior. The course will survey introductory topics such as personality, psychopathology, learning and memory, lifespan development, and social psychology.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have strong academic records for a minimum of five previous semesters of collegiate study.

title: Social Psychology
description: Introduction to the theories, methods, findings, and problems encountered in the study of people as social beings. Content areas include the self, attitudes and social cognition, conformity and obedience to authority, stereotypes and prejudice, altruism, aggression and social conflict, and applications of social psychological concepts.

title: Community Psychology
description: This course focuses on person-environment interactions and the ways society impacts individual and community functioning. Community Psychology attempts to address key social issues, including poverty, substance abuse, school failure, community development, delinquency, violence, and high risk behavior in a variety of settings, including clubs, churches, schools, families, agencies, and neighborhoods.

title: Politics Seminar
description: Serves as the capstone course for politics majors. Stresses critical discussion of influential works in 20th-century political studies. Required of all politics majors.

title: Positive Psychology
description: This course examines the scientific study of optimal human functioning. Students will learn what factors have been found to help people flourish and grow. Time will be spent helping students discover some of their own strengths and how they can be optimized.

title: Group Dynamics
description: Exploration of group process (including group structure and function, leadership, decision making, and problem solving). Learning methods will include both didactic and experiential components.

title: Cross Cultural Psychology
description: This course examines many of the common topics in psychology from an international, multicultural perspective. Students also learn what factors lead to differences in human characteristics across cultures.

title: Health Psychology
description: Health Psychology is a course dedicated to showing  students  how  psychology  and  health  are  connected  and  how  psychological principles can be applied to the enhancement of health, treatment of disease and prevention of illness. In this course, we will apply the biopsychosocial model to understanding the onset, course and outcome of various medical issues. We will also explore the issue of health disparities to promote understanding of differences among groups of people and how these differences can affect health. Finally, the course will utilize a life-course perspective to examine the needs and health challenges of people throughout the lifespan.

title: Industrial and Organizational Psychology
description: This course provides an introduction to theory and application of Industrial and Organizational Psychology.  This area of study examines psychology applied to the workplace.  The two-part name reflects the field’s dual nature, with the “Industrial” part concerned with the human resource functions of analyzing jobs, and appraising, selecting, placing and training employees. The “Organization” part is concerned with the psychology of work, including group dynamics, leadership, and consumer behavior. We will consider various theoretical approaches, theory-based applications, and cultural factors while studying a wide variety of organizations. Both application and empirical findings will be emphasized throughout the course.

title: Forensic Psychology
description: This course provides an understanding of the relationship between psychology and law by showing how psychological research and theory can inform the legal process. Some specific areas of study in forensic psychology include data gathering and operations (police work, investigation), legal proceedings (expert witnesses, competency and psychological assessment, jury selection), and institutionalization.

title: Abnormal Psychology
description: Study of the biological and psychosocial factors related to the development of psychopathology. Focus on various mental health disorders, with emphasis on classification based on the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders.

title: Cognition
description: Survey of research and theory in the study of human information processing. The course covers the acquisition, representation, and use of knowledge with emphasis on the processes of attention, memory, language, and problem solving and decision making.

title: Research Methods in Psychology
description: This course will use a process-oriented approach to provide an overview of the most common research methods in the behavioral sciences. Students will be provided information regarding each step of the research process from idea development to dissemination of results. Students will then have the opportunity to apply this knowledge through the implementation of their own research projects.

title: Counseling Theories
description: Study of some of the various theoretical approaches to counseling, including psychoanalysis, cognitive-behavioral therapies, person-centered therapy, Behavioral therapy and Postmodern Approaches.

title: Biological Basis of Behavior
description: The goal of this course is to study the relationship between the physiological and psychological bases of behavior. This course will emphasize the biological basis of nervous systems disorders and mental illnesses, the pharmacology of drug use and abuse and the neural circuitry of emotion. The course will also examine the organization of the nervous system and the mechanisms of neuronal communication.

title: Personality
description: Systematic study of the structure, organization, and determination of personality including a survey of the major contemporary personality theories.

title: Life Span Development
description: A developmental approach to the study of persons from conception to death. Attention is given to the physical, emotional, social, and cognitive development of the individual as well as to theoretical and methodological issues relevant to understanding different age groups. Applications are made to the contexts relevant to various stages of development. Recommended: EDUC 203 OR HDFS/PSYC/ SOCI 101

title: Sensation and Perception
description: The goal of this course is to provide a detailed examination of the theories and research pertaining to various sensory and perceptual processes with an emphasis on vision. Students will participate in simulated research activities to explore and illustrate key principles and ideas.

title: Drugs and Human Behavior
description: The goal of this course is to provide an understanding of substance use and its impact on individuals, families and communities. This course will emphasize the biological basis for addiction and the pharmacological effects of drugs and alcohol. The course will also examine the motivations, social implications and legal ramifications of drug use, as well as the methods and effectiveness of drug treatment and prevention.

title: Topics in Psychology
description: Selected topics in psychology not currently included in course offerings. Possible topics may include cross-cultural psychology, psychology of religion, advanced experimental research, and moral development. May be repeated for additional credit as the topic/study changes.

title: Experimental Psychology
description: In-depth study of human behavior, the formulation of research problems and hypotheses, research design, statistical inference, decision making, and writing research reports. The experimental research process will be experienced by way of a semester long experimental project of the student's choosing.  This course meets the major-writing intensive requirement for the Psychology B.A., B.S., and Biopsychology majors.

title: Psychology Practicum
description: Field experience for junior and senior psychology majors wishing to clarify vocational goals. Students work under supervision of a professional for a minimum of 40 hours per credit. Ten additional hours of reading and other integrated activities per credit required.

title: Counseling Skills
description: Introduction to and practice of basic counseling skills to create and build the therapeutic relationship with attention to affective, cognitive, behavioral, and faith components of the change process. This course is didactic, practical, and experiential, with emphasis on skill development and application of counseling theories. Emphasis on self-awareness, as well as cultural and ethical issues related to counseling.

title: Psychological Testing
description: An overview of the use of psychological tests and observational techniques in the assessment of individuals.  This course meets the major-writing intensive requirement for the Psychology B.A. major.

title: Independent Study
description: Junior and senior students with at least a 3.0 GPA in their major may select a problem for independent investigation in a special area of psychology.

title: Statistical Methods in Psychology
description: This course provides a foundational background in the use and application of key statistical methods used in psychology. Students will learn to handle data and critically evaluate the appropriate statistical methods for presenting data within the field of psychology. The basic principles of probability, descriptive statistics, inferential statistics, and the use of statistical software appropriate to psychology will be taught. STAT 269 recommended.

title: Psychology Seminar
description: Discussion and analysis of the relationship between psychology and the Christian faith. Considers how Christian perspectives inform, critique, and further a professional orientation in psychology.

title: Biblical Theology and Global Stewardship (QERC Costa Rica)
description: The intent of this course will be to discern what the Bible and Christian theology articulate about the nature of the world and the place of human persons in that world. The role of (Christian) human persons in caring for creation will be discerned with special attention to issues of agrarian practices, land economies, and Sabbath principles. A variety of resources will be used to discern how to be good stewards of creation in response to modern day concerns and dilemmas in various habitats, ecological situations, and in the politics and economic practices of governments as human persons address the contemporary situation. Creation will be strongly articulated as being inherently tied to redemption, and vice-versa. As an extension of the theological aspect of this course, the natural sciences will be utilized in a variety of forms to demonstrate what the ‘contemporary situation’ actually is or might be

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record, have second semester junior status, and/or have completed at least 69 credits of undergraduate study. An overall minimum GPA of 3.5, and a minimum GPA of 3.75 for courses in the major is required, and the student must have departmental approval for enrollment.

title: Tropical Medicine
description: This course introduces students to the most important bacterial, viral, parasitic, and mycotic pathogens in the tropics and to clinical features of the associated diseases.  The course will focus on diseases not ordinarily covered in depth in a microbiology course, such as cholera, tuberculosis, leprosy, arboviral infections, and hemorrhagic fevers, among others.  Topics covered will include geographic distribution, etiology, transmission, pathogenesis, clinical features of relevant diseases, immunologic considerations, diagnosis, histological identification of medically important parasites, treatment, control and the social determinants of health.  The class will also look into the Costa Rican health system and the social determinants of health as time allows.  Also, human behavior associated with the transmission of infection and of local perceptions of the disease will be included.

title: Major Honors I
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record, have second semester junior status, and/or have completed at least 69 credits of undergraduate study. An overall minimum GPA of 3.5, and a minimum GPA of 3.75 for courses in the major is required, and the student must have departmental approval for enrollment.

title: Judaism
description: Judaism provides faith, culture, and a way of life for millions around the world. Students will explore foundational beliefs, contemporary practices, and the Jewish experience of persecution, perseverance, and hope. Students will also consider Christian-Jewish relations and the role of the modern state of Israel.

title: Tropical Ecology and Sustainability
description: Ecology is the study of relationships between organisms and their environment. A study of ecology in a biological hotspot like Costa Rica is an act of sampling type of organisms and focusing on how they live and interact with other environmental elements. The approach taken in this course is to bring together students and expert faculty guides to explore tropical ecology in the Costa Rican context. Expert faculty guides will rotate into the QERC community and lead students in the study of particular ecological elements as defined by their own specialization. Students will also observe the interactions of humans, as an organism in the environment, and examine the ways humans construct relationships with the environment that foster varying degrees of sustainability. Topics may include botany, microbial biology, ornithology, herpetology, stream biology, invertebrate biology, conservation, etc.

title: Islam
description: This course introduces and surveys Islam as a religion, a way of life and as the foundation for several empires and flourishing civilizations. Given the significance of Islam in the current global and national environments, the course explores the rich diversity within the Islamic world and analyzes the roles Islam plays in US and world affairs including Islam's relationship with Christianity.

title: Christianity in Africa
description: Historical and theological survey of Christianity in Africa from biblical times to the present. Students discuss interactions between Christianity and local cultures and to consequent developments in Christian theology. Students consider interactions between African primal religions and Islam in Africa with Christianity.

title: Christianity in Latin America
description: Historical and theological survey of Christianity in Latin America. Students examine the spread and growth of Christianity and accompanying theological developments within their local and pre-Columbian, colonial, post-colonial and contemporary contexts. Students discuss the living faith of Latin American Christians.

title: History and Systems of Psychology
description: Study the historical development of psychology by examining the foundations of the profession and the changes, within and outside psychology, that have influenced the current theories that dominate the field.

title: Christianity in Asia
description: Historical overview and contemporary survey of Christianity in Asia. Students consider the western Asian foundations of Christianity and later developments in central (Persian and Mongol Empires), southern (India) and eastern Asia (Japan, Korea and China). Students explore the confluence of history and theological developments.

title: Religions of China and Japan
description: History and development of the major religion of China, Tibet, and Japan. Students explore the distinction between religion indigenous to these areas and religions imported and assimilated into the indigenous cultures. Students analyze the interaction between these two types of religions directs attention to the influence of culture on religion and religion on culture.

title: Religions of India
description: Students explore the history and development of the major Vedic traditions of the Indian subcontinent as well as religious communities such as Muslims and Jains. Students engage with the impact of historical religion on modern India.

title: History of Christianity
description: Survey of Christianity from the time of Jesus to the present. Major developments, important turning points, and influential individuals will be highlighted. Differences and similarities between major Christian traditions (Protestant, Catholic, Orthodox, etc). and denominational differences within Protestantism will be explored.

title: Religious Pluralism in America
description: Religious pluralism has been a contested ideal throughout American history. Students examine both the reality of religious pluralism in the United States and the debates/conflicts that religious diversity has engendered. In addition to examining “religious freedom” as a legal construct and cultural ideal, students will consider claims that America is essentially a Christian nation and the related phenomenon of white Christian nationalism.

title: Christianity in North America
description: Christianity has shaped the social landscape of North America since Europeans arrived in the late 1400s. Students will consider the variety of forms Christianity has taken in North America, with an emphasis on its place in contemporary American life. Students will consider the ways Christianity has both reflected and reinforced America’s cultural and racial diversity and will evaluate the successes and failures of North American Christians in contributing to the common good.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record (3.5 cumulative GPA) for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Religions of the World
description: This course introduces the world's major religious traditions, including primal religions, Hinduism, Buddhism, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. Students will examine historical and current beliefs and practices of these traditions and will understand that the study of religion and Christian theological resources for reflecting on religious diversity.

title: Independent Study
description: Selected topics pursued independently with frequent consultation with a faculty supervisor.

title: Natural Sciences Capstone
description: Historical, philosophical, and ethical aspects of the natural sciences, with some discussion of various models for relating science and Christian faith

title: Research Methods in the Sciences
description: This course will focus on research methods including advanced literature searching, experimental design and modeling. Students working under the guidance of a departmental faculty member will select a research topic, conduct a literature review, and write a research proposal.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record (3.5 cumulative GPA) for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Teaching Lab Sciences in Secondary Education
description: A seminar emphasizing disciplinary content issues specific o the teaching of Biology, Chemistry, and Physics. Topics include curricular and instructional decision-making processes for the sciences; assessment techniques for the sciences; laboratory adaptations for exceptional learners; instructional technology applications; current trends in science pedagogy, safety considerations in the laboratory and professional development.

title: Service, Mission & Change
description: Comprehensive introduction to the field of service-learning, with an emphasis on the role of the church in service, social justice, missions, and change. Topics addressed include benefits and challenges of short-term missions, cross-cultural relationships, power and privilege, racism, servanthood, and using systems thinking of approaching social change. This course employs service-learning pedagogy; therefore, students will participate in a service project during the semester and will engage in guided reflection to apply theory and develop increased self-awareness. Students may participate in an additional summer of service, in which financial assistance is available through a grant process via the Agape Center.

title: Cultural Anthropology
description: Explore the human experience with anthropology’s holistic, global, comparative perspective. Understand the culture concept and use it to deepen understanding of self and other. Learn about how anthropologists do research and apply anthropology in service of human well-being.

title: Native American Cultures
description: Learn about Indigenous peoples of the Americas, primarily those of North America. Explore diversity within Native American cultures and points of similarity, with emphasis on worldviews and the central place of spirituality. Appreciate the richness of indigenous cultures and the dialectical relationship with socio-economic-historical contexts. Understand culture decimation and resilience, and the role of revitalization movements.

title: Introduction to Sociology
description: Study humans in their social environment, including the interplay between human behavior and social structure. Appreciate how sociologists frame inquiry and use research methods to generate knowledge. Consider important social problems and opportunities in sociological perspective. Reflect on one’s own social location and consider how Christian faith connects with social responsibility.

title: Urban Sociology
description: Survey of the spread of global urbanization from pre-industrial times until today. Emphasis placed on understanding urban life in different cultures and societies, with analysis of urban problems and possibilities in a broad range of countries. Attention will be given to the connection of urban and ecological systems and the policies and organizations moving cities to social, economic, and ecological justice.

title: Topics in Religion
description: Study of a particular theme or topic pertaining to world religions, world Christianity, or the study of religion.

title: Research Methods
description: Study quantitative and qualitative research methods in the social sciences. Consider the philosophy of research, research design, and data collection and analysis. Practice research methods such as surveying, interviewing, observing, and ethnography. Learn to read and evaluate peer-reviewed research. Write a research proposal.

title: Sociocultural Theory
description: Read classical and contemporary theory. Examine the history of sociocultural theories in sociology and anthropology. Make connections between social theory and Christian faith.

title: Community and Organizational Innovation
description: Examine principles and practices of effective innovation within the social and behavioral science fields, focusing on the intersection of creativity, collaboration, and social impact. Explore leadership theories, ethical frameworks, communication strategies, and change management practices that drive meaningful transformation. Engage with case studies and collaborative exercises to develop skills in leading with empathy, integrity, and purpose. Understand and apply skills and strategies addressing real-world challenges to initiate and sustain innovation in professional settings.

title: Conflict and Restorative Practices
description: Practices - Engage with theories and practices of restorative approaches to conflict transformation. Examine ethical and moral perspectives on justice, conflict and conflict resolution. Use case studies and active engagement to develop skills for transforming personal and group conflict.

title: Religion and Society
description: Explore religion as a social phenomenon in diverse social contexts. Learn theories of the origin, nature, and functions of religion, including classic and contemporary approaches. Analyze social problems and opportunities associated with religion.

title: Global Justice
description: Explore the meaning of justice in global and biblical perspective, with emphasis on restorative justice. Consider how justice is embedded in cultural systems, with focus on one global culture. Critically examine social institutions with respect to justice and the influence of these systems on people’s lives. Investigate ways that people reform and innovate ideas and systems related to justice.

title: Race and Gender in the United States
description: Examine contemporary U.S. society from diverse viewpoints. Explore multiple perspectives regarding race and gender. Consider ways Christian faith informs engagement with persons, groups, social issues, and ideas as relates to race and gender.

title: Foundations of Service, Mission and Social Change II
description: A continuation of SERV 231 focusing on reflection and analysis of the summer service-learning experiences.

title: Topics in Sociology and Anthropology
description: Selected topics in sociology not currently included in course offerings. May be repeated for additional credit as the topic/study changes.

title: Sociology Practicum
description: Field experience for junior and senior Sociology majors wishing to clarify vocational goals. Students work under professional supervision for a minimum of 40 hours per credit. Ten additional hours of reading and other integrative activities per credit required.

title: Major Honors I
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record, have second semester junior status, and/or have completed at least 69 credits of undergraduate study. An overall minimum GPA of 3.5, and a minimum GPA of 3.75 for courses in the major is required, and the student must have departmental approval for enrollment.

title: Senior Seminar
description: Integrate Christian perspectives with philosophical and ethical issues in the major. Explore career and vocation, articulating how Christian faith connects. Produce and present original work.

title: Independent Study
description: Junior and senior students with at least a 3.0 GPA in their major may select a problem for independent investigation in a special area of sociology.

title: Professionalism in Social Work
description: The course provides foundational information for being a professional, ethical and competent social worker including understanding professional behavior, faith and practice issues, social work competencies, and the purpose of the Code of Ethics. Additionally, the course will focus on discipline specific writing expectations including use of the APA style guide. Students review the program handbook and learn the admittance and progression requirements for the Social Work major.

title: Social Work Practice with Individuals
description: This is the first course offered in the practice series and is designed to introduce students to the philosophy and characteristics of helping relationships within the generalist framework in Social Work. It introduces basic relationship building, communication, social work principles and values, critical thinking and problem solving skills for use with any client system (individual, family, group, organization, community), but has a primary focus on working with individuals within a social context. A one-day per week practicum experience ( SOWK 251 ) runs concurrent with course work and offers an opportunity for students to begin understanding, evaluating, and assessing practice. SOWK 250 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major.

title: Human Behavior in the Social Environment
description: This course provides the student with a framework for examining the person as a bio-psycho-social-spiritual-cultural being and for studying the processes by which human development occurs throughout the life cycle. There will be an exploration of the dynamic interrelationships between biological, psychological, spiritual, and environmental factors that come into play when assessing individuals within a social framework. Content will utilize current research and empirically based theories to focus on the interactions of individuals, groups, society, and economic systems.

title: Introduction to Social Welfare and Social Work
description: This course explores the history and values of Social Work as a profession and social welfare as an institution, both of which have grown from our common human needs and social problems in modern, industrial society. The course requires discussion of different practice areas of social work, such as mental health, child welfare, poverty, aging and education, and begins to help students develop an understanding of service delivery systems through discussion of values, ethics, and fields of practice. A philosophical, theoretical, and historical framework for Social Work and social welfare will be developed, focusing on populations at risk and the promotion of social and economic justice. The course has a 16-hour volunteer experience within a social service type agency requirement.

title: Social Problems
description: Understand how social structures and dynamics can build justice and equality or can perpetuate injustice and inequality. Contemplate ethical responsibility and strategies for positive social change with individuals, social systems, and structures. Explore topics such as race, gender, class, culture, religion, disabilities, and others

title: Introduction to Child Welfare
description: This course wi ll offer an overview of child welfare history and discuss current policies and practice concerns in the child welfare field. Students will learn about child protective services, foster care, adoption, and resources utilized to support child welfare service delivery. Applying the cultural humility approach, the students will consider diverse populations' experiences in the child welfare system. The course will also cover safety and risk assessment and discuss current best practices in child welfare interventions from a trauma-informed lens .

title: Social Work Practice with Groups
description: This is the third class in a series of practice courses in Social Work. The course examines the dynamics of the small group. Various theoretical conceptualizations of the role of the Social Worker in the group are discussed. Concepts such as group dynamics, norms, and roles are examined. Group leadership skills for planning, facilitating, and evaluating groups will be developed and practiced. Content will include identifying and implementing group related empirically based interventions and services designed to achieve client and community goals. There is also a focus on the interplay between Social Work and community groups. SOWK 372 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major.

title: Field Experience: Social Work Practice with Individuals
description: A half-day per week practicum experience runs concurrent with course work and offers an opportunity for students to begin understanding, evaluating, and assessing practice. SOWK 251 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major.

title: Social Work Practice with Families
description: This course is the second offered in the practice series and will begin to develop specific helping skills for generalist Social Work practice. Topics will include the problem-solving model, and will develop interviewing, assessment, treatment planning and evaluation skills. Family developmental issues will be addressed. A primary focus of this course will be the development of a knowledge base and the skills necessary to provide Social Work intervention with families at risk. A one-day per week practicum experience ( SOWK 361 ) runs concurrent with course work. SOWK 360 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Social Work.

title: Field Experience: Social Work Practice with Families
description: A half-day per week practicum experience runs concurrent with course work. SOWK 361 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major.

title: Topics in Social Work
description: Selected topics in social work not extensively covered in other required courses that are designed to assist students in competency development across treatment areas.

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record, have second semester junior status, and/or have completed at least 69 credits of undergraduate study. An overall minimum GPA of 3.5, and a minimum GPA of 3.75 for courses in the major is required, and the student must have departmental approval for enrollment.

title: Social Work Practice with Communities and Organizations
description: This course is the fourth class offered in the practice series and is an integral part of generalist social work practice. It is designed to develop the skills and abilities of students to practice macro social work. The primary focus will be on the interrelationships between the environment, agency structures and general service delivery systems. Students will develop specific community organizing and networking skills. A strong component of this course will focus on program evaluation and community assessment and intervention.

title: Social Welfare Policy and Social Change
description: This course will introduce students to the arena of social policy and to the sociopolitical environment in which policy takes place. Students will develop a capacity to examine and critically analyze social welfare policy within historical and societal contexts. Selected areas of policy will be examined using theoretical and analytical principles. The course will focus on the implementation of advocacy strategies to combat discrimination, to promote social and economic justice, and will empower students to be agents of social change in the United States and abroad.

title: Field Experience: Social Work Practice with Communities and Organizations
description: This is the fourth field course offered in the practice series and is designed to introduce students to the philosophy and characteristics of applied macro practice within the generalist framework in Social Work. This 1 credit practicum experience offers an opportunity for students to begin developing, implementing and assessing macro social work practice within community and organizational contexts. SOWK 476 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major.

title: Preparation for Field Practice
description: This course is designed to prepare students for entering their senior year field practice experience. Student and agency expectations will be discussed regarding objectives, procedures, roles and responsibilities as well as relevant practice issues such as record keeping, stress management, and use of supervision in practice. Practical information regarding agency placements will be reviewed. Final coordination and student interviews with prospective placement will occur during this course. SOWK 484 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major.

title: Seminar in Social Work Practice
description: This course focuses on the knowledge, principles and skills of social work services. Emphasis is placed on generalist social work practice and utilizing a strength perspective in service delivery. Focus will be on the integration of models of service delivery with different client systems. Course will incorporate the values of the profession and analyze ethical dilemmas and the ways which they affect practice, services and clients. SOWK 485 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record.

title: Field Practice in Social Work
description: This course focuses on the knowledge, principles and skills of social work services. Emphasis is placed on generalist social work practice and utilizing a strength perspective in service delivery. Focus will be on the integration of models of service delivery with different client systems. Course will incorporate the values of the profession and analyze ethical dilemmas and the ways which they affect practice, services and clients. SOWK 490 requires a minimum grade of C+ in all previous SOWK courses to progress in the major.

title: Intermediate Spanish
description: During this course, students build intermediate grammar, vocabulary and conversation skills. Students expand skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening. Students enhance communication skills by using subjunctive, command, conditional and future verb forms. By end of the course, students will be able to communicate in Spanish in written and oral form at the ACTFL intermediate level. In addition, they will be able to articulate knowledge of cultural traditions of Spanish speaking countries and will be able to explain cultural and linguistic differences between U.S. English and Spanish.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record.

title: Fundamentals of Spanish I
description: This introductory-level course develops students’ basic proficiency in Spanish language and culture. It develops students’ knowledge and ability to communicate orally and in writing in Spanish at the novice level; identify and produce grammatical structures and vocabulary at the novice level; demonstrate knowledge of Hispanic culture; and articulate connections between Christian faith and language study.

title: Spanish for Healthcare Professionals
description: This course develops intermediate grammar, vocabulary and communication skills in Spanish for Health Care Professionals and expands knowledge of Hispanic culture. Students focus on subjunctive, command, conditional and future verb forms. The course is specifically designed for majors in Nursing, Pre-Med and other healthcare fields. The course prepares students to use Spanish in health-related fields. Through discussions and cultural readings, students explore Hispanic perceptions and values. This course prepares students to greet patients, perform a physical exam, discuss symptoms and treatments, and schedule appointments in a culturally appropriate manner.

title: Introduction to Research in Social Work
description: This course explores the philosophy, concepts, principles, and methods of empirical research in Social Work. Characteristic stages and formats of social research are reviewed, with the primary aim of developing beginning competence in the critical assessment of published research and the use of research methods in the field of Social Work. Emphasis will be on the development of an understanding of a scientific, analytic, and ethical approach to building knowledge for practice. Both quantitative and qualitative methods will be presented. There will also be a focus on developing a student's ability to use research methods for the purposes of evaluation in Social Work practice, programs, and policies. This course should be taken in the junior or senior year.

title: Fundamentals of Spanish II
description: Building on SPAN 101 , this course continues to advance students’ basic in Spanish language and culture. This course will strengthen students’ knowledge and ability to communicate orally and in writing in Spanish at the novice level; identify and produce grammatical structures and vocabulary at the novice level; demonstrate knowledge of the Hispanic culture; and articulate connections between the Christian faith and language study.

title: Topics in Latin American Literature
description: Read, discuss, and respond to literature by significant Latin American writers. Explore the techniques of diverse literary expressions within the Latin American tradition and reflect on the connections between history, culture and literature. Readings will vary by semester and instructor but will reflect diverse Latin American experiences and may include the pre-Columbian, Colonial, Romantic, Modernist or contemporary periods and genres in fiction, nonfiction, poetry, and drama. Strengthen skills in reading, writing, and speaking at an advanced Spanish level. Appreciate some of the masterpieces of Latin America’s best writers such as Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz, Rubén Darío, Jorge Luis Borges, Gabriel Garcia Marquez and Isabel Allende.

title: Spanish Conversation and Composition
description: Advanced practice in oral and written Spanish based on everyday situations. Study of Hispanic culture through selected readings. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Language, Literature & Writing.

title: Culture and Identity in Latin America
description: Students explore the diverse cultures and unique cultural identities present in contemporary Latin America through a variety of texts, films and music that may vary from semester to semester. Students study the numerous geographic regions that compose Latin America and identify both unifying elements and elements that create diversity. Students examine how history has shaped the culture present today in Latin America. Students discuss regional differences in South America, Brazil, Central America, the Caribbean, and Mexico. Students discuss contemporary issues in Latin America such as social class, urbanization, ethnicity, education and religion.

title: Contemporary Mexico
description: An exploration of Mexican society and culture through the examination of topics such as social class, education, urbanization, religious faith and expression, indigenous and national identity, the family, and economic development. Some attention will also be given to U.S.-Mexican relations and border issues.

title: US Latino Migration Narratives
description: Students explore migration narratives written by a diverse group of contemporary US Latino writers. The readings include the experiences of Mexican, Central American, Puerto Rican, Cuban and Dominican migrants as well as children’s experiences. Students examine the historical and political causes driving the migration of particular Hispanic groups. Class readings, discussions and assignments center on migration through the lenses of identity, education, language, faith, and family. Students should be prepared to complete advanced-level readings in Spanish and participate fully in class discussions in Spanish.

title: The Counter Reformation and the Spanish Baroque
description: This course studies the literature, art and history of 17th Century Spain through the reading of literary texts. We will use a cultural approach to perform a critical reading of these texts. Reading these texts will allow student to develop a knowledge of its literary productions as well as the religious, political and philosophical culture of the time. The work in this class allows for students’ development of critical and analytical sensitivity to literary language. We will discuss how the authors respond to the normative ideologies of their own times and approach literary, aesthetic, social, political and cultural issues.

title: Spanish Translation and Interpretation
description: This course introduces students to the principles of translation and provides opportunities to apply these principles by translating a variety of texts, including journalistic, literary, scientific, business, rhetorical, and spoken. The main goal of this course is to improve reading and writing skills in Spanish for both academic and professional contexts and to apply these skills in authentic and collaborative contexts.

title: Independent Study
description: Junior and senior students with at least a 3.0 GPA in their major may select a problem for independent investigation in a special area of Social Work.

title: Topics in Spanish Peninsular Culture
description: A study of various topics in Spanish culture that shaped or represented national, regional, and European identities in Spain. Topics may include the political, religious, literary, and artistic expression in Spain during the Arab occupation, the reconquest and unification, the colonization of the Americas, the Habsburg and Bourbon dynasties, the crisis of 1898, the dictatorship of Primo de Rivera, the Civil War, the Franco regime, and post-Franco Spain.

title: Business Spanish
description: Introduction to the Spanish of commerce and international business as well as the art of negotiation in the Spanish-speaking world. Emphasis on oral and written communication, including basic commercial practices, terminology, and documents.

title: Spanish Linguistics
description: A linguistic study of Spanish, to include its morphology, syntax, and contemporary dialectal variations. Concepts from general and descriptive linguistics will be introduced to allow exploration of the subject matter in Spanish.

title: Independent Study
description: Advanced Hispanic language/culture study or research.

title: Trans-Atlantic Literature
description: A study of the role that the written word played in building a transatlantic literary culture that linked the Spanish peninsula to the American colonies: the ways in which Spain shaped the literature of its colonies, and how the discovery shaped Spanish readers' understandings of the world and their place in it. Beginning with travel accounts and letters from America, students will explore the ways in which these reports depict and construct the image of the American other, the African slave, and the Spanish colonizer-particularly the role of intellectuals in the development of a circum-Atlantic identity.

title: Sport Facility & Event Management
description: This course is designed to assist the sport management student in acquiring the necessary knowledge and skills needed to manage a sport facility and to plan a sporting event.

title: Ethics & Law in Sport Management
description: This course integrates the ethical strategies of sport management along with the demonstration of how constitutional law, contract law, tort law, labor and anti-trust law, and discrimination apply to the sport management industry. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Health, Nutrition & Exercise Science.

title: Economics and Governance in Sport
description: The primary goal of this course is to provide students interested in pursuing a career in the sport management field with a basic knowledge and understanding of the principles, processes and strategies related to economics/finances and governance of operating organizations whose mission involves the provision of sport related services and/or products. The principles of economics/finances will be utilized to evaluate amateur and professional sports, including topics like league structure, team decision making, labor relations, incentive structures, free agency, salary caps, and stadium financing and the role of public policy. Macro and micro perspectives of governance and policy in sport organizations will be initiated to examine organizational structures, authority, sanctions, appeals processes, and the influence of governmental bodies and sports commissions on sport governing bodies.

title: Sport Management Practicum
description: This course provides the prospective sports manager with practical training and experience involving progressive learning in specific areas of interest. The student and advisor jointly select an approved site and plan the practical experience. Potential sites include, but not limited to, sport facilities, professional sports, television stations, and the commercial sports industry. Progressive and summary reports and regular meetings with the faculty sponsor and field supervisor are required. The student is evaluated by the faculty supervisor and the field supervisor.

title: Sponsorship & Marketing in Sport Management
description: Sport sponsorship and marketing is designed to apply marketing principles to the area of sport, sport events, and sport products. Sponsorship and marketing strategies including sales, promotions, and advertising of sport will be emphasized. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Health, Nutrition & Exercise Science.

title: Topics in Sport Management
description: In-depth investigation and development of an area of current sport management interest.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Senior Seminar for Sport Management
description: Serves as a capstone course for Sport Management Majors. Stresses reading current literature, discussing critical ideas, and formulating the relationship between Christian faith and the sport management profession.

title: Independent Study in Sport Management
description: Independent research with faculty supervision.

title: Major Honors
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Introductory Statistics
description: An introductory course in statistics for students in the social and natural sciences. Students will build numeric and graphical summaries of data, apply sampling and basic probability terminology, identify features of the binomial distribution, use the normal distribution and the Central Limit Theorem to find probabilities, construct hypotheses for one-sample and two-sample settings and test them, discuss conditions and potential errors, and build confidence intervals. An introduction to computing software (Minitab) as a tool is included. A scientific calculator with STAT mode for data entry is required.

title: Probability and Statistics
description: Students will learn foundational topics in the disciplines of probability and statistics. Students will investigate a set of data graphically and numerically and calculate the probability of various outcomes using probability concepts and discrete and continuous probability distribution functions. These topics are combined to introduce inference where students learn to draw conclusions about populations based on data. Minitab is introduced as a tool for conducting basic data analysis. A scientific calculator with STAT mode is required.

title: Sport Management Careers and Content
description: This course is an introduction to the field of sport management and will be completed by first-year students during the fall semester. Career options and managerial content will be reviewed. Foundational studies of sport, including history, philosophy, and professional skills will be discussed as they relate to the field of sport management.

title: Applied Statistics for Management
description: An introduction to statistics with focus on a variety of business applications. Students will build numeric and graphical summaries of data, apply sampling and basic probability terminology, identify features of the binomial distribution, use the normal distribution and the Central Limit Theorem to find probabilities, construct hypotheses and test them, discuss conditions and potential errors, build confidence intervals, and estimate parameters in simple linear regression and discuss their strength. An introduction to statistics in Excel is included as an aid. A scientific calculator with STAT mode for data entry is required

title: Inferential Statistics
description: Statistical inference is the art of estimation and making decisions about populations based on sample data. This course is a second course in statistics building on concepts introduced in STAT 269 , STAT 281 or STAT 291 . Students will conduct inference using data from the following settings: one-sample, two-sample, one-way ANOVA, simple linear regression, and categorical data in one or more variables. Students will complete an applied project during the semester. Students learn to conduct analyses in Minitab. A scientific calculator with STAT mode is required.

title: Topics in Spanish Peninsular Literature
description: This course studies the literature of Spain through the reading of texts from Medieval times to the present according to the topic selected. We will read texts from the most relevant authors of Spain to allow students to develop a knowledge of its literary and artistic productions as well as the religious, political, and philosophical culture from which they emerge. The work in this class allows for a development in the students’ critical and analytical sensitivity to literary language. We will discuss how the authors respond to the normative ideologies of their own times and approach literary, aesthetic, social, political, and cultural issues.

title: Experimental Design
description: Students will learn experimental designs and analyses for a variety of problems: completely randomized, randomized complete block, Latin square, completely randomized with factorial treatments, unbalanced and/or incomplete, multiple comparisons; introduction to SAS® and R.

title: Nonparametric Statistical Methods
description: Students will learn introductory nonparametric statistical analysis including estimation and hypothesis testing. Key terminology in the discipline is explained. This course covers methods such as dichotomous data, one-, two-, and k-sample problems, and independence tests. Throughout the course, the efficiencies and the conditions for validity of the various methods are considered. Connections across settings are highlighted. While this course is intended to fulfill requirements in statistics and data science majors or minors, it may also be of interest to students with basic statistical understanding who would like to explore analysis of non-normal data. Data analysis in R is included.

title: Introduction to Ecology and Sustainability
description: Students will learn about ecological principles and how these are applied to natural and agricultural ecosystems. Students will discuss the three aspects of sustainability - ecological, economic and social. Students will study the trophic levels in ecosystems, biomes, and global biogeochemical cycles (hydrologic, carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus cycles) as a foundation to understanding global environmental issues. The study of the interactions within and between species (competition, predation, parasitism, mutualism) will be connected to population ecology and applied to conservation of biodiversity.

title: Operations Research
description: Linear programming, simplex method, project management with PERT/CPM, deterministic dynamic programming, probabilistic dynamic programming, integer programming, queuing theory, and an introduction to SASÂ®.

title: Mathematical Statistics
description: Mathematical statistics explores the theory underlying probability, statistical estimation, and hypothesis testing with more rigor than is covered in STAT 291 . Students will learn to prove theorems related to random variables and their distributions, distributions of functions of random variables, moment generating functions, sampling distributions, limiting distributions, and the Central Limit Theorem.

title: Lab: Ecology and Sustain
description: Lab: Ecology and Sustainability

title: Sophomore Field Experience
description: Students in this course will conduct targeted observations in an urban and a rural or suburban school during regularly scheduled hours twice per week for a total of at least 40 hours. In addition to school visits, students will attend orientation sessions focused on the Danielson Framework for Teaching, professionalism and ethics, resume preparation, and teaching in diverse schools. Related course assignments will be made in EDSP 207 Introduction to Special Education and EDUC 203 Educational Psychology.

title: Food, Power and Society
description: An introduction to the issues, both national and global, associated with our food system. Particular attention will be paid to the industrial food production and consumption, social food movements, food politics, and the cultural ways in which societies engage with food.
