The course will draw on literature and perspectives from sociology, anthropology, and sustainability to unpack how inequality, environmental, and social degradation are results of our global food system. Students will then explore ways in which actors are working against these powers to reclaim food security, justice and sovereignty. Course has a 15-hour Service-Learning experience with a social service agency working in the food system.

title: Field Experience III Grade 2-4
description: Students are actively engaged in the teaching and learning process for approximately 120 hours in a grade 2-4 classroom. Students complete assignments from concurrent courses, including using PA academic standards and anchors in literacy and math, science, and social studies for instructional planning, implementation, and assessment. They create and modify lessons and assessments as needed for students with special needs who are included in their classroom. Students use assessment data to inform their classroom decision-making.

title: Field Experience II in PreK - Grade 2
description: Students are actively engaged in the teaching and learning process for approximately 120 hours in a PreK-grade 2 classroom. Students complete assignments from concurrent courses, including using PA academic standards and anchors in literacy and math for instructional planning, implementation, and assessment. They create and modify lessons and assessments as needed for students with special needs who are included in their classroom.

title: Junior Field Experience
description: Junior-level field experience for secondary and K-12 teacher certification students which provides opportunity for approximately 80 hours of observation and practice in elementary or secondary school settings as appropriate to the certification. Students are expected to connect theoretical ideas acquired in prerequisite and co-requisite courses with actions and decision-making processes associated with effective teaching. Focus on instructional planning and assessment. Students earn credential in Youth Mental Health First Aid.

title: Environmental Science and Sustainability Capstone
description: This course is a problem solving seminar reviewing the philosophical underpinnings of sustainable development and extending them by solving problems. Students will explore the fundamental assumptions of sustainability; call upon the skills learned in their specific tracks of study and work together as an integrative team to vocationally address an unsustainable situation in a specific geographic location. Course will include exploration of sustainability studies as a Christian vocation, explicit focus on project management, work tracked in increments relative to goals, and development of portfolios for each participant for use in seeking future employment. Course is intended for Seniors.

title: Independent Study
description: Investigation and application of advanced statistical concepts.

title: Field Experience III in Grade 4-5
description: Students are actively engaged in the teaching and learning process for approximately 120 hours in a grade 4-5 classroom. Students complete assignments from concurrent courses including, using PA academic standards and anchors in literacy and math, science, and social studies for instructional planning, implementation, and assessment. They create and modify lessons and assessments as needed for students with special needs who are included in their classroom. Students use assessment data to inform their classroom decision-making.

title: Nordic Cultures and Sustainability
description: This course offers students an immersive exploration of the histories, cultures and futures of Norway, Sweden, Denmark, and Iceland through a combination of readings, travel, and visits to key sites. It focuses on key themes such as energy, architecture and city planning, agriculture, transportation, waste management, climate change, religion, literature, and wellbeing. Students will visit major cities— Oslo, Stockholm, Copenhagen, and Reykjavík—as well as remote mountain, fjord, and island locations. Students will experience local life firsthand through a homestay. This experience will grant students unique and applicable perspectives on sustainable living in four of the world’s highest-ranked sustainable countries.

title: Field Experience II in Grade 6-8
description: Junior-level field experience for middle level teacher certification students which provides opportunity for approximately 80 hours of observation and practice in a middle school classroom. Students complete assignments from concurrent courses including, using PA academic standards and anchors in literacy and math for instructional planning, implementation, and assessment. They create and modify lessons and assessments as needed for students with special needs who are included in their classroom. Students identify elements of middle school philosophy and the intersection of quality middle schools with the unique needs of young adolescents. Students earn credential in Youth Mental Health First Aid.

title: Secondary Pre-Student Teaching Experience
description: Senior-level field experience in a secondary school context which provides opportunity for observation, reflection, and practice. Students will have opportunities to examine the whole school context through interactions with building-level administrators, school counselors, special educators, classroom teachers, and other professionals and para-professionals. Students will also spend time in certification area specific school settings. Students are expected to connect theoretical ideas acquired in prerequisite and corequisite courses with actions and decision-making processes associated with effective teaching. Focus on establishing positive classroom climate, developing instructional plans and assessment, and professional interactions with other educators in preparation for the student teaching experience.

title: Teaching Practicum
description: Teaching-related experiences in classrooms and/or non-traditional setting which provide opportunities for enhancing personal and/or professional skills, acquisition and/or application of content knowledge and pedagogic experiences,  exploration of additional career opportunities available to teachers, and integration of theory and practice in varied settings with diverse audiences. Credit award is based on length and intensity of involvement as well as the number and depth of learning objectives/outcomes to be accomplished.

title: Student Teaching Seminar
description: A seminar for teacher candidates emphasizing professional development, educational problem-solving, and integration of faith and vocation. Focus on pedagogical and professional issues that arise during the professional semester. Reinforcement of skills and content learned throughout the Teacher Education Program and support for development of teaching portfolio.

title: Pre-Student Teaching Experience in Health and Physical Education
description: Senior-level field experience in a Health and Physical Education context which provides opportunity for observation, reflection, and practice. Students will have opportunities to examine the whole school context through interactions with building-level administrators, school counselors, special educators, classroom teachers, and other professionals and para-professionals. Students will also spend time in certification area specific school settings. Students are expected to connect theoretical ideas acquired in prerequisite and corequisite courses with actions and decision-making processes associated with effective teaching.  Focus on establishing positive classroom climate, developing instructional plans and assessment, and professional interactions with other educators in preparation for the student teaching experience.

title: Pre-Student Teaching Experience in Music Education
description: Senior-level field experience in a Music Education context which provides opportunity for observation, reflection, and practice. Students will have opportunities to examine the whole school context through interactions with building-level administrators, school counselors, special educators, classroom teachers, and other professionals and para-professionals. Students will also spend time in certification area specific school settings. Students are expected to connect theoretical ideas acquired in prerequisite and corequisite courses with actions and decision-making processes associated with effective teaching.  Focus on establishing positive classroom climate, developing instructional plans and assessment, and professional interactions with other educators in preparation for the student teaching experience.

title: Dual Certification Internship
description: Provides supervised, site-based experience beyond the primary Professional Semester/student teaching for students pursuing dual certification. Students design and deliver instruction, acquire professional skills and experience, and perform teaching roles and responsibilities appropriate for the second area of certification. Students register for a minimum of three credits, but may elect a maximum of nine credits. Students must declare and plan for the internship at the time that they apply to the Professional Semester.

title: Special Education Internship
description: Students in this course spend a minimum of 80 hours in a special education classroom setting. In this placement, students design and deliver instruction and assessment. Students also use data to inform instructional decisions.

title: Student Teaching: Health and Physical Education
description: Observation and participation in teaching elementary, and middle or high school health and physical education under the supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers in nearby schools.

title: Student Teaching: Special Education
description: Participation in teaching in a special education context under the supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers.

title: Student Teaching: Grades PreK-4
description: Participation in teaching under the supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers in public schools.

title: Student Teaching: Secondary
description: Participation in teaching in the area of certification under the supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers in public schools.

title: Student Teaching: Grades 4-8
description: Participation in teaching under the supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers in public schools.

title: Student Teaching: Art
description: Practical experience in instructional planning and teaching art under the supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers in public schools. Includes both elementary and secondary levels. One semester (12 weeks) as assigned.

title: Student Teaching: Music
description: Practical experience in teaching under supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers in public schools. Placement is made in student's area of emphasis: vocal or instrumental. One semester (12 weeks) as assigned.

title: Early Childhood Internship
description: In this senior level experience, students participate in developing, teaching, assessing, and modifying curriculum for preschool children, in accordance with the PA Learning Standards for Early Childhood. Students do both independent and collaborative planning and teaching under the supervision of University faculty.

title: Student Teaching:  Domestic and International
description: Participation in teaching under the supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers and supervisors. A minimum of seven weeks completed in a Pennsylvania school and a minimum of six weeks completed in a school outside of the United States.

title: Pre-Student Teaching Experience in Art Education
description: Senior-level field experience in an Art Education context which provides opportunity for observation, reflection, and practice. Students will have opportunities to examine the whole school context through interactions with building-level administrators, school counselors, special educators, classroom teachers, and other professionals and para-professionals. Students will also spend time in certification area specific school settings. Students are expected to connect theoretical ideas acquired in prerequisite and corequisite courses with actions and decision-making processes associated with effective teaching.  Focus on establishing positive classroom climate, developing instructional plans and assessment, and professional interactions with other educators in preparation for the student teaching experience.

title: Student Teaching:  Dance
description: Practical experience in instructional planning and teaching dance under the supervision of University faculty and experienced cooperating teachers in public schools. Includes both elementary and secondary levels. One semester (12 weeks) as assigned.

title: Pre-Student Teaching Experience in Dance Education
description: Senior-level field experience in a Dance Education context which provides opportunity for observation, reflection, and practice. Students will have opportunities to examine the whole school context through interactions with building-level administrators, school counselors, special educators, classroom teachers, and other professionals and para-professionals. Students will also spend time in certification area specific school settings. Students are expected to connect theoretical ideas acquired in prerequisite and co-requisite courses with actions and decision-making processes associated with effective teaching. Focus on establishing positive classroom climate, developing instructional plans and assessment, and professional interactions with other educators in preparation for the student teaching experience.

title: Introduction to Theatre
description: Explore the art and craft of live Theatre through the study of the roles of Theatre practitioners including the Playwright, the Director, the Actor, and the Designers. Engage in creative activities that focus on specific Theatrical skills, and develop critical and analytical skills from an audience perspective and through historical context.

title: Production Practicum
description: Full-time theatre majors are required to participate in at least one departmental production activity each semester.

title: Production Practicum
description: Participation required in at least one departmental production activity. Required every semester the student is a full-time major.

title: Acting II
description: This course continues the actor's development of vocal and physical agility through the application of twentieth and twenty-first century acting techniques which are either faithful to Stanislavski thought or deviate from it. Modern acting theory and techniques are explored through scenes and monologues.

title: Acting I
description: This foundational course introduces the student to the craft of acting and the various ways in which the actor's art has been conceived from the ancient Greeks to the present, including the creative process, approaches to rehearsal, collaboration, vocal and physical agility, improvisation, script analysis, scene study, characterization, and audition techniques. Introduction to the Stanislavsky technique. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Theater & Dance.

title: Introduction to Technical Theatre and Design
description: An introduction to the backstage arts and technology of theatrical production, designed to give the student a broad understanding of the history and development of theatre crafts, as well as an understanding of the basic principles and technical procedures used in the design and execution of scenery, costumes, lighting, and sound.

title: Directing I: Fundamentals of Play Directing
description: Basic elements of directing, with laboratory work in theatrical problems and short scenes. Readings and discussions on directing theory.

title: Survey of Dramatic Literature and Criticism
description: Considers the development of dramatic literature, theory, and criticism from the Antiquity to postmodernism. Students interact with the ideas of seminal theorists and apply them through a variety of play scripts and productions. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Theater & Dance.

title: Musical Theatre
description: Introduction to the theory and practice of musical theatre production. Includes preparing and performing a musical.

title: Stage Management
description: Introduction and application of basic principles of stage management, with emphasis on the paperwork and procedures required in theatrical productions. Considers other forms of performance which utilize a stage manager such as opera, dance, and touring productions.

title: Acting III
description: This course continues the actor's development of vocal and physical agility, range, and expression through the application of twentieth and twenty-first century acting techniques and theorists as well as the introduction of period style.

title: Acting Techniques for Musical Theatre
description: Study and performance of scenes, duets, trios, and ensembles from the Musical Theatre repertoire. Topics include the structure of sung theatre, exercises, the synthesis of music, text and score, and the relationship between character, music and sound.

title: Theatre for Young Audiences
description: Introduction to the theory and practice of theatre for young audiences. Includes preparing and performing a play for children.

title: Directing II
description: This course explores specialized issues in the craft of contemporary Play Direction including race, gender, postmodernism, period style and various genres including avant garde, epic theatre and absurdism. Students research and analyze how specific contemporary issues influence and shape the techniques of forming the Directorial Concept, production process, communication with designers, and actor coaching. Students complete script analysis projects, mock rehearsal assignments, scene projects, and complete the direction of a short one-act play for public performance.

title: History of Theatre I: Antiquity to Renaissance
description: This survey course considers evidence of the theatrical past including theatre architecture, production values, acting and directing styles, and the political and social movements that informed these traditions. Through selected readings, discussion, projects, and multimedia, students consider seminal moments in the development of the theatre from its conjectured origins in ancient Greece and Rome to the European Renaissance.

title: History of Theatre II: Renaissance to Postmodernism
description: A continuation of THEA 340 . Considers the politics of historiography and the implications of cultural theory in shaping our own contemporary 'readings' of the theatrical past. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Theater & Dance.

title: Scenographic Techniques
description: Exploration and application of graphic fundamentals for use in theatrical design. Emphasis on two dimensional forms of communication such as drafting, costume rendering, and mechanical perspective.

title: Theatre for Social Change
description: Exploring Theatre created in the pursuit of social justice. Interact with 20th century performance practices that use theatre as a venue for consciousness raising and civic dialogue. Engage in laboratory sessions which invite the student to evaluate the efficacy of specific processes and devise and perform an original piece of Theatre for Social Change.

title: Audition Techniques
description: For seniors in the BA in Theatre and the BFA in Musical Theatre programs. Research and preparation of auditions for work in professional theatre venues. Discussions and reading address repertoire, techniques, and necessary procedures. Students prepare 4-6 professional auditions for class simulation. The term culminates in a showcase for invited agents and Theatre professionals.

title: Studies in Irish Theatre: Gaiety School of Acting
description: An applied study of Irish theatre facilitated by the faculty at the Gaiety School of Acting, Dublin, Ireland. Intensive acting workshops are complemented by seminars in Irish dramatic literature and attendance at a variety of Dublin theatres.

title: Theatre Design
description: Studio course designed to give the student an examination into the chosen area of study. Emphasis on textural interpretation, design theory and aesthetics, development of specific design concepts, and the means to express and communicate the design.

title: Theatre Internship
description: Theatre work under the supervision of a theatre professional.

title: Acting IV
description: This course continues the actor's development of vocal and physical agility and the development of period style. Through the study and application of acting techniques, the student will explore the most psychophysical approaches to acting. Non-realistic styles, improvisation and devised theatre pieces are explored through collaboration and ensemble scene work.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study or research in theatre under the supervision of an instructor. Instructor's approval must precede enrollment.

title: Theatre Capstone Seminar
description: Explores the relationship of theatre arts to faith and ethics. Requires students to discover connections between theatre practice and their philosophical presuppositions while preparing them for careers in the respective fields. Must be taken in conjunction with a senior-level project. This course meets the writing intensive requirement for the Dance, Theatre and Musical Theatre majors. Fulfills Writing in the Major for the Department of Theater & Dance.

title: Theatre Capstone Project
description: Senior department majors will conduct a significant final portfolio project as supervised and approved by the Department faculty.

title: Advanced Musical Theatre
description: This course is designed to integrate the learned skills of singing, acting, and dance into a synthesized, cohesive musical theatre performance. Including preparing and performing a significant role in a musical.

title: Topics in Theatre
description: Selected topics in theatre not currently included in course offerings. Topics may include performance styles and techniques; studies of particular periods, movements, or innovators in theatre history, theory, dramatic criticism, and literature; technical production and design.

title: Brethren in Christ Life and Thought
description: Examine the founding, historical development, and present situation of the Brethren in Christ Church, Messiah University’s founding denomination. Consider how the Brethren in Christ have interacted with other religious traditions and with society generally.

title: Introduction to Christian Theology
description: Students engage historical and contemporary expressions of classic Christian understandings of God, Christ, the Holy Spirit, humanity, creation, redemption, etc. Students will articulate issues of theological method, especially as this relates to the integration of faith and learning in various academic disciplines.

title: Creative Dramatics
description: Exploring of the theories and techniques of pedagogical, ministerial, and developmental uses of process drama and theatre activities and exercises with target groups in a variety of environments including educational institutions, youth groups, camps and adult groups. Participate in, design and conduct Creative Dramatics Sessions which use the processes of drama and theatre to pursue goals such as self-expression, exploration of issues and solutions, and personal and social development.

title: African American Theology
description: Students explore African American Christian belief and practice, and a faith forged in the crucible of struggle. Follow the theological journey from enslaved African roots to contemporary expressions of liberation, survival, activism, and wholeness. Analyze and reflect on seminal texts in Black and Womanist theology and reflect on one's own convictions. Engage in discussions and learn tools for intercultural and contextual understanding. Practice scriptural reasoning, reflecting on pressing theological questions, and articulating one’s own convictions about Christian ethics and faithful social engagement in church and society.

title: Anabaptist Theology
description: Explore core Christian beliefs through a study of the history and present teachings of a variety of Anabaptist churches. Identify similarities and differences between Anabaptists and a variety of other Christian traditions. Consider the peace witness of this tradition.

title: Pentecostal Theology
description: Students explore Christian theology, spirituality, and ethics through an analysis of the beliefs and practices of Pentecostal and charismatic movements around the world, identifying similarities and differences with a variety of other Christian traditions.

title: Roman Catholic Theology
description: Students gain introductory level knowledge of Christian theology with special attention to the beliefs, practices, and traditions of the Roman Catholic Church, identifying similarities and differences with a variety of Protestant perspectives.

title: Theology and Christian Unity (formerly Ecumenical Theology)
description: Examines various Christian theological traditions (e.g., Catholic, Orthodox, Reformed, Anabaptist, Pietist, Wesleyan/Holiness) exploring areas of commonality and difference and assessing possibilities and problems related to the goal of Christian unity.

title: Theology and American Culture
description: Examines core Christian beliefs through key movements, people, and debates in North American church life, both past and present. By reading sermons, confessions, and other theological documents in their historical contexts, students will recognize both the unity and diversity of Christian belief in America. They will also gain the ability to analyze the relationship between theology and culture.

title: Faith and Society
description: Foundational Christian beliefs are surveyed with attention to the Gospel's social mandate. The course includes reflection on the Christian commitment to social activism and peacemaking.

title: Theology, Violence, and Nonviolence
description: Students examine how core Christian beliefs and moral teachings relate to the issues of violence and non-violence. Writing in the major requirement for the Peace and Conflict Studies major.

title: Theology II
description: This course considers the biblical and theological themes pertinent to conflicts that occur on personal, national, and global levels (and are rooted in such evils as racism, sexism, materialism, and militarism) and introduces students to possibilities for prophetic intervention that works toward peacemaking, the restoration of justice, and divine and human reconciliation.

title: Reformation Theology
description: Students gain an introduction to Reformation Theology, core Christian doctrine, and ethics, with particular attention to the theologies of the Reformation traditions. Students identify similarities and differences with other Christian traditions, as well as debates within the Reformation traditions over the course of their respective histories.

title: Theology of Religions
description: A review and analysis of how Christian theologians have understood the relationship between Christianity and other religions.  An experiential learning component will focus on developing interfaith skills that demonstrate faithful and hospitable Christian identity.

title: Theology I
description: Students survey the history and present expressions of classic Christian understanding of God, humanity, the church, and redemption. Students engage basic theological methods and hermeneutical issues. Writing in the Major requirement for the Biblical, Religious & Philosophical Studies Department.

title: Contemporary Theology
description: This course will explore the work of a selected recent theologian or theological school and movement. Through reading primary and secondary theological literature, students will encounter the ways in which the theologian(s) under consideration interacts with other theologies, social and political problems, and the intellectual currents of his or her social setting.

title: Wesleyan/Holiness Theology
description: Examine major Christian doctrines from the perspective of the Wesleyan/Holiness movement. Consider doctrines distinctive to the Wesleyan/Holiness movement, such as Christian perfection and social holiness. Identify similarities and differences with a variety of other Christian traditions.

title: Topics in Theology
description: Examines in depth specific issues or doctrines. Course content varies according to topic.

title: Theology and Gender
description: Students will reflect on the meaning and significance of gender for Christian life and for the articulation of Christian doctrinal convictions from historical and contemporary perspectives. Explore the ways recent theologians have incorporated insights related to gender studies from a variety of academic disciplines. Engage the work of women theologians in both North American and two-thirds world contexts as well as the impact of social location on theological perspectives.

title: Independent Study
description: Selected topics pursued independently with frequent consultation with a faculty supervisor.

title: Major Honors II
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record (3.5 cumulative GPA) for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Biblical and Religious Studies Seminar
description: Students integrate the different subjects they have studied as undergraduates and hone their skills for lifelong learning after college. The specific subject matter of the course will vary from semester to semester. As a seminar, students will be expected to present their own work and lead discussions.

title: Major Honors I
description: Independent research program for students who have a strong academic record (3.5 cumulative GPA) for a minimum of five previous semesters of college study.

title: Global Christian Theology
description: Students gain introductory level knowledge of Christian theology with special attention to the beliefs, practices, and traditions of the Roman Catholic Church, identifying similarities and differences with a variety of Protestant perspectives.

title: Aquatic Aerobics
description: Emphasis on basic exercises one can perform safely in the pool.

title: Intermediate Swimming
description: This course is designed to focus on the knowledge and skills necessary to 1) develop and demonstrate the mechanics necessary to swim the five basic strokes (front crawl, backstroke, elementary backstroke, breaststroke and sidestroke), 2) be safe in and around the water, recognizing potential hazardous conditions related to swimming and water recreation, and 3) to improve endurance and fitness and thereby develop a greater understanding of swimming as a component of a healthy lifestyle. Students considering intermediate swimming should possess the ability to perform the following beginning swimming skills: tread water, swim on the front and the back using any combination of alternating and simultaneous arm and leg actions, and swim underwater.

title: Beginning Racquetball
description: To provide the opportunities for the student to 1 describe and understand the relationship between care of the body and well-being as it pertains to racquetball, exercise the body and participate in ongoing physical activities, and develop habits of exercise and physical activity that could be maintained over a lifetime; 2 learn and understand the rules, strategy, terminology and scoring of the sport; 3 provide a positive personal and social experience through a leisure time activity that will benefit immediate physical outlet needs; and 4 learn to play with better intensity, execute shots with accuracy and consistency and maintain a high level of physical energy.

title: Beginning Volleyball
description: This course is designed to provide students with opportunities to learn basic volleyball skills. There will be a focus on improving the skills of each student. Throughout the course there will be many opportunities for students to interact within drills and in competitive and noncompetitive situations.

title: Intermediate Racquetball
description: To provide the opportunities for the student to 1 describe and understand the relationship between care of the body and well-being as it pertains to racquetball, exercise the body and participate in ongoing physical activities, develop habits of exercise and physical activity that could be maintained over a lifetime; 2 learn and understand the rules, strategy, terminology, and scoring of the sport; 3 provide a positive personal and social experience through a leisure time activity that will benefit immediate physical outlet needs, and 4 learn to play with better intensity, execute shots with accuracy and consistency and maintain a high level of physical energy.

title: Beginning Tennis
description: To provide the opportunities for the student to 1 describe and understand the relationship between care of the body and well-being as it pertains to tennis, exercise the relationship between care of the body and well-being as it pertains to tennis, exercise the body and participate in ongoing physical activities, and develop habits of exercise and physical activity that could be maintained over a lifetime; 2 learn and understand the rules, strategy, terminology, and scoring of the sport; 3 provide a positive personal and social experience through leisure time activity that will benefit immediate physical outlet needs, and 4 learn to play with better intensity, execute shots with accuracy and consistency, and maintain a high level of physical energy.

title: Intermediate Volleyball
description: This course is designed to help students further develop their volleyball skills. There will be a focus on improving the skills of each individual through competitive and noncompetitive experiences.

title: Skiing & Snowboarding I
description: Thirty hours of skiing/snowboarding at the skill level of the individual participant. Regular instruction in stopping, turns, skiing/snowboarding under control, moguls, carving, safety, etc. with plenty of slope time to practice.

title: Lifetime Wellness
description: This course supports strategies for engaging in regular physical activity to promote long-term physical and psychological health. Students are encouraged to recognize the integrated relationship between body, mind and spirit. The course includes setting personal wellness action steps, physical activity logging, review and discussion of current resources, and reflection on behavioral choices that sustain health.

title: Dance and Rhythm Activities
description: Emphasis on developing habits of exercise and physical activity that could be maintained over a lifetime through dance. The course will include skills, patterns, and terminology to successfully participate and enjoy a positive experience that is popular among people of all ages.

title: Self Defense
description: This course will help develop basic self-defense skills. Students will gain skills using proper distance, timing, and use of body dynamics. This class is taught in a non-competitive environment.

title: Skiing & Snowboarding II
description: Thirty hours of skiing/snowboarding at the skill level of the individual participant. Advanced instruction in stopping, turns, skiing/snowboarding under control, moguls, carving, safety, etc. with plenty of slope time to practice.

title: Creative and Expressive Movement
description: Focus on health-related physical fitness through continuous movement and individual monitoring of pace/levels of exertion. Cooperative activities include aerobic (moderate to vigorous) and anaerobic (e.g., body awareness, self-expression, synchronized movements and group challenges) within a stimulating, challenging and enjoyable atmosphere.

title: Beginning Yoga
description: Students will learn and practice basic moves, proper technique and breathing for performing general yoga exercises.

title: Racquet Activities
description: Students will have an opportunity to learn basic rules, scoring, safety, etiquette, skills, and strategies. Badminton will include power play and singles/doubles. Emphasis will be placed on developing basic skills to encourage participation in the activities to be physically active for a lifetime.

title: Strength Training
description: This course provides students with the instruction and guidance to design and implement a basic strength training program for life-long fitness. The course focuses on fundamentals of strength training including training principles, techniques, and safety issues. Development of and participation in an individual strength-training program is central to the course.

title: Team Activities
description: The course provides an introduction to an understanding and applying rules, strategies, position play, lead-up activities, safety and skills of the team sports as recreational activity.

title: Aquatics
description: Students will experience a variety of aquatic exercise activities, including aqua aerobics, interval training, aqua jogging, lap swimming, aqua yoga and Ai Chi. Students will learn safety and training principals of water exercise and will modify intensity, as needed.

title: Safety and R.A.D. Systems for Women
description: This course is designed to 1) develop and enhance in each female student, the options of self-defense, and offer practical avenues for them to respond to increasing acts of violence against them, and 2) provide a college level course for the women of Messiah University in which general safety, self-defense, and post encounter options are introduced and put into practice in a controlled environment. Rape Aggression Defense (R.A.D). Basic class is taught by a R.A.D. certified instructor, following the R.A.D. curriculum. The basic course is the cornerstone of the R.A.D. system.

title: Tennis & Volleyball
description: The course focuses on a progression of basic knowledge: skills and strategies that are needed to play and enjoy tennis and volleyball as lifetime fitness activities. Includes fundamental and developmental activities, space and court positioning, and basic principles for play.

title: Advanced Volleyball
description: Advanced volleyball is designed to enhance already existing volleyball skills. There will be a focus on improving skills through competitive and noncompetitive experiences. Advanced offenses, defenses, and strategies will be introduced and developed throughout the class activities.

title: Developmental Gymnastics/Recreational & Rhythmical Activities
description: Developmental gymnastics includes balance beam and tumbling, focusing on both elementary and lower level skills/stunts. Emphases placed on basic body positions and learning sequences, spotting and safety procedures. Recreational and rhythmical activities focuses on competitive, cooperative and inclusive games, and some basic dance forms (e.g., folk, line).

title: Non-Traditional Games
description: This course provides an introduction to the basic skills, rules, and strategy for popular playground and contemporary games. Games will emphasize cooperation, enjoyment, and general physical activity rather than requiring a high degree of athletic skill. Activities may include tchoukball, four-square, and others.

title: Indoor Cycling
description: Students will be guided through a progressive exercise program that gradually increases physical activity over the course of the semester. This course teaches students basic concepts associated with indoor cycling including how to build endurance and muscular strength through structured indoor cycling workouts. Instruction will include how to use intervals, hill climbs, and coasts for building an effective workout designed to improve health and fitness. The course will also emphasize the importance of habitual physical activity with regard to health and disease risk.

title: Beginning Pilates
description: This course provides an introduction to the basic moves and techniques of Pilates. Emphasis will be placed on developing balance skills, core strength, and breath control. Students will develop basic Pilates skills, and variations thereof, that they can use through their lifetime to improve health.

title: Disc Activities
description: This course provides an introduction to the basic skills, rules, and strategy for popular games involving the use of discs. Activities may include Disc Golf, Ultimate Frisbee, and other disc games.

title: Exercise and Weight Management
description: Students will be guided and encouraged to eat a healthy diet, exercise regularly (walk, bike, swim) at least 3 days per week, and complete a brief daily devotional. The goal of the course is to have students develop healthier lifestyles through balancing diet, exercise, and spiritual discipline. Students will develop personal goals based on the 'S.M.A.R.T. Goals' principles and will be assessed based on their progress and eventual accomplishments of their goals.

title: Flag Football and Basketball
description: Students will develop an appreciation for team activities that promote skill-related and health-related physical fitness. Emphasis will be placed on basic skills, modified and small group activities, strategies and offensive and defensive play.

title: Wellness for Student Athletes
description: Student s will learn to describe principles to improve physical, mental, emotional, spiritual, and digital health an d wellne ss. Student s will i dentify the importance of behavior choices in overall health, wellness, and resilience. Registration restricted to student athletes . Fulfills general educatio n holistic wellness and QuEST wellness.

title: Managerial Accounting & Finance
description: This course focuses on the interpretation of financial information for making effective business decisions. Students will examine accounting and finance primarily from the perspective of the user (e.g., senior manager) of the information as opposed to the preparer (e.g., accountant). Topics of this course include financial theories for maximizing returns, financial statements, common financial analysis frameworks, ratios, financial modeling, cash flow analysis, asset management, liabilities, shareholder equity, investments, and valuations.

title: Couch to 5K
description: This course is for students who are currently inactive or habitually get low amounts of exercise. Students will be guided through progressive workouts that prepare students to improve health and fitness. The culminating event for this course will be completion (through walking or jogging) of a 5k event. Course will also address the importance of habitual physical activity with regard to health and disease risk.

title: Professional Standards and Expectations
description: An intensive course designed to familiarize students with guidelines and expectations related to standards of professional practice and ethical decision-making from a faith-based perspective prior to enrolling in clinical experiences.

title: Athletic Taping and Bracing
description: An intensive, hands-on course to prepare students for work in the clinical setting. Teaching and learning will take place in an interactive learn-by-doing environment to ensure competence across common taping, bracing, casting, and wrapping techniques.

title: Athletic Training Clinical Experience II
description: The second clinical experience is an opportunity for students to experience a traditional and non-traditional athletic training setting, the student-preceptor relationship, and clinician-patient interactions. Throughout the curriculum, clinical experiences provide space for implementation of learned skills, practice of all aspects of patient-centered care, and development of professionalism, critical thinking and evidence-based medicine skills. Additional emphasis will be on professional communication with preceptors and patients and inter-professional collaboration within a broader healthcare team. This experience will require a minimum of 240 contact hours.

title: Therapeutic Interventions
description: Therapeutic Interventions is the study of the theory, mechanism, and application of therapeutic interventions in the treatment of physically active populations.  2 hour, once per week lab will provide students with space to practice, develop confidence and better understand the reasoning behind selection and application of therapeutic interventions.

title: Emergency Management
description: An intensive, hands-on course in emergency preparedness and response as they relate to care of the physically active. Students expected to present evidence of completed CPR/AED certification at start of course.

title: Scientific Inquiry I
description: Course will introduce students to the role of research in the profession of athletic training. Students will learn basic principles related to evidence-based practice, research design, and statistical methods. Students will identify a research question conduct a review of literature and submit an IRB (if applicable).

title: Orthopedic Assessment I
description: This course provides an in-depth study of orthopedic conditions and assessment techniques related to the upper and lower extremity including the hip, thigh, knee, shank, foot, ankle, shoulder, elbow, wrist, and hand.

title: Fitness Walking
description: Students will be guided through a progressive exercise program that gradually increases physical activity over the course of the semester. This class will specifically focus on walking for fitness. Emphasis will be on proper form, understanding how to regulate one's work rate, and improving health. Course will also address the importance of habitual physical activity with regard to health and disease risk.

title: Orthopedic Assessment II
description: An in-depth study of functional anatomy, orthopedic conditions, and assessment techniques related to the head, neck, and spine.

title: Scientific Inquiry II
description: Course will further develop students understanding of the importance of research in the profession of athletic training. Focus will be on development of appropriate methodology, implementation and data collection/analysis. Project will conclude with minimum deliverable of presentation to peers.

title: General Medical and Pharmacology Topics
description: Examination of common medical conditions and pharmacological treatments. Additional emphasis will be on inter-professional collaboration within a broader healthcare team.

title: Gross Human Anatomy
description: A hands-on lecture/lab combination designed to develop thorough understanding of functional human anatomy. Consideration of all aspects of human functional anatomy and intelligent design will be undertaken from a faith-based perspective.

title: Gait Analysis and Pathomechanics
description: Study of basic physics principles and human anatomy as they relate to the analysis of human motion. Specific focus will be placed on human gait analysis and identification/correction of movement abnormalities to reduce the risk of injury in the active population. This is an intensive course with integrated lecture and lab.

title: Clinical Care in Athletic Training
description: In-depth review and resolution of case studies related to broad spectrum of patient care in athletic training. Patient care course is designed with two goals in mind. First, application of evidence-based medicine principles to case studies related to injuries and medical conditions occurring within the active population. Second, thorough application of conceptual knowledge developed to this point in the athletic training program from a faith-based perspective. Areas of focus will include prevention, clinical decision-making, patient-centered care, documentation, assessment, therapeutic interventions, and return-to-activity.

title: Behavioral Health
description: This course will explore the role of an athletic trainer in identifying, assessing, and referring individuals within the active population that are identified as at-risk or currently suffering with a mental or behavioral health issue. Additional emphasis will be on inter-professional collaboration within a broader healthcare team. Consideration of all course content will be undertaken from a faith-based perspective.

title: Administration and Liability
description: Investigation into administration and legal issues related to the practice of athletic training. Areas of focus include liability coverage, professional ethics, development and management of an athletic training facility, and the role of an athletic trainer in the utilization of medical insurance and the healthcare system.

title: Athletic Training Clinical Experience IV
description: The fourth clincial experience is fully immersive and may be completed in the geographic region of the students’ choice with approval of the director of clinical education.  Focus will be on progression to autonomous supervised practice, immersion in the day-to-day operations as experienced by the preceptor, and completion of guided learning opportunities related to transition to professional practice.  Throughout the curriculum, clinical experiences provide space for implementation of learned skills, practice of all aspects of patient-centered care, and development of professionalism, critical thinking, and evidence-based medicine skills. The final immersive clinical experience is an opportunity for students to demonstrate proficiency in these areas. Consideration of course content will be undertaken from a faith-based perspective. This experience will require at least 240 hours.

title: Athletic Training Clinical Experience I
description: The first clinical experience is an opportunity for students to experience a traditional and non-traditional athletic training setting, the student-preceptor relationship, and clinician-patient interactions. Throughout the curriculum, clinical experiences provide space for implementation of learned skills, practice of all aspects of patient-centered care, and development of professionalism, critical thinking and evidence-based medicine skills. Additional emphasis will be on professional communication with preceptors and patients and inter-professional collaboration within a broader healthcare team. This experience will require a minimum of 180 contact hours.

title: Epidemiology and Public Health
description: Students will explore social determinants of health and the role of the Athletic Trainer and Registered Dietitian in the creation and delivery of public health strategies aligned with efforts to reduce or minimize the spread of illness and disease.

title: Nutrition for Recovery and Performance
description: Course will include an evaluation of current best practices related to human nutrition for performance, recovery, healing, weight management, disease prevention, and overall wellness within physically active populations. Additional emphasis will be on inter-professional collaboration within a broader healthcare team.

title: Human Performance Training
description: A review of exercise physiology principles will lead to development and prescription of targeted exercise programs designed to improve specific aspects of human performance.

title: Clinical Experience Extension
description: This course is designed for students who need to complete clinical experience hours beyond previously registered coursework, due to special circumstances. Instructor permission is required.

title: Business Analytics
description: This course examines the role of analytics in organizations. Students examine the various methods and processes for transforming data into insights to improve managerial decisions. Topics include data management, data visualization, predictive modeling, data mining, forecasting simulation and optimization. Special attention is paid to the art of collecting, analyzing, understanding, communicating, and formulating decisions about business related activities using data and big data. Hands-on projects apply concepts and tools to translate data sets into relevant, actionable information that is communicated in ways to drive analytics into action.

title: Graduate Seminar
description: Course serves as a guided and directed learning experience to provide students an opportunity to deepen their knowledge in identified growth areas related to the five practice domains of an athletic trainer.

title: Artificial Intelligence Solutions
description: This course equips you to tackle real-world challenges with the power of Artificial Intelligence (AI). We'll move beyond foundational concepts and delve into the practical application of AI for developing effective solutions. Students will learn about AI techniques related to machine learning algorithms, deep learning frameworks, and natural language processing tools. Students will learn to use the right technique for the problem. Students will also develop a data-centric approach to AI solutions through practical exercises and projects.

title: Artificial Intelligence Law, Ethics, and Policy
description: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming society, but this progress brings a new set of challenges. By the end of this course, students will demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of the legal, ethical, and policy considerations that will shape the future of AI. Navigating the Legal Landscape: Students will explore emerging legal frameworks surrounding AI, examining issues like liability, intellectual property, and privacy in the context of AI development and deployment. Ethical Dilemmas of AI: Students will uncover the ethical considerations of AI, including bias, fairness, and accountability. Analyze real-world scenarios and engage in discussions about responsible AI development. Policy Implications: Students will examine the critical role of policy in shaping the future of AI. Discuss how governments and organizations are developing policies to ensure the ethical and safe use of AI technologies. Case Studies and Discussions: Students will engage with thought-provoking case studies to understand the practical implications of AI law, ethics, and policy.

title: Innovation and Entrepreneurship
description: This course focuses on new product, service, and business model innovation with a special emphasis on entrepreneurial ventures. Topics in this course include market strategies, industry analysis, competitive forces, business model generation, customer value proposition creation, LEAN startup practices, securing financial capital, and business pitching practices.

title: Concepts in Artificial Intelligence
description: This course is designed to provide a solid foundation in the core ideas and technologies that power AI. Throughout the course, students will gain a comprehensive understanding of key terms like machine learning, deep learning, and neural networks. Students will learn how these techniques are used to create intelligent systems that can learn from data, solve problems, and even make decisions. Students will also delve into real-world applications of AI in various fields like natural language processing (think chatbots and voice assistants). By the end of the course, students will have a strong grasp of AI's potential and impact on society.

title: Athletic Training Clinical Experience III
description: The third clinical experience is an opportunity for students to experience a traditional athletic training setting with an emphasis on collision sport. Throughout the curriculum, the clinical experiences provide space for implementation of learned skills, practice of all aspects of patient-centered care, and development of professionalism, critical thinking and evidence-based medicine skills. Additional emphasis will be on inter-professional collaboration within a broader healthcare team. This experience will require a minimum of 300 contact hours with an expectation that the student will complete 20 hours/week.

title: Applications of Business Analytics
description: Students will dig deep into the exciting world of data analytics, showcasing its practical applications across diverse fields. You'll move beyond the theory to explore how data analytics is used to solve real-world problems and drive impactful results. The key areas of exploration are business intelligence and decision making, financial analytics, risk management, operations management, and supply chain management. This course will include visualization and presentation.

title: Management Capstone
description: This course is an independent learning experience designed to allow the student the opportunity to incorporate the material learned in the MBA program in an integrative, applied project. Students in this course complete a field project in an organization of their choice, supervised by a faculty member. (Dual degree MSN students must demonstrate nursing and business competencies in their capstone project through completion of 180 clinical/project hours.)

title: Professional Issues and Ethics for Counselors
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the history and philosophy of the counseling profession, professional roles and functions, ethical and legal considerations, and professional organizations and credentialing. Current issues within the professional counseling field will provide a context for exploring ethical and professional orientation. An emphasis will be placed on multicultural and self-care issues as related to ethical decision making. Students will demonstrate knowledge of and ability to apply ACA ethical code. Students will also be introduced to the practice of essential interviewing and counseling skills, as well as various basic crisis response skills within the realm of professional counseling practice. Students must complete this course within the first twelve months of being enrolled in the program.

title: Human Resource Management
description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of human resource management (HRM). Students will learn to integrate HRM with financial management, strategic planning, and core business skills to drive organizational success. Key topics include effective workforce planning, budgeting and resource allocation, decision-making strategies, productivity measurement, and performance improvement.  This course equips organizational leaders with the tools to align HR management with organizational goals to deliver measurable results.

title: Lifespan Development
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the nature and needs of individuals at all developmental stages and from a variety of racial and ethnic backgrounds. Topics will include theories of individual and family development across the life span, the family life cycle, theories of learning and personality development, human behavior, community resilience, theories of addictions, and strategies for facilitating optimum development over the lifespan.

title: Foundations of Clinical Mental Health Counseling
description: This course provides students with an introduction to the field of clinical mental health counseling. Students will learn about the mental health system and the roles of counselor, consultant, and advocate within that system. Students will gain knowledge about professional issues affecting mental health counselors including licensing, ethical and legal issues, program evaluation and multicultural competence. Students will practice treatment planning and documentation within a biopsychosocial model.

title: Foundations of School Counseling
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the historical development of school counseling as well as the professional roles and functions of the school counselor. Students will gain knowledge about professional issues affecting school counselors as well as develop an awareness of the ethical and legal issues specific to counseling in the school setting.

title: Business Strategy and Execution
description: This integrative course focuses on values-led organizations, examining topics such as vision, mission, core values, strategy, planning, decision making and management execution. In addition, this course provides opportunities for students to apply management techniques to aid in strategic planning and execution in a changing business environment, and to examine how faith informs and gives purpose to business and profits.

title: Multicultural Issues for Counseling Professionals
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the cultural context of relationships, issues and trends in multicultural and diverse societies. In addition, students will learn about multicultural and pluralistic trends and treatment strategies related to such factors as culture, race, ethnicity, nationality, age, gender, sexual orientation, mental and physical characteristics, education, disability, family values, religious and spiritual values, socioeconomic status and unique characteristics of individuals, couples, families, groups and communities.

title: Counseling Children and Adolescents
description: This course will explore therapeutic strategies used with children & adolescents. The focus will be on the practice of techniques used in counseling interventions with children and adolescents as well as a consideration of the presentation of common childhood and adolescent disorders. In addition to the role of practitioner, the role of consultant, advocate, and collaborator will be explored. Relevant legal and ethical issues will also be considered, such as the role of parental privilege, child/adolescent client's rights, confidentiality, and child abuse notification requirements.

title: Autism Spectrum Disorders
description: The course will focus on effective educational practices designed to meet the needs of children and early adolescents with autism spectrum disorders in the continuum of alternative educational settings. Specific emphasis will be placed on understanding the characteristics and impact of these disorders on learning, assessment for the purpose of designing instruction and monitoring progress, curriculum selection and development and implementation of evidence-based practices and strategies found to be effective in supporting students with autism spectrum disorders.

title: Counseling Theories
description: This course provides students with an intensive look at counseling theories to provide consistent models to conceptualize client presentation and to select appropriate counseling interventions. Students will examine the historical development of affective, behavioral and cognitive theories and will apply material to case studies. Students will also explore counseling models that are consistent with current professional practice and research to help facilitate a personal theoretical approach. Students will demonstrate essential interviewing skills, communication of empathy, and the application of a particular counseling theory in mock counseling sessions.

title: Effective Practices of School Counselors
description: This course focuses on the strategies and techniques used by school counselors as they respond to the individual personal/social needs of all students. Students will learn to evaluate assessment information to identify needs, set goals, and develop services to promote the success of students. A multidisciplinary focus will be taken and family, social, and cultural contexts will be considered. Students will receive a brief overview of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental disorders-5 as well as an overview of crisis prevention and intervention.

title: Psychopathology and Diagnosis Across the Lifespan
description: This course is an exploration of mental illness across different ages. It includes the study of the classification, etiology, and treatment of psychopathology and personality disorders which are present across the lifespan, as well as an examination of the current Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of the American Psychiatric Association (DSM-5). Students will explore diversity issues that may influence the diagnosis and treatment process.

title: Organization and Administration of School Counseling Programs
description: This course covers the nature, development, and management of school counseling programs, including the differences between elementary and secondary school contexts. Students will learn to assess, plan, and integrate the elements of a comprehensive school counseling program that reflects the programmatic and ethical standards of the ASCA NM. A special focus will be given to the academic achievement and college and career readiness of students. Consideration will be given to the use of both formative and summative data disaggregation and analysis to ascertain the success of program elements.

title: Career Counseling Across the Lifespan
description: This course provides students with an understanding of career development theories and decision making models, career information resources, career planning, and career counseling processes. The course also addresses the special issues concerning legal and ethical standards in career counseling, multicultural issues and gender bias, the mutual impact of career and cultures, as well as an appreciation for career trends across the lifespan.

title: Contemporary and Integrative Theories of Family Counseling
description: This course centers on contemporary (e.g., solution-focused, narrative, constructivist, etc). and integrative (multi-systemic, metaframeworks, etc). theories and models of marriage, couple and family counseling. A focus will be on understanding appropriate modalities for family assessment and treatment including developing one's theoretical basis for practice and responsible use of integrative techniques.  Special emphasis will be placed on evidence-based treatments and a common factors approach.

title: Foundations of Marriage, Couple and Family Counseling
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the historical and theoretical bases for the practice of marriage, couple, and family counseling. Students will also learn about the scope of marriage, couple and family counseling. Special attention is given to family systems theory including an overview of foundational systems-oriented theories (Bowenian, Strategic, Structural, etc). Students are encouraged to examine their own assumptions about families and to develop increased congruence between their assumptions and the various theoretical perspectives on family development, functioning, interaction and intervention. Special attention will be given to the impact of issues such as gender, culture, and ethnicity on the family system.

title: Premarital and Marital Counseling
description: This course focuses on approaches that have been developed for thorough counseling with premarital and marital couples. Students will be introduced to selected counseling/therapy models which seek to identify and describe both normal (functional) and abnormal (dysfunctional) marital and coupling patterns or facilitate client goals throughout the family life cycle and with couples from diverse backgrounds. A variety of possible theoretical approaches and interventions useful in the assessment and treatment of couples will be examined. Emphasis will be on utilizing the resources, strengths, and creative change generating capacities of the couple in moving toward a set of mutually agreed upon goals.

title: Spiritual Formation and Faith Based Counseling
description: In this course, students explore personal and professional issues of faith and spirituality as they pertain to clinical competency in professional counseling. Through experiential learning, students conceptualize client development using a variety of spiritual formation models and understand how diverse orientations may relate to his or her own spiritual faith journey and identity. Students also have the opportunity to develop a counseling framework that works through the identification of universal themes and respect for alternative faith traditions with a particular emphasis on the Christian perspective.

title: Spiritually Integrated Psychotherapy (SIP)
description: Students will explore personal and professional issues of religion and spirituality as they pertain to clinical competency in professional counseling. Through study of Spiritually Integrated Psychotherapy (SIP) training content designed by the credentialing organization of the Association for Clinical Pastoral Education (ACPE), and readings designed to provide in-depth discussion of those terms and concepts, students will engage in experiential learning to conceptualize specific client issues within a spiritual lens. Students also have the opportunity to develop a spiritual counseling framework that will work within a respectful perspective of Christian and other faith traditions that may be held by the client.

title: Human Sexuality
description: This course is designed for counseling professionals whose work will bring them into contact with clients experiencing problems and concerns with their sexuality. The course is designed to develop: a students' knowledge base related to human sexuality, b an understanding of the varied sexuality issues which may be encountered in professional counseling practice, c the assessment and intervention skills required to deal with sexuality issues, and d increased awareness of one's personal perceptions, attitudes and ethical considerations related to sexuality issues. Students will consider how counseling about sexuality issues is related to the counselor's and the client's spiritual framework and belief system.

title: Counseling Techniques
description: This course offers the student the opportunity to continue to investigate and practice some of the foundational and advanced counseling techniques used by professional counselors. Since instruction is both didactic and experiential, the course offers the student the opportunity for supervised practice of several different types of counseling techniques which will increase the student's skill and insight.  Students will begin to develop an understanding of the relationship between the particular techniques chosen by professional counselors and the problems presented by clients. Students will also begin to develop a system of conceptualization that fosters an understanding of the client as a whole and an orientation towards wellness and prevention as desired counseling goals. In addition, basic supervision models practices, and processes will be discussed during this course.

title: Group Counseling
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of group counseling and group dynamics, focusing on theoretical frameworks, types of groups, and essential skills for effective group work. Students will explore the integration of social justice considerations, strategies for managing group dynamics, and group leadership techniques. The course also covers the ethical implications of group work, the incorporation of technology, and best practices for creating inclusive and engaging group experiences. Through experiential group activities, students will apply course material to assist with developing practical skills for facilitating and leading diverse groups.

title: Marriage, Couple and Family Counseling
description: This course is designed for those focusing on the clinical mental health track and is intended to help these students develop a conceptual knowledge and effective professional skill for work with couples and families. Emphasizing systems theory, this course examines foundation theories as well as models and methods of intervention with couples and families.

title: Crisis, Trauma, and Grief Counseling
description: This course examines the dynamics and treatment of developmental and situational crises, trauma, and grief in a variety of settings. Students will apply crisis intervention theory and models of intervention to various problem areas, such as suicide, sexual assault, domestic violence, substance abuse, divorce, grief and loss, and disaster relief.

title: Expressive Arts in Counseling
description: The purpose of this course is to enhance competence of effective application of expressive arts in counseling with diverse clients. Students will learn how to administer and process therapeutic activities in a way that leads to positive change, increased self-awareness, and improved mental health. The course instructor will demonstrate several expressive arts interventions including activities utilizing creative writing, visual arts, and sandtray therapy. Students will practice in dyads, reflect on their learning by integrating a review of relevant literature, and submit recorded mock sessions to the instructor for feedback. Students will also integrate the skills learned in this class into their evolving theoretical orientation and create a treatment plan that reflects conceptualization skills and multicultural competence. Students will be required to purchase additional materials (e.g., sandtray manipulatives) for the course.

title: Effective Practices for Every Learner
description: This course will enable students to investigate the legislation and landmark litigation that govern student eligibility for special education, Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973 service plans, gifted education and services for English Language Learners. Students will also learn about the major areas of exceptionality including the characteristics, incidence, etiology and diagnostic criteria connected with those areas of exceptionality. Educational and therapeutic services available to diverse learners with exceptionalities and without will also be explored. With this foundation, students will be able to analyze and apply best practices and sound professional strategies to support exceptional and diverse learners in educational environments.

title: Substance Abuse/Addiction and Families
description: This course provides students with an overview of the processes of substance abuse counseling. Topics include issues related to addictions, assessment and diagnosis, and treatment strategies and treatment planning. Special emphasis will be given to family dynamics related to addiction and addiction recovery.

title: Research Design & Statistics
description: This course provides students with an understanding of research methods such as qualitative, quantitative, single case designs, action research, and outcome based research. Fundamentals of statistics and statistical analysis, ethical and legal considerations in research, and the importance of research within the counseling profession will also be discussed. The course provides students with the skills which are necessary to evaluate the current research in the field of counseling.

title: Anger, Ostracism, Forgiveness and Reconciliation
description: This course is designed to help students learn how anger, ostracism, forgiveness, and reconciliation can be approached in counseling with client-based case conceptualization and treatment planning. Students will learn strategies and techniques for effective anger processing; communication and negotiation; and conflict resolution that can lead to forgiveness and reconciliation. Factors that relate to the multicultural context of the client's perspective will be explored as a part of this process.

title: Assessment Techniques for Individuals, Couples, and Families
description: This course provides students with an understanding of intellectual, academic achievement, personality, and psychopathology tests and other appraisal methods used in the counseling field for individuals, couples, and families. Focus will be given to assessment of different dimensions of functioning--behavioral, emotional, psychological, and personality among children, adolescents, adults, couples, and family dynamics. Students will develop skills in the selection, administration, and interpretation of assessment tools used to evaluate client issues and functioning for individuals, couples, and families.

title: Evidence Based Treatment of Psychopathology
description: This course reviews evidence-based treatments of mental and emotional disorders for clients across the lifespan. Students learn to create and execute comprehensive treatment plans using theory based case conceptualization and counseling and psychotherapy outcome research.

title: Marriage, Couple and Family Counseling Skills and Practice
description: This course is designed to provide students with specific knowledge and skills to work with couples and families in counseling. Techniques, assessments, interventions, and strategies will be researched, discussed and practiced to develop preventive approaches as well as approaches to facilitate optimal functioning in the midst of various challenges in marriage, couple, and family life (e.g., stepfamily issues, divorce/remarriage, extramarital affairs, etc). A special emphasis will be placed on families and change, coping with stressful events and transitions in family life.

title: Counseling Practicum
description: This is an initial field placement of 100 hours, 40-50 of which are direct service hours with individuals, couples, families and/or groups. Students are also encouraged to work with clients with diverse cultural backgrounds (different from their own) for 10 hours (direct or indirect) and with clients with disabilities for 10 hours (direct or indirect).

title: Counseling Internship II
description: This course is a 300 clock hour experience, 120-125 of which are direct service hours with individuals, couples, families, and/or groups. Students are also encouraged to work with clients with diverse cultural backgrounds (different from their own) for 10 hours (direct or indirect) and with clients with disabilities for 10 hours (direct or indirect).

title: Counseling Internship I
description: This course is a 300 clock hour experience, 120-125 of which are direct service hours with individuals, couples, families and/or groups. Students are also encouraged to work with clients with diverse cultural backgrounds (different from their own) for 10 hours (direct or indirect) and with clients with disabilities for 10 hours (direct or indirect).

title: Secondary School Counseling Internship
description: This course is a 300 hour experience under the supervision of an approved site supervisor who is a practicing secondary school counselor. As part of the 300 hours, interns are required to have a minimum of 120 clock hours of direct service to students and are to demonstrate master's-level performance in: the essential services of counseling, consulting, coordinating, and appraising; planning and implementation of classroom guidance activities; professional conduct in interactions with field and college supervisors, students, teachers, administrators, and community members; and such other activities defined as the responsibility of the school counselor at their secondary school placement.
