description: This course is designed for nurse educator and nursing leadership students who seek an integrated understanding of advanced health assessment, pathophysiology, and pharmacology to enhance their clinical knowledge and interdisciplinary practice skills. Emphasis is placed on synthesizing these concepts to support safe, evidence-based care across diverse patient populations. Through case studies, clinical scenarios, and applied learning activities, students will build a solid framework for recognizing and responding to complex patient needs. Students will advance their knowledge and skills to provide a higher level of comprehensive care in their respective roles. This course does not fulfill nurse practitioner track requirements.

title: Advanced Pharmacology
description: This course provides the knowledge and skills to pharmacologically manage a patient's health problems in a safe, high quality, and cost-effective manner. Emphasis is on the development of therapeutic decision-making in drug selection for the patient based on health problems, individual variations, and economic considerations. Focus is on prescriptive practice, client education, and monitoring therapeutic response to pharmacological agents in diverse clients across the lifespan. Students in a Family Nurse Practitioner track must complete this course within 4.5 years of degree or certificate conferral, or the course must be repeated before graduation.

title: Critical Appraisal for Evidence-Based Practice in Nursing
description: This course provides an integrated approach to statistics, research methods, and evidence-based practice (EBP) using the John Hopkins Model to prepare graduate nursing students with advanced skills needed to critically appraise and apply the best scientific and experiential (patient and provider) evidence in clinical practice. Students will learn to apply statistical reasoning, utilize databases for evidence retrieval, and evaluate ethical concerns in research. Emphasis is placed on the interpretation and synthesis of a variety of research and non-research evidence to support safe, high-quality, and culturally sensitive care. Additionally, students will gain experience in providing leadership in planning for practice change and disseminating recommendations based on scholarly findings.

title: Advanced Writing for Healthcare  Professions
description: This course reviews foundational writing concepts to prepare graduate students for academic, professional, and scholarly written communication. Emphasis is placed on review of APA formatting and writing style to develop competence for the application of writing to a professional and academic context.

title: Curriculum in Nursing Education
description: This course focuses on implementing principles of curricular development, instructional design, and program evaluation through design of a course syllabus. Emphasis is placed on aligning curricula with educational standards, including accreditation standards. Students advocate for policy change to support learning within a diverse population, identify interprofessional education strategies, and analyze use of technology in nursing curricula delivery.

title: Teaching & Learning in Nursing Education
description: The focus of this course is professional role development of the nurse educator. Theoretical and philosophical foundations of nursing education and learning theories are introduced. Strategies to facilitate learning and support diverse learning needs of students are explored.

title: Principles of Measurement and Evaluation
description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of key principles and best practices in assessment and evaluation within nursing education. Students will learn how to design, implement, and analyze assessments to measure student learning, clinical competencies, and program effectiveness. Emphasis will be placed on test item construction and analysis, rubric development, and the role of self and peer assessment. Students will examine strategies to advance critical decision making that support student development into safe competent nurses.

title: Wind Conductors Symposium II
description: The purpose of this course is to develop a student's advanced ability, in the area of wind conducting, to 1 communicate musical ideas in a score through gestural communication, employing appropriate and expressive baton technique; 2 apply skills and knowledge of music theory, music history and musicianship to score study; and 3 demonstrate a critical awareness of conducting technique and principles of cause and effect between gesture and sound.

title: Written Comprehensive Exam: Choral
description: The written comprehensive exam is a complement to the recital where students will have the opportunity to articulate decisions pertaining to selection of choral repertoire and the musical choices displayed in the presentation of the music with committee members from the conducting faculty. The student will also be asked to present ideas relating to the leadership role taken on by choral conductors and the responsibilities that accompany that role.

title: Clinical Specialty Practicum Experience
description: This course is an optional course for students wanting to focus on a clinical specialty as nurse educators. Students have the opportunity to acquire 150 hours of additional clinical experience by partnering with a professional educator or advanced practice nruse in a clinical specialty of interest. Individual or group seminars are conducted to discuss topics and issues related to the clinical focus area.

title: Theory, Roles, and Issues of Advanced Nursing Practice
description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of the theoretical foundations, evolving roles, and critical issues shaping advanced nursing practice. Students will engage in a comprehensive examination of the theoretical frameworks and models underpinning advanced nursing practice and their application to research, evidence-based practice, and quality improvement. Additionally, the role of the advanced practice nurse will be explored across a variety of settings, through the lens of contemporary issues, fostering a deep understanding of their applications in complex healthcare environments.

title: Teaching Practicum
description: This course focuses on developing nurse educator competencies in both higher education and clinical practice learning environments to facilitate role formation. Students use various teaching modalities that facilitate critical thinking and clinical decision-making in learners. Emphasis is placed on applying theory and evidence-based teaching practices through mentored practicum experiences. Throughout the practicum, students will assume increasing responsibility in various educator roles, fostering their ability to implement effective solutions, promote interdisciplinary collaboration, and influence positive changes within nursing education. The course prepares students for nursing education roles, empowering them to utilize student-centered approaches and foster a culture of safety, civility, and excellence. This is a variable credit course (1 – 3 credits). One credit equals 60 hours of practicum, and 180 hours are needed. Prerequisites: NURS 520 , NURS 521 , and NURS 522 .

title: Leadership for Advanced Nursing Roles
description: This course is designed to cultivate leadership skills essential for nursing professionals across diverse roles and settings. Students will develop a greater capacity for leadership through exploration of theory, professional competencies, and reflective practice. Key concepts covered include communication, relationship management, personal and professional accountability, and adapting to change. Additionally, the course emphasizes evidence-based practice (EBP), compliance with standards, quality and safety, and the critical role of nurses in organizational leadership. For Fall 2025, this course will run in it's prior version with 4 credits.

title: MSN Capstone
description: This course provides students with the opportunity to integrate and apply the knowledge, skills, and competencies acquired throughout their graduate program, including connections to faith, to address a relevant issue in healthcare or nursing practice, using evidence-based approaches to improve patient outcomes, clinical practices, healthcare systems, or nursing education. With close faculty advisement, students will identify a pertinent clinical/educational issue, conduct a thorough literature review, design an intervention, improvement plan, or scholarly project, evaluate its impact, and disseminate outcomes. Emphasizing leadership and professional role development, critical thinking, and communication skills, the capstone experience prepares students for advanced roles in nursing practice, education, or healthcare leadership. Prerequisite: NURS 550 or NURS 551 . (3 credits - 180 clinical hours).

title: Leadership Practicum
description: This course focuses on developing nurse leader competencies in a variety of healthcare settings to facilitate role formation. Students develop leadership skills, including strategic decision-making, interprofessional team collaboration, human resource management, and quality improvement. Emphasis is placed on the application of theory and leadership practices through practicum experiences. Throughout the practicum, students will assume increasing responsibility in various leadership roles, fostering their ability to implement effective solutions, promote interdisciplinary collaboration, and influence positive changes within healthcare systems. The course prepares students for advanced leadership roles, empowering them to drive high-quality, patient-centered care and foster a culture of safety and excellence. This is a variable credit course (1 – 3 credits). One credit equals 60 hours of practicum, and 180 hours are needed. Prerequisites/corequisites: NURS 560 and LEAD 550 .

title: Translating Evidence into Practice
description: This course examines the evidence-based practice process from problem identification through dissemination. Emphasis is placed on preparing students to conduct a DNP project.

title: Principles of Population Health
description: This course examines how advanced clinical roles apply population health principles, by focusing on social determinants of health, disparities, epidemiology, and public health interventions. Students will analyze and translate public health data as well as engage in disaster preparedness and program evaluation. Emphasis is placed on information technology, leadership, policy development, and multidisciplinary collaboration, with consideration of the socioeconomic impact of interventions. A 20-hour clinical component provides hands-on experience in community assessment and evaluation.

title: Clinical Extension
description: This course is designed for students who need to complete clinical hours beyond previously registered coursework, due to special circumstances. Instructor permission is required.

title: Advanced Family Nursing Practice of Adults I
description: This course expands on adult assessment concepts, emphasizing the diagnosis and management of acute and chronic conditions commonly encountered in primary healthcare settings. Students will apply advanced pathophysiology, pharmacology, health assessment, diagnostics, and differential diagnosis to guide clinical decision-making based on evidence-based practice guidelines. Using a systems approach, the course highlights chronic care management, health promotion, disease prevention, and restorative/supportive care. During clinical practicum hours, students participate in hands-on experience in history-taking and physical assessment, while fostering communication strategies to build professional relationships and promote interprofessional collaboration. Prerequisite: NURS 601 . Corequisite: NURS 701C . (5 credits, 3 credits theory, 2 credits clinical = 120 hours)

title: DNP Project Proposal
description: This course focuses on developing the DNP project practice question, intervention, and proposal, while examining organizational culture and stakeholder commitment. Students will defend their proposal for approval. All DNP project courses ( NURS 632 , 633, 731, and 732) must be taken sequentially. If interruption in project course sequence occurs, students must repeat the course(s) or gain approval for progression from the graduate nursing program. Prerequisite: NURS 623 .

title: DNP Project Readiness
description: This course focuses on completion of the preparatory steps required for successful implementation of the DNP project. Emphasis is placed on finalizing implementation plans, obtaining necessary approvals or exemptions, and ensuring readiness for project execution. All DNP project courses ( NURS 632 , 633, 731, and 732) must be taken sequentially. If interruption in project course sequence occurs, students must repeat the course(s) or gain approval for progression from the graduate nursing program. Prerequisite: NURS 632 .

title: Clinical: Advanced Family Nursing Practice of Adults II
description: Clinical. Corequisite: NURS 703

title: Advanced Family Nursing Practice of Children and Adolescents
description: This course expands on pediatric assessment concepts, emphasizing the diagnosis and management of acute and chronic conditions commonly encountered in primary healthcare settings. Students will apply advanced pathophysiology, pharmacology, health assessment, diagnostics, and differential diagnosis to guide clinical decision-making based on evidence-based practice guidelines. Using a systems approach, the course highlights chronic care management, health promotion, disease prevention, and restorative/supportive care. This course equips students with competencies in diversity, equity, and inclusion to improve pediatric population health. Emphasizing adaptive communication and managing difficult conversations, students will apply these skills during clinical practicum hours. Prerequisite/Corequisite: NURS 703 . Corequisite NURS 707C . (4.5 credits, 3 credits theory, 1.5 credits clinical = 90 hours).

title: Clinical: Advanced Family Nursing Practice of Adults I
description: Clinical. Corequisite NURS 701 .

title: Advanced Family Nursing Practice of Adults II
description: This course expands on adult assessment concepts, emphasizing the diagnosis and management of acute and chronic conditions commonly encountered in primary healthcare settings. Students will apply advanced pathophysiology, pharmacology, health assessment, diagnostics, and differential diagnosis to guide clinical decision-making based on evidence-based practice guidelines. Using a systems approach, the course highlights chronic care management, health promotion, disease prevention, and restorative/supportive care. During clinical practicum hours students will increase independence in history-taking and physical assessment skills. Additionally, students will develop a participatory approach to care, while exploring concepts of care coordination, professional accountability, conflict resolution, and leadership within interprofessional teams. Prerequisite: NURS 701 . Corequisite: NURS 703C . (5 credits, 3 credits theory, 2 credits clinical = 120 hours)

title: Foundational Concepts of the Family Nurse Practitioner Role I
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the foundational concepts of the family nurse practitioner (FNP) role, focusing on the integration of clinical knowledge, professional standards, and nursing core values. Students will examine the principles of billing, coding, and prescribing within the FNP scope of practice, emphasizing ethical, patient-centered care and regulatory compliance. Students develop advanced skills in physical assessment and history-taking, with an emphasis on accurate SOAP note documentation. Students will participate in an interprofessional simulation to promote a climate of mutual learning, respect, and shared values. Prerequisites: NURS 503 , NURS 504 , and NURS 505 .

title: Clinical: Advanced Family Nursing Practice of Children and Adolescents
description: Clinical. Corequisite: NURS 707

title: Foundational Concepts of the Family Nurse Practitioner Role II
description: This course builds upon the foundational knowledge and skills required for the family nurse practitioner (FNP) role, with an emphasis on clinical safety, accountability, and competence. Students will explore strategies to create a safe and supportive work environment. Students will hone effective documentation, referral, and consultation practices to ensure high-quality, continuous care. Additionally, students will develop the essential skills for performing office procedures, including diagnostic and therapeutic interventions, within the scope of FNP practice. Prerequisite: NURS 721 .

title: Clinical: Advanced Family Nursing Practice for Women's Health
description: Clinical. Corequisite: NURS 709

title: Clinical Residency
description: The Clinical Residency course immerses students in a variety of individualized, student-directed, and scholarly and/or practice-based activities to support professional growth and leadership development. MSN students complete clinical residency hours as needed to fulfill the direct patient care requirement of their degree. Post-masters DNP students complete clinical residency hours as needed to fulfill the requirement of 1000 post-baccalaureate clinical hours. This is a variable credit course (0.5 – 5 credits). One credit equals 60 hours of clinical.

title: Advanced Family Nursing Practice for Women's Health
description: This course expands on women’s health assessment concepts, emphasizing the diagnosis and management of acute and chronic conditions commonly encountered in primary healthcare settings. Students will apply advanced pathophysiology, pharmacology, health assessment, diagnostics, and differential diagnosis to guide clinical decision-making based on evidence-based practice guidelines. Using a systems approach, the course highlights chronic care management, health promotion, disease prevention, and restorative/supportive care. Students will examine the impact of social determinants of health and advocate for policies to reduce healthcare disparities in vulnerable women. Clinical practicum hours focus on assessing and managing women’s health needs across the lifespan. Prerequisite/Corequisite: NURS 703 . Corequisite NURS 709C . (4.5 credits, 3 credits theory, 1.5 credits clinical = 90 hours).

title: Advanced Family Nursing Practice of Older Adults
description: This course expands on older adult assessment concepts, emphasizing the diagnosis and management of acute and chronic conditions commonly encountered in primary healthcare settings. Students will apply advanced pathophysiology, pharmacology, health assessment, diagnostics, and differential diagnosis to guide clinical decision-making based on evidence-based practice guidelines. Using a systems approach, the course highlights chronic care management, health promotion, disease prevention, and restorative/supportive care. Through clinical practicum hours, students will provide compassionate care while promoting self-care and quality of life in older adult populations. Ethical dilemmas encountered by advanced practice nurses when caring for the older adult population are analyzed. Prerequisite: NURS 703 . Corequisite: NURS 725C . (4.5 credits, 3 credits theory, 1.5 credits clinical = 90 hours).

title: Clinical Diagnostics and Differential Diagnosis for Advanced Family Nursing Practice
description: This course focuses on methods of accurate diagnosis of common health conditions in primary healthcare settings. Emphasis is placed on diagnostic frameworks for clinical decision-making, appropriate diagnostic test selection, and timely interpretation of diagnostic testing findings. Through analysis of diagnostic data, students will develop differential diagnoses using a systematic approach that integrates clinical reasoning, knowledge of pathophysiology, and patient-centered assessment. Cost-effectiveness, health equity, and patient outcomes are examined using an ethical framework to ensure safe, quality care. Prerequisite: NURS 601 .

title: Advanced Family Nursing Practice Clinical Immersion
description: This advanced clinical practice course provides Family Nurse Practitioner (FNP) students with comprehensive, hands-on experience with direct patient care. Focusing on all aspects of the FNP role, students engage in immersive clinical practice to hone their skills in assessing, diagnosing, and developing treatment plans for managing diverse patient populations across the lifespan. Through supervised clinical experiences, students will prepare for the transition to practice within the full scope of the FNP role. This course emphasizes evidence-based decision-making, refining differential diagnosis skills, and integrating cultural, ethical, and psychosocial considerations in patient care. Prerequisites NURS 707 , NURS 709 , NURS 721 , and NURS 725 . (4.5 credits – 0.5 theory, 4 credits clinical - 240 hours).

title: DNP Project Analysis and Evaluation
description: This course is a culmination of the DNP project. Students will analyze their data, evaluate project objectives, and discuss challenges and opportunities in evidence translation/quality improvement. Students will develop deliverables for dissemination. Dissemination of clinical scholarship is discussed. DNP project courses ( NURS 632 , 633, 731, and 732) must be taken sequentially. If interruption in project course sequence occurs, students must repeat the course(s) or gain approval for progression from the graduate nursing program. Prerequisite: NURS 731 . 3 credits (1 credit theory, 2 credits clinical – 120 project hours). For Spring 2026, this course will run in it's prior version.

title: Managing Healthcare Quality and Safety
description: This course prepares healthcare leaders to use data to interpret and improve quality and patient safety outcomes. Key areas of focus include quality improvement models, application of theories related to quality and safety, and the use of quality improvement methods and tools.  Students will apply a quality and safety lens to examine and evaluate actual problems in healthcare and consider how data can be used to inform decision-making for system level improvement.

title: Foundations of Supervised Practice in Nutrition and Dietetics
description: This course provides an introduction to supervised practice in medical nutrition therapy, food and nutrition services management and community nutrition.

title: Foundations of Supervised Practice in  Nutrition and Dietetics Lab
description: This course provides 100 supervised practice hours on-site in the field area of medical nutrition therapy, food and nutrition services management or community nutrition.

title: Capstone Project I
description: This course focuses on research methodology including, advanced literature searching, research design, interpretation of research data and ethical issues. Students explore the research needs of organizations providing food and nutrition services, select a project area, and design a simple research project to address the need.

title: Seminar in Medical Nutrition Therapy
description: This course explores advanced topics and issues in Medical Nutrition Therapy.

title: Introduction to Capstone
description: This course focuses on exploring the research needs of an organization providing food and nutrition services, selecting a project area, developing a project proposal and initiating the institutional review board process for their project.

title: Internship Extension
description: This course is designed for students who need to complete internship hours beyond previously registered coursework, due to special circumstances. Instructor permission is required.

title: Clinical: Advanced Family Nursing Practice of Older Adults
description: Clinical. Corequisite: NURS 725

title: Seminar in Community Nutrition
description: This course explores advanced topics and issues in Community Nutrition and Food Insecurity.

title: Supervised Practice in Community Nutrition
description: This course includes preceptor supervised practical experiences in outpatient nutrition education, and community programs for food insecure individuals.  Additional activities and assignments related to supervised practice experiences are assigned.

title: Capstone Project II
description: Students analyze and disseminate the results of the research project conducted in Capstone Project I.

title: Supervised Practice in Medical Nutrition Therapy
description: This course provides preceptor supervised practical experiences primarily in Medical Nutrition Therapy in acute care settings. Students will learn to assess nutritional status, prioritize patient needs, design appropriate nutrition interventions, and monitor and evaluate effectiveness of Nutrition Therapy. Additional activities and assignments related to supervised practice are assigned.

title: Supervised Practice in Food and Nutrition Services Management
description: This course includes preceptor supervised practical experiences in food and nutrition services management.  Additional activities and assignments related to supervised practice experiences are assigned.

title: DNP Project Implementation
description: This course focuses on DNP project implementation and data collection using the Johns Hopkins Evidence-Based Practice Model. All DNP project courses ( NURS 632 , 633, 731, and 732) must be taken sequentially. If interruption in project course sequence occurs, students must repeat the course(s) or gain approval for progression from the graduate nursing program. Prerequisite: NURS 632 . (3 credits clinical - 180 project hours) For Fall 2025, this course will run in it's prior version.

title: Leadership in Food and Nutrition Management
description: This course will cover topics vital to the development of leadership and management skills providing theoretical and practical application in Food and Nutrition Management practice settings.

title: OT Process and Practice
description: This course is designed to address the three-phase occupational therapy process, including evaluation, intervention, and outcomes. The evaluation process (occupational profile & analysis of occupational performance) will include administering standardized and non-standardized assessments. Intervention plans, intervention implementation, and review will be explored. Teaching and learning theories and how they relate to OT practice will be reinforced, practiced and applied.  Finally, students will learn different techniques to assess outcomes. Throughout the course, the students will have opportunities to practice skills, including assessment, intervention, outcomes assessment, and documentation of those processes.

title: Professionalism in OT
description: Professionalism in Occupational Therapy is a foundational course exploring the essential attributes, attitudes, and behaviors required for success in occupational therapy. Students will engage in self- reflection, case analyses, and interactive discussions to develop a deep understanding of professional values, ethics, communication skills, and cultural competence necessary for effective occupational therapy practice. Through practical applications and real-world scenarios, students will cultivate the professional behaviors essential for building therapeutic relationships, collaborating with colleagues, and delivering client-centered care.

title: Evidence-Based Inquiry
description: The purpose of this course is for students to understand the importance of evidence-based practice in occupational therapy.  Students will demonstrate the use of a systematic process to locate, analyze and appraise published literature to become thoughtful consumers of research and scholarship.  Various ways of disseminating evidence to consumers and incorporating evidence into practice will be discussed.

title: Research Methods and Design
description: This course introduces students to the steps involved in the research process. Under the supervision and guidance of research mentor, students will develop a research question, review the literature and design a research proposal relevant to the field of occupational therapy. Students will gain perspectives of the necessary steps involved in the research process and the importance of adhering to ethical practices in research.

title: Lab: OT Process-Psychosocial Practice
description: This lab course provides opportunities for students to integrate and apply what they learned in OT Process: Psychosocial Practice by participating in active, hands on, and experiential learning. Emphasis will be placed on applying the skills of evaluation, treatment planning, intervention, and documentation in preparation for entry level practice with individual with mental illness. Students will apply the concepts of group dynamics and implement group facilitation strategies.

title: Lab: Therapeutic Techniques
description: This lab course offers opportunities for students to integrate and apply what they have learned in Clinical Skills by participating in active, hands on, and experiential learning. Emphasis will be placed on the safe and practical application of the techniques and strategies learned in OCCU 544 .

title: OT Process: Psychosocial Practice
description: This course focuses on the occupational therapy process as it applies to persons of different ages with psychological, social and/or cognitive disabilities. Frames of reference and theories will be reviewed and applied to guide clinical reasoning and evidence-based decision making for assessment and intervention. Students will learn group process and group dynamics as they apply to occupation-based evaluation, intervention, and outcomes assessment. Students will analyze the contextual factors that contribute to psychosocial disorders and consider how issues of disparity, social injustice, and occupational deprivation affect the individual's ability to re-enter into society.

title: Therapeutic Techniques
description: Students will be introduced to mobility skills, splinting, therapeutic exercise, physical agent modalities, critical care issues, vital signs, manual muscle testing, positioning and basic handling techniques. Students will be expected to identify, describe, explain and demonstrate basics skills in the above areas and client, clinician, and caregiver safety will be emphasized.

title: Professional Development in Nutrition and Dietetics
description: This course introduces professional development activities required for the registered dietitian credential. Topics include assessment of professional knowledge and skill, leadership assessment, career development, faith as it relates to vocation and credentialling criteria and maintenance. Professional conference attendance may be required

title: Level I Fieldwork: Pediatric Practice
description: In this course, students will use knowledge and skills learned in OT Process: Pediatric Practice and Common Conditions courses and apply the occupational therapy process with children and youth. Students will practice observation and interpersonal skills and relate didactic coursework to the clinical fieldwork experience while developing an understanding of the client's needs in a pediatric setting. Students will demonstrate emerging clinical reasoning skills and an understanding of the OT process through directed observations, supervision by a fieldwork educator in a practice environment, simulations, and/or debriefing. This course prepares students for Level II Fieldwork.

title: Neurological Conditions in OT Practice
description: This course examines the structure and function of the human nervous system with practical applications to occupational therapy practice. Students examine the effects of neurological dysfunction and conditions associated with the nervous system on occupational performance across the lifespan. Occupation-based models, frames of reference, assessment tools, and an introduction to intervention strategies used when working with persons with neurological and neurodegenerative conditions will be addressed.

title: Lab: Neurological Conditions in OT Practice
description: In this lab students demonstrate competencies and skills related to OT practice with individuals across the lifespan who have neurological dysfunction. Students apply occupation-based models and frames of reference to guide clinical reasoning skills to select appropriate assessments when working with clients with neurological and neurodegenerative conditions.

title: Level I Fieldwork: Community Interventions
description: The purpose of this Level I fieldwork is for students to design and implement occupation-centered programs in community-based settings. Students develop an understanding of how psychological and social factors influence engagement in occupation. Students work with a community partner, assessing the needs of the community and developing and implementing an occupation-centered program under the guidance of a faculty member. The experiential learning activities provide further opportunities for students to practice/refine observation, communication, and interpersonal skills as well as demonstrate professional behaviors.

title: Lab: OT Process-Pediatric Practice
description: This lab course offers opportunities for students to integrate and apply what they have learned in OT Process: Pediatric Practice by participating in active, hands- on, and experiential learning. Emphasis will be placed on applying the skills of evaluation, treatment planning, intervention, and documentation in preparation for entry level pediatric practice.

title: OT Process: Pediatric Practice
description: This course focuses on the occupational therapy process as it applies to pediatric populations. Emphasis will be placed on the use of occupation-based models of practice and frames of reference to guide clinical reasoning for intervention in children whose occupational performance is affected by various developmental, physical, and psychosocial conditions. Screening, assessment, formulation of intervention plans, treatment implementation, discontinuation of services, and documentation of services will be addressed.

title: Level I Fieldwork: Adult Practice
description: The goal of Level I fieldwork is to introduce students to fieldwork, apply knowledge to practice, and develop understanding of the needs of clients" (ACOTE, 2023). In this Level I Fieldwork, students experience the OT process for the adult/older adult population through one or more of the following instructional methods: virtual environments, simulated environments, standardized patients, faculty practice, faculty-led site visits, and supervision by a fieldwork educator in a practice environment that focuses on the adult/older adult population. By applying knowledge learned in didactic coursework, students continue to develop observation skills, therapeutic use of self, professional reasoning, empathy, and a client-centered approach to the OT process. Emphasis will be placed on professionalism and communication skills, with a formal evaluation of student performance provided. Students will have the opportunity to complete this course either locally or internationally, with various clinical experiences available.

title: Lab: OT Process-Adult Practice
description: This lab offers opportunities for students to integrate and apply what they have learned in OT Process Adult Practice by participating in active, hands-on, and experiential learning. Emphasis will be placed on applying the skills of evaluation, treatment planning, intervention, and documentation in preparation for entry level adult practice.

title: OT Process: Adult Practice
description: This course focuses on the occupational therapy process as it applies to adult populations. Emphasis will be placed on occupation-based models of practice and frames of reference to guide clinical reasoning for intervention in adults whose occupational performance is affected by various physical and psychosocial conditions. Screening, assessment, formulation of intervention plans, treatment implementation, and discontinuation of services and documentation of services will be addressed.

title: Administration, Management & Leadership for Ethical OT Practice
description: This course emphasizes the OT's role as a leader and manager who models ethical behaviors, fostering an environment and culture of integrated ethics across a variety of service delivery systems.  An effective leader's qualities are examined, including the essential skills of planning, organizing, leading, and performing outcome evaluations.  Understanding of health care policy and how it affects reimbursement and delivery of services will be addressed. Emphasis will be placed on the essential professional skills required to synthesize legislation, policy, quality improvement, and advocacy as an occupational therapy practitioner and leader.

title: Lab: Adaptations & Assistive Technology
description: In this lab students integrate and apply learning from Adaptations and Assistive Technology by participating in active, hands on, and experiential learning. Students will apply skills in assessment, identification, fabrication, adaptation, and training in the use of assistive technologies for individuals with deficits in function due to disability or impairment.

title: Level II-A Fieldwork
description: This is the first 12-week fieldwork experience. Students deliver OT services under the supervision of a licensed occupational therapist. The goal of Level II Fieldwork is to develop competent, entry-level, generalist occupational therapists. Students apply the OT process, delivering services in evaluation, treatment and documentation. Knowledge of the profession, professional behaviors, communication and management skills are evaluated.

title: OT Process: Older Adult Practice
description: This course focuses on the occupational therapy process as it applies to the older adult population. Emphasis will be placed on occupation-based models of practice and frames of reference to guide clinical reasoning for intervention in the older adult population whose occupational performance is affected by various physical and psychosocial conditions. Screening, assessment, formulation of intervention plans, treatment implementation, and discontinuation of services and documentation of services will be addressed.

title: Global, Cultural, and Spirituality Factors in OT
description: This course is designed to address the role of culture and spirituality in local and global health and well-being.  Students will explore their cultural identity, examine the impact of cultural understanding and learn about other cultural groups through discussion, self-assessment, and reflection.  Students will demonstrate an understanding of culturally responsive practices when relating to individuals of diverse backgrounds.  Students will apply the client factors of values, beliefs, and spirituality within the occupational therapy profession and address them during occupational therapy practice.  This course provides tools and strategies within the occupational therapy profession to provide person-centered intervention to promote health and well-being.  Students also reflect on their own spirituality, values, and beliefs as a foundation to understand the spirituality, values, and beliefs of the client.

title: Health Promotion in OT: Community Interventions
description: This course emphasizes the role of the OT in the promotion of health, wellness, health education and prevention. The course addresses the socio-cultural, socio-economic and socio-political contexts and their ramifications/impact on health and healthcare access. Students examine and discuss issues of racism, discrimination, health disparity, social determinants and social injustice. The needs of underserved populations are explored and strategies to promote health and wellness for populations at risk are developed.

title: Lab: OT Process-Older Adult Practice
description: This lab offers opportunities for students to integrate and apply what they have learned in OT Process: Older Adult Practice by participating in active, hands on, and experiential learning. Emphasis will be placed on applying the skills of evaluation, treatment planning, intervention, and documentation in preparation for entry level adult practice.

title: Research Seminar
description: Under the supervision and guidance of a research mentor, this course will provide opportunities for students to implement a research study, analyze the results, and disseminate the results in a professional poster and oral presentation. Students will also write a final manuscript using publication standards.

title: Fieldwork Extension
description: This course is designed for students who need to complete fieldwork hours beyond previously registered coursework, due to special circumstances. Instructor permission is required.

title: Enrichment Seminar
description: In this course, students will explore the transition from an occupational therapy student to a practicing occupational therapist, including certification and licensure processes.  Students will explore their professional responsibilities, including professional development, involvement in professional organizations, and their future role as a Level II Fieldwork student.  Students will also expand their roles by applying the principles of teaching and learning and practice skills required in academic settings by reviewing critical skills required for Level II Fieldwork with their peers.  Preparation will include caring for mental health during Level II Fieldwork and initial years as a practitioner.

title: Level II-B Fieldwork
description: This is the second 12-week fieldwork experience. Students deliver OT services under the supervision of a licensed occupational therapist. The goal of Level II Fieldwork is to develop competent, entry-level, generalist occupational therapists. Students apply the OT process, delivering services in evaluation, treatment and documentation. Knowledge of the profession, professional behaviors, communication and management skills are evaluated.

title: Clinical Gross Anatomy
description: Clinical Gross Anatomy is designed to provide a comprehensive overview of the structural, functional and developmental anatomy of the human body. Particular consideration will be given to the bony structures, vasculature, innervation, musculature, and relationships of the various structures to one another.  The student should learn the general form, location, and relationships of these features and acquire a vocabulary essential for future PT studies.  This course is also intended to provide a detailed knowledge of the body regions and aims to integrate a basic understanding of embryology and surface anatomy. Case studies of the body are presented in parallel with the designated body regions

title: Lab: Clinical Gross Anatomy
description: The Lab course provides opportunities for human cadaver dissection to reinforce the learning of human anatomy.  Other learning activities will include models, surface anatomy and palpation, and case studies

title: Research I
description: This course introduces evidence-based practice. Students search the literature and perform critical analyses for various levels of evidence available to support physical therapy practice. Students develop research questions using the components of the patient/client management model to direct future research projects.

title: Adaptations and Assistive Technology
description: This course focuses on adaptations and the use of assistive technology to maximize function and occupational performance across the lifespan. Application of clinical reasoning skills as related to the selection, procurement, modification and training in the use of assistive technology will be emphasized. Evaluation and intervention related to assistive technology will be explored. Course topics will include mobility, communication, activities of daily living, low vision, orthotics/prosthetics, environmental modifications, and electronic devices.

title: Applied Physiology
description: This course covers function and dysfunction of the body at various levels (e.g., cellular, tissue, organ and system). Students learn about disorders of selected systems such as cardiovascular, digestive, nervous, pulmonary, immune, renal and musculoskeletal. Application to prevention, health, wellness and fitness are explored.

title: Foundational Skills in Physical Therapy
description: In this course, students use applied anatomy to assess human movement-normal and abnormal. Basic physical therapy functional skills and documentation are covered.

title: Pharmacology and Rehabilitation
description: This course will present the primary drug classes and the physiologic basis of their action. Drugs will be grouped according to their general effects and the type of disorders they are routinely used to treat. Special emphasis will be placed on drugs that are commonly used to treat people receiving physical therapy. This course will likewise address how drug therapy interacts with physical therapy, and how drugs can exert beneficial effects as well as adverse side effects that impact on rehabilitation.

title: Introduction to PT Diagnosis (Dx) and Management
description: This course introduces basic physical therapy skills, the physical therapy patient/client management model and the role of the American Physical Therapy Association and its Guide to Physical Therapist Practice. In addition, the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF) model to physical therapy practice for patient interview and outcomes is addressed.

title: Human Movement
description: This course is focused on how the principles of biomechanics, kinesiology, and movement principles are applied to the practice of physical therapy. Mechanical properties of biological tissue, kinematics, kinetics, muscle actions, movement patterns, and joint structure and function are examined. Foundational biomechanics and motor control principles provide a foundation for overall movement analysis. Students learn about normal posture, gait, and functional activities associated with individuals across the lifespan. Students will compare qualitative aspects of movement behavior and identify potential compensatory movement patterns during tasks of daily living.

title: Lab: Introduction to PT Diagnosis (Dx) & Management
description: Basic physical therapy skills, test and measures, and documentation are presented for students to practice and master. These skills consist of basic range of motion and exercise, manual muscle testing, goniometry, reflex assessment, sensory testing, assessment of soft tissue and barriers to motion of the axial skeleton.

title: Lab: Human Movement
description: Lab-immersive experiences will utilize real-life experiences and common movement tasks of human motion. Functional tasks will be analyzed and deconstructed into fundamental movement patterns and meaningful regional impairments. Practical skills that are critical to physical therapy examination will be applied using both models and human bodies during focused, student-centered learning activities. These skills include, but are not limited to the following: palpation of relevant bony landmarks, observational posture examination, observational gait analysis/deviations, fundamental movement assessment procedures, movement behavior / task analysis applied to common daily activities. Technology will be leveraged to expand applications to movement analysis.

title: Integrated Clinical Experience (ICE)
description: This course is a combination of classroom and clinical site education that reinforces knowledge and translates didactic learning into clinical practice in the areas of ethics, verbal, written and interprofessional communication. Students have opportunities to practice treatment and evaluation techniques learned up to this point in the curriculum. Skills may include but are not limited to tests, measures, mobility training, therapeutic exercise prescription, documentation, pharmacological assessment, modalities, and education.

title: Lab: Clinical Neuroanatomy
description: Students identify neuroanatomical structures, landmarks and nerve innervations using models and virtual resources. Basic neurological screening and assessment techniques are explored in the lab. Case studies based on the client/patient management model are incorporated for students to expand their skill sets.

title: Foundations in Professionalism
description: This course introduces the role of self as a physical therapist working with clients with disease and disability. A faith-based Christian perspective is presented as an initial step of spiritual formation as a healthcare professional. Emphasis is on the healthcare team and interprofessional education collaboration. The scope and history of physical therapy, professional behaviors, and the APTA Vision Statement and Core Values are covered.

title: Musculoskeletal Physical Therapy I
description: This course covers the physical therapy patient/client management model applied to the musculoskeletal practice patterns commonly seen in physical therapy. Special attention is given to the upper extremity, head and neck. Case studies integrate important client-centered variables, such as psychosocial issues, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology and nutrition.

title: Clinical Neuroanatomy
description: This course examines the structure and function of the human nervous system. The clinical relevance of the autonomic, peripheral and central nervous systems are covered and applied to a neurological evaluation for clients commonly seen in physical therapy. Students complete instructional modules on selected neurological diseases/disorders using components of the patient/client management model.

title: Lab: Therapeutic Interventions
description: This course introduces students to the foundational concepts and principles necessary to apply therapeutic interventions used in physical therapy practice. Content emphasizes the physiological principles, clinical applications, indications, contraindications, and safety considerations associated with each intervention. Focus is placed on the safe and effective use of intervention management strategies to promote tissue healing, modulate pain, restore function, and optimize movement. Through lecture, lab, and case-based learning, students will develop the knowledge and hands-on skills necessary to select, apply, and evaluate interventions based on patient presentation, clinical goals, and evidence-based practice.

title: Clinical Medicine: Musculoskeletal
description: In this course, students examine the clinical relevance of musculoskeletal diseases and disorders commonly seen in physical therapy in patients/clients across the life span. Students receive an introduction to related musculoskeletal topics, such as diversity, psychosocial implications, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology, and nutrition. The multi-faceted variables that influence growth, development and recovery associated with the patient/client with musculoskeletal dysfunction are presented.

title: Therapeutic Interventions
description: This course introduces students to the foundational concepts and principles necessary to apply therapeutic interventions used in physical therapy practice. Content emphasizes the physiological principles, clinical applications, indications, contraindications, and safety considerations associated with each intervention. Focus is placed on the safe and effective use of intervention management strategies to promote tissue healing, modulate pain, restore function, and optimize movement. Through lecture, lab, and case-based learning, students will develop the knowledge and hands-on skills necessary to select, apply, and evaluate interventions based on patient presentation, clinical goals, and evidence-based practice.

title: Lab: Musculoskeletal Physical Therapy I
description: Students practice and demonstrate the selected components of the client/patient management model relevant to identified musculoskeletal practice patterns of the upper extremity, head and neck through the use of case studies. Students are evaluated in assessing and managing the client/patient with selected musculoskeletal practice patterns.

title: Lab: Foundational Skills in Physical Therapy
description: Basic hands-on PT skills are presented for students to practice and master. This includes skills such as body mechanics, positioning and draping, infection control, vital signs, clinical emergencies, basic wheelchair prescription and mobility, transfers, bed mobility, basic gait training, and the use of assistive devices.

title: Musculoskeletal Physical Therapy II
description: This course covers the patient/client management model applied to musculoskeletal practice patterns commonly seen in physical therapy, with emphasis on the lower extremity, thoracolumbar spine and sacroiliac joint. Case studies integrate essential considerations such as psychosocial issues, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology and nutrition.

title: Lab: Musculoskeletal Physical Therapy II
description: Students practice and demonstrate the selected components of the client/patient management model relevant to identified musculoskeletal practice patterns of the lower extremities, spine and sacroiliac joint through the use of case studies. Students are evaluated in assessing and managing the client/patient with selected musculoskeletal practice patterns.

title: Neuromuscular Physical Therapy II
description: This course covers the physical therapy patient/client management model applied to neurological practice patterns associated to the client/patient with specific diagnoses, such as spinal cord injury, brain injury, concussion, multiple sclerosis, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, cerebellar disorders, Parkinson's disease and other neurological disorders. Case studies integrate important client-centered variables, such as psychosocial issues, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology and nutrition.

title: Lab: Neuromuscular Physical Therapy I
description: Students practice and demonstrate the selected components of the client/patient management model relevant to identified neurological practice patterns relevant to the client diagnosed with stroke and vestibular dysfunction when presented with case studies. Motor learning and control principles are emphasized in conjunction with other neurophysiological approaches. Students are evaluated in assessing and managing the client/patient with selected neurological practice patterns.

title: Clinical Medicine: Cardiopulmonary
description: In this course, students examine the clinical relevance of cardiopulmonary diseases and disorders commonly seen in physical therapy in patients/clients across the life span. Students receive an introduction to related cardiopulmonary topics, such as diversity, psychosocial implications, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology, and nutrition. The multi-faceted variables that influence growth, development and recovery associated with the patient/client with cardiopulmonary dysfunction are presented.

title: Neuromuscular Physical Therapy I
description: This course covers the physical therapy patient/client management model applied to the neurological practice patterns commonly seen in physical therapy, with special attention given to the client/patient with a stroke and vestibular dysfunction. Motor learning and control, proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) and other neurophysiological techniques are introduced. Case studies integrate important client-centered variables, such as psychosocial issues, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology and nutrition.

title: PT Continuum of Care I: Acute and Subacute
description: This is the first of three continuum of care courses. This course focuses on the acute and subacute physical therapy settings, which are typically housed in a healthcare facility where the patient's medical condition require 24-hour medical/nursing care. This course incorporates 4 planned case studies in the areas of stroke, below knee amputation, spina bifida, and total knee replacement. Students have opportunities to experience setting-specific assessment and treatment techniques, interprofessional collaboration, discharge strategies and setting-specific documentation. Students receive training in billing, managerial skills, and public relations.

title: Clinical Medicine: Integumentary and Other Systems
description: Students examine the clinical relevance of integumentary and other systems diseases and disorders commonly seen in physical therapy in patients/clients across the life span. Students receive an introduction to related integumentary and other systems topics, such as diversity, psychosocial implications, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology, and nutrition. The multi-faceted variables that influence growth, development and recovery associated with the patient/client with integumentary and other systems dysfunctions are presented.

title: Lab: PT Continuum of Care:Acute and Subacute
description: Students practice specific skills related to the acute and subacute setting within nursing simulation labs to promote execution of selected components of the patient/client management models. This course incorporates 4 planned case studies in the areas of stroke, below knee amputation, spina bifida, and total knee replacement. Competency is assessed on setting-specific skills.

title: Integumentary and Other Systems Physical Therapy
description: This course covers the physical therapy patient/client management model as it is affected by dysfunction with the integumentary and other systems and any associated practice patterns. Students are introduced to management of wounds, ulcers, and burns. Dysfunction related to the lymphatic, endocrine and metabolic, genital and reproductive, gastrointestinal, renal and urologic, hematologic, hepatic and biliary and immune systems. Case studies integrate psychosocial diagnostic imaging, pharmacology and nutrition.

title: Research II
description: Students have the opportunity to review and critically analyze appropriate research designs through an in-depth literature review. Student research groups initiate their projects by developing a research question and following up with the scientific process. Students submit a research proposal to the College's Institutional Review Board (IRB) for approval.

title: Lab: Cardiopulmonary Physical Therapy
description: This course covers practical application of appropriate interventions that consist of functional skills applied to the cardiopulmonary system practice patterns. Use of case studies focus on student critical thinking in assessment, diagnosis and management of patients/clients across the lifespan.

title: Clinical Medicine: Neuromuscular
description: In this course, students examine the clinical relevance of neurological diseases and disorders commonly seen in physical therapy in patients/clients across the life span. Students receive an introduction to related neurological topics, such as diversity, psychosocial implications, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology, and nutrition. The multi-faceted variables that influence growth, development and recovery associated with the patient/client with neurological dysfunction are presented.

title: Lab: Integumentary and Other Systems Physical Therapy
description: This course covers practical application of appropriate interventions consisting of functional skills applied to the integumentary and other systems' practice patterns. Use of case studies focus on student critical thinking in assessment, diagnosis and management of patients/clients across the lifespan.

title: Cardiopulmonary Physical Therapy
description: This course covers the physical therapy patient/client management model applied to the cardiac and pulmonary practice patterns. Emphasis is on coronary artery disease (CAD), congestive heart failure (CHF), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and their effects on patient outcomes. Case studies integrate psychosocial issues, pathophysiology, diagnostic imaging, pharmacology and nutrition.

title: Geriatric Physical Therapy
description: The characteristics of the geriatric population and associated concerns that warrant physical therapy are addressed in this course. In addition, health promotion, wellness and fitness are also covered. Specific assessments, intervention strategies, management techniques and discharge planning of the client/patient are considered. Population-specific documentation, legislation, community resources, adaptive equipment and supplies are addressed.

title: Lab: Geriatric Physical Therapy
description: Students integrate the content of the course Geriatric Dx and Management. Techniques and approaches for the assessment and treatment of the geriatric population are practiced through hands-on, experiential learning. Through the use of case studies, student competence is assessed.

title: Lab: Neuromuscular Physical Therapy II
description: Students practice and demonstrate the selected components of the client/patient management model relevant to identified neurological practice patterns relevant to the client diagnosed with spinal cord injury, brain injury, concussion, multiple sclerosis, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, cerebellar disorders, Parkinson's disease and other neurological disorders. Motor learning and control principles are emphasized in conjunction with other neurophysiological approaches. Students are evaluated in assessing and managing the client/patient with selected neurological practice patterns.

title: Equity and Belonging in Healthcare
description: This course provides the student with opportunities to examine the role of culture in health and wellness as it relates to self, clients/patients, physical therapy, and local and global healthcare arenas. Students use technology to interact with diverse individuals and to practice culturally sensitive practices and competencies. Cultural competency assessments and research expand student learning and abilities. Students discuss a faith-based perspective and relate it to other religions.

title: Lab: PT Continuum of Care II:Rehab and Home Health
description: Students practice specific skills related to rehab and home health settings within on-site exercise labs and the Universal Apartment to promote execution of selected components of the patient/client management models. This course incorporates 4 planned case studies in the areas of stroke, below knee amputation, spina bifida, and total knee replacement. Competency is assessed in setting-specific skills.

title: Comprehensive Assessments
description: This course provides preparation for the comprehensive written and lab practical assessments. Students demonstrate competency of the content learned and practiced in Year 1 and part of Year 2. (4 semesters, 1 Winter Intensive). The culminating experience provides a profile of student competence in cognitive, affective and psychomotor domain as it relates to the Program's curriculum and to the physical therapy profession.

title: Service Learning in Action
description: This course provides students with opportunities to provide service and faith in action through immersion in diverse and underrepresented communities. The physical therapist’s role in advocacy, social responsibility and citizenship will be explored. Christian service learning in action is the foundation for this experience.

title: Pediatric Physical Therapy
description: The characteristics of the pediatric population and associated concerns that warrant physical therapy are addressed. Diverse pediatric settings are explored. In addition, health promotion, wellness and fitness are also covered. Specific assessments, intervention strategies, management techniques and discharge planning of the client/patient are considered. Population-specific documentation, legislation, community resources, adaptive equipment and supplies are addressed.

title: Lab: Pediatric Physical Therapy
description: Students apply the content of the course Pediatric Diagnosis and Management. Techniques and approaches for the assessment and treatment of the pediatric population of various settings are practiced through hands-on, experiential learning. Through the use of case studies, students competence is assessed.

title: Research III
description: This course promotes the progression of the Thesis Research Project as students refine and implement the methodology by collecting and organizing the data for analysis. Ongoing faculty advisor mentoring occurs throughout the semester to provide enriching discussion, feedback and direction.

title: Lab: PT Continuum of Care III:Outpatient
description: Students practice specific skills related to outpatient settings within on-site exercise labs to promote execution of selected components of the patient/client management models. This course incorporates 4 planned case studies in the areas of stroke, below knee amputation, spina bifida, and total knee replacement. Competency is assessed in setting-specific skills.

title: Healthcare Issues and Trends
description: This course explores the external forces that drive social, legislative, and economic influences in the delivery of physical therapy services and healthcare in the US. The course addresses topics such as advocacy, legal practice standards, professionalism, global healthcare, and expanding healthcare specialization and services.

title: Clinical Experience I
description: This 8-week full time clinical experience reinforces knowledge and translates didactic learning into clinical practice. Emphasis is on professional practice and patient management using the performance dimensions of supervision/guidance, quality, complexity, consistency and efficiency. By the end of this clinical experience, the student will be at Advanced Beginner Performance Level or higher as defined by the Clinical Performance Instrument (CPI).

title: Advanced Therapeutic Techniques
description: This course provides an evidence based manual therapy approach toward evaluation and management of musculoskeletal conditions. Emphasis will be on enhancing the student's clinical reasoning and manual therapy skills. Manual therapy techniques include: soft tissue (myofascial release, active release, instrument assisted soft tissue mobilization), muscle energy, strain-counter strain, cupping, mobilization with movement, nerve gliding, and HVLA thrust manipulation.

title: Lab: Advanced Therapeutic Techniques
description: In lab, manual therapy techniques will include: soft tissue (myofascial release, active release, instrument assisted soft tissue mobilization), muscle energy, strain-counter strain, mobilization with movement, nerve gliding, and thrust mobilization.

title: Health Promotion, Wellness, and Fitness
description: In this course, students learn about the eclectic role of the physical therapist in the areas of health promotion, wellness and fitness. Students develop educational materials for the community and perform a professional educational presentation.

title: Selected Topics in PT
description: This course addresses physical therapy in various specialty areas. Students explore physical therapy practice in pain science, sports, women's health, lymphedema, oncology, hand therapy, obesity management, complimentary therapies, and aquatics. As part of the learning process, students interview select specialty therapists to gain more in-depth knowledge.

title: Physical Therapy Administration and Management
description: This course covers the managerial and supervisory aspects of and the business practices in physical therapy. This consists of the following related topics: provision of health services, values and ethical principles, external oversights, and business acumen. Students apply the business principles and practices to develop a business plan to start a niche physical therapy practice.

title: PT Continuum of Care II: Rehab and Home Health
description: This is the second of three continuum of care courses. This course focuses on rehab and home health physical therapy settings. This course continues to use the 4 planned case studies in the areas of stroke, below knee amputation, spina bifida and total knee replacement to compare and contrast possible client/patient progression and recovery. Students learn about the transition between settings, associated client/patient changes, setting-specific assessment and treatment techniques, interprofessional collaboration, discharge strategies and related documentation. Students receive training in billing, managerial skills, and public relations.

title: Research IV
description: In this course, students continue their research project, which can be one of the following: Systematic Review, Critically Appraised Topic (CAT), Clinical Trials, Diagnostic studies, Qualitative Studies, or other types of qualitative or quantitative research. Students are expected to complete their research paper and design the research poster. Students are also expected to submit their projects to relevant conferences as part of the research dissemination process. Research findings will be disseminated via a poster presentation at an appropriate venue chosen by the Messiah University DPT Program. Examples of appropriate research dissemination venues include physical therapy national and international conferences, Messiah University DPT Program Research Symposium as well as Messiah University interprofessional research related events.

title: Clinical Experience II
description: This 10-week full time clinical experience reinforces knowledge and translates didactic learning into clinical practice. Emphasis is on professional practice and patient management using the performance dimensions of supervision/guidance, quality, complexity, consistency and efficiency. By the end of this clinical experience, the student will be at Intermediate Performance Level or higher as defined by the Clinical Performance Instrument (CPI).

title: PT Continuum of Care III: Outpatient
description: This is the third of three continuum of care courses. This course focuses on the outpatient physical therapy setting. This course continues to use the 4 planned case studies in the areas of stroke, below knee amputation, spina bifida and total knee replacement to compare and contrast possible client/patient progression and recovery. Students learn about setting-specific assessment and treatment, including return to work models of care. Students work to become proficient in the fast-paced environment that requires treatment of multiple clients simultaneously in a timely manner throughout the workday. Students receive training in billing, managerial skills, and public relations.

title: Professional Leadership and Advocacy
description: In this course, students will discuss professional issues facing the modern physical therapy practitioner, including ethical decision making, state and national current physical therapy issues, and legislative efforts.  Students will examine evolving roles of professional leadership affected by such factors as changing societal demands, trends in health care, government regulations, and the expanding body of knowledge. Professional, ethical and social advocacy issues will be addressed. These experiences will prepare students for leadership roles as change agents in the profession.

title: Clinical Experience III
description: This 12-week full time clinical experience reinforces knowledge and translates didactic learning into clinical practice. Emphasis is on professional practice and patient management using the performance dimensions of supervision/guidance, quality, complexity, consistency, and efficiency.

title: Social Business Modeling
description: This course focuses on developing social business models that satisfy an unworkable, unavoidable, urgent, or underserved customer need. Students will examine various social business models that are self-supporting, generate economic growth, and enable communities to flourish.

title: Professional Seminar I
description: This course provides foundational information for being a professional, ethical, and competent social worker including understanding professional behavior, faith and practice issues, social work competencies, interprofessional collaboration, and the purpose of the Code of Ethics. Additionally, the course will focus on discipline specific writing expectations including use of the APA (American Psychological Association) style guide. Students review the program handbook and other requirements for the Master of Social Work program.

title: Professional Growth Beyond Graduation
description: This course provides strategies for professional development beyond graduation for the entry-level physical therapist. Students will receive training to prepare for the successful completion of the National Physical Therapy Examination. Students develop a resume and learn to use social media platforms for their job search. Students are educated in the importance of graduate outcomes and the need for their participation in the process. Students discuss their impact in teaching, service, and the global society.

title: Social Entrepreneurship: Theory and Practice
description: This course explores the theory and practice of social entrepreneurship. Students will examine how entrepreneurs can solve global, social problems and enable communities to flourish.

title: Diversity and Social Justice
description: This course emphasizes an intersectional understanding of the impact of diverse identities on the human experience of oppression and discrimination, the systemic nature of oppression, power, and privilege, and the responsibility of the social work profession to engage in the promotion of social, racial, economic, and environmental justice and human rights. The concepts of cultural humility and ADEI (Anti-Racism, Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion) are considered and applied to all levels of social work practice.

title: Generalist Social Work Practice with Individuals
description: This course in the generalist practice series is designed to introduce students to the philosophy and characteristics of helping relationships within the generalist framework (engagement, assessment, intervention, termination, and evaluation) in social work. It introduces basic relationship building, communication, social work principles, values, and ethics, critical thinking, and problem-solving skills for use with any client system (individuals, families, groups, organizations, and communities), but has a primary focus on working with diverse individuals within a social context and in interprofessional collaboration.

title: Generalist Social Work Practice with Families
description: This course in the generalist practice series begins to develop specific helping skills for generalist social work practice. Topics include the problem-solving model, interprofessional collaboration, and interviewing, assessment, treatment planning and evaluation skills. Family developmental issues will be addressed. The primary focus of this course will be the development of a knowledge base and the skills necessary to provide social work intervention at the mezzo level, especially with diverse families-at-risk.

title: Social Welfare Policy and Change
description: This course will introduce students to the arena of social policy and to the sociopolitical environment in which policy takes place. Students will develop a capacity to examine and critically analyze social welfare policy within historical and societal contexts. Selected areas of policy will be examined using theoretical and analytical principles. The course will focus on the implementation of advocacy strategies to combat racism and discrimination, and to promote social, economic, and environmental justice, and will empower students to be agents of social change in the United States and abroad.

title: Field Instruction and Seminar II
description: This course provides the second 200 hours of generalist field practicum in the same agency under the supervision of an MSW-level social worker. Opportunities will be provided to connect social work theory with practice and to develop generalist social work skills with various client systems within an agency context utilizing theory-based practice knowledge. Students meet regularly with a Field Liaison for field seminars.

title: Professional Seminar II
description: This course provides information for being a professional, ethical, and competent advanced generalist social worker including an enhanced understanding of professional behavior, faith and practice issues, advanced social work competencies, interprofessional collaboration, and the NASW Code of Ethics. Students review the program handbook and other requirements for the Master of Social Work program as those requirements apply to the advanced generalist level. Students begin planning for the capstone project.

title: Generalist Social Work Practice with Groups
description: This course in the generalist practice series examines the dynamics of the small group. Different theoretical conceptualizations of the role of the social worker in the group are discussed. Concepts such as diversity, group dynamics, norms, and roles are examined. A model for group leadership is introduced and skills for planning, facilitating, and evaluating groups will be developed and practiced. Content will include identifying and implementing group related empirically based interventions and services designed to achieve client and community goals. There is also a focus on the interplay between social work and community groups and interprofessional collaboration.

title: Generalist Social Work Practice with Organizations and Communities
description: This course in the generalist practice series is an integral part of generalist social work practice. It is designed to develop the skills and abilities of students to practice macro social work. The primary focus will be on the inter-relationships between the environment, agency structures and general service delivery systems. Students will develop specific community organizing, networking, and interprofessional collaboration skills. A strong component of this course will focus on program evaluation and community engagement, assessment, and intervention.

title: Field Instruction and Seminar I
description: This course provides specific social work service delivery in selected social welfare and related agencies. Students will participate in a minimum of 200 hours of generalist practice experience under the supervision of an MSW-level social worker. Opportunities will be provided to connect social work theory with practice and to develop generalist social work skills with various client systems within an agency context utilizing theory-based practice knowledge. Students meet regularly with a Field Liaison for field seminars.

title: Mental Health and Social Policy
description: This course provides an understanding of mental health policy and service delivery in the United States and the impact of mental health policies on social work practice.
