It reviews multiple perspectives on mental health and mental illness and the history of social policies influencing mental health care. The course examines current trends in service delivery and financing (such as managed care and health insurance reform) and explores legal and ethical issues in the provision of mental health care. Special consideration will be given to how the contemporary mental health system is experienced by diverse populations.

title: Advanced Social Work Practice with Individuals and Families
description: This course builds upon the theory and skills learned in human behavior, the generalist practice courses, and is applied in the field practicum. This course addresses the theoretical perspectives used in advanced social work practice with individuals and families across the lifespan as well as builds competency in the use of specific evidence-based practice models and interprofessional collaboration. The focus is on providing a solid grounding in engagement, assessment, treatment planning, and intervention skills and strategies that are culturally responsive and relevant to current social work practice with diverse populations in varied contexts.

title: Clinical Experience Extension
description: This course is designed for students who need to complete clinical experience hours beyond previously registered coursework, due to special circumstances. Instructor permission is required.

title: The Social Work Leader and Manager
description: This course prepares social work practitioners to understand their role as leaders in managing and working in complex human service organizations. Evidence-based management practices to effectively respond to organizations’ current challenges and opportunities are explored, with a focus on anti-racism, diversity, equity, and inclusion in the workplace.

title: Advanced Social Work Practice with Groups
description: This course builds upon the theory and skills learned in human behavior, the generalist practice courses, and is applied in the field practicum. This course addresses the theoretical perspectives used in advanced social work practice with groups (both ‘treatment’ groups and ‘task’ groups) as well as builds competency in the use of specific evidence-based models of advanced group work practice and interprofessional collaboration. The focus is on providing a solid grounding as a leader/facilitator in the group planning process, engaging, and assessing groups, and identifying, building, and utilizing intervention and evaluation skills in the group process that are culturally responsive and relevant to current social work practice with diverse populations in varied contexts.

title: Advanced Social Work Practice with Organizations and Communities
description: This course builds upon the theory and skills learned in human behavior, the generalist practice courses, and is applied in the field practicum. The course addresses the theoretical perspectives used in advanced social work practice in organizations and communities as well as builds competency in leadership and the use of specific evidence-based models of organizational and community change and interprofessional collaboration. The focus is on providing a solid grounding in culturally responsive engagement, assessment, intervention, and evaluation skills and strategies aimed at macro-level systems.

title: Human Behavior in the Social Environment
description: This course examines the person as a bio-psycho-social-spiritual-cultural being and the processes by which development occurs throughout the life cycle. There will be an exploration of the dynamic interrelationships between biological, psychological, spiritual, and environmental factors that create human diversity and likeness. Content will utilize current research and empirically based theories to focus on the interactions of individuals, groups, society, and economic systems.

title: Field Instruction and Seminar IV
description: This course provides the second 250 hours of advanced field practicum in the same agency under the supervision of an MSW-level social worker. Opportunities will be provided to connect advanced social work theory with practice and to develop advanced social work skills with various client systems within an agency context utilizing theory-based practice knowledge. Students meet regularly with a Field Liaison for field seminars. The advanced field practicum should reflect the student’s chosen track as much as possible.

title: Advanced Social Work Research Methods
description: This course builds upon the basic understanding of quantitative and qualitative research methods in social work and emphasizes the integration of research concepts and skills in evaluating social work programs and practice (at all levels) in the field practicum. This course is designed to assist students in applying research methods to practice settings and developing an empirical approach to practice to contribute to the knowledge base of the profession while enhancing practice, policy, and/or service delivery.

title: Field Instruction and Seminar III
description: This course provides specific social work service delivery in selected social welfare and related agencies. Students will participate in a minimum of 250 hours of advanced practice experience under the supervision of an MSW-level social worker. Opportunities will be provided to connect advanced social work theory with practice and to develop advanced social work skills with various client systems within an agency context utilizing theory-based practice knowledge. Students meet regularly with a Field Liaison for field seminars. The advanced field practicum should reflect the student’s chosen track as much as possible.

title: Professional Seminar III
description: This course serves as the capstone course where students complete and present an individualized field practicum-based project that demonstrates professional, competent, and ethical advanced social work practice. In addition, students learn about the professional licensing process in their state and work on preparation for the licensing exam.
